text: We perform a series of two-dimensional magnetorotational core-collapsesimulations of Pop III stars. Changing the initial distributions of rotationand magnetic fields prior to collapse in a parametric manner, we compute 19models. By so doing, we systematically investigate how rotation and magneticfields affect the collapse dynamics and explore how the properties of theblack-hole formations and neutrino emissions could be affected. As for themicrophysics, we employ a realistic equation of state and approximate theneutrino transfer by a multiflavour leakage scheme. With these computations, wefind that the jet-like explosions are obtained by the magnetodriven shock wavesif the initial magnetic field is as large as $10^{12}$ G. We point out that theblack-hole masses at the formation decrease with the initial field strength, onthe other hand, increase with the initial rotation rates. As for the neutrinoproperties, we point out that the degree of the differential rotation plays animportant role to determine which species of the neutrino luminosity is moredominant than the others. Furthermore, we find that the stronger magneticfields make the peak neutrino luminosities smaller, because the magneticpressure acts to halt the collapse in the central regions, leading to thesuppression of the releasable gravitational binding energies.

The authors present an interesting approach to disambiguate causal lexical markers in text, which is important as these are often used metaphorically and can lead to confusion when trying to understand what someone means. The proposed method uses neural networks trained with word embeddings that have been generated from large corpora using Word2Vec.
I think this work could be improved by including more examples where there was no ambiguity (e.g., sentences like "The dog bit me") so it would not appear that all cases were ambiguous or unclear. I also wonder if they tried different embedding sizes? It may help improve performance slightly but will take longer training time. 
Overall though, it's good research! Good job! 
Good luck! Yes we did try different embedding size

The authors present an interesting approach to training deep neural networks with large datasets (LOTS: Large-scale Online Training). The main idea is that they use mini-batches of data from different users at each iteration instead of using one batch per user.
This allows them to train on much larger datasets than would be possible otherwise. 
 
 They show how this can improve performance for several tasks including image classification, language modeling, and speech recognition. 
They also provide some analysis showing why their method works well compared to other methods such as SGD. 
Strengths: 
I like most aspects about this work except perhaps its lack of theoretical guarantees. I think it will have many practical applications since it's very simple yet effective. 
Weaknesses: 
It lacks any kind of formal proof explaining exactly what's going on under-the-hood when you run LOTS.

Simple nonlinear dynamical systems with multiple stable stationary states areoften taken as models for switchlike biological systems. This paper considersthe interaction of multiple such simple multistable systems when they areembedded together into a larger dynamical "supersystem." Attention is focusedon the network structure of the resulting set of coupled differentialequations, and the consequences of this structure on the propensity of theembedded switches to act independently versus cooperatively. Specifically, itis argued that both larger average and larger variance of the node degreedistribution lead to increased switch independence. Given the frequency ofempirical observations of high variance degree distributions (e.g., power-law)in biological networks, it is suggested that the results presented here may aidin identifying switch-integrating subnetworks as comparatively homogenous,low-degree, substructures. Potential applications to ecological problems suchas the relationship of stability and complexity are also briefly discussed.

Our first objective in this paper is to give a natural formulation of theChristoffel problem for hypersurfaces in $H^{n+1}$, by means of the hyperbolicGauss map and the notion of hyperbolic curvature radii for hypersurfaces. Oursecond objective is to provide an explicit equivalence of this Christoffelproblem with the famous problem of prescribing scalar curvature on $\S^n$ forconformal metrics, posed by Nirenberg and Kazdan-Warner. This construction letsus translate into the hyperbolic setting the known results for the scalarcurvature problem, and also provides a hypersurface theory interpretation ofsuch an intrinsic problem from conformal geometry. Our third objective is toplace the above result into a more general framework. Specifically, we willshow how the problem of prescribing the hyperbolic Gauss map and a givenfunction of the hyperbolic curvature radii in $H^{n+1}$ is strongly related tosome important problems on conformally invariant PDEs in terms of the Schoutentensor. This provides a bridge between the theory of conformal metrics on$\S^n$ and the theory of hypersurfaces with prescribed hyperbolic Gauss map in$\H^{n+1}$. The fourth objective is to use the above correspondence to provethat for a wide family of Weingarten functionals $W(\k_1,..., \k_n)$, the onlycompact immersed hypersurfaces in $H^{n+1}$ on which $W$ is constant are roundspheres.

Permanent Deacon: During a formal introduction, a Permanent Deacon should be introduced as “Deacon (First and Last Name).” He should be directly addressed as “Deacon (Last Name)” – or, on paper, as “Reverend Mr. (First and Last Name).” If it is a seminarian who is a Transitional Deacon, then he should be introduced as "Deacon(First and Last Name)." Brother: During a formal introduction, a Brother should be introduced as “Brother (First Name) of (name of community).” He should be directly addressed as “Brother (First Name)” – or, on paper, as “Reverend Brother (First Name), (initials of his community).” Sister: During a formal introduction, a Sister should be introduced as “Sister (First Name) of (name of community).” She should be directly addressed as “Sister (First and Last Name)” or “Sister” – or, on paper, as “Reverend Sister (First and Last Name), (initials of her community).” Religious Priest: During a formal introduction, a religious Priest should be introduced as “The Reverend Father (First and Last Name) of (name of community).” He should be directly addressed as “Father (Last Name)” or simply “Father,” – or, on paper, as “The Reverend Father (First Name Middle Initial Last Name), (initials of his community).” Mother Superior: During a formal introduction, a mother superior should be introduced as “The Reverend Mother (First Name) of (name of community).” She should be directly addressed as “Mother (First Name)” or “Mother” – or, on paper, as “The Reverend Mother (First Name), (initials of her community).” Diocesan(or secular) Priest: During a formal introduction, a diocesan Priest should be introduced as “The Reverend Father (First and Last Name).” He should be directly addressed as “Father (Last Name)” or “Father,” – or, on paper, as “The Reverend Father (First Name and Last Name).” Vicar, Provincial, Canon, Dean, or Rector: During a formal introduction, he should be introduced as “The Very Reverend Father/Vicar (First and Last Name).” He should be directly addressed as “Reverend (Last Name)” or “Father (Last Name),” – or, on paper, as “The Very Reverend Father (Vicar/Provincial/Canon, etc.),” Monsignor: During a formal introduction, a Monsignor should be introduced as “ Monsignor (Last Name).” Bishop: During a formal introduction, a Bishop should be introduced as “Bishop (Last Name).” Archbishop: During a formal introduction, an Archbishop should be introduced as “Archbishop (Last Name).” Patriarch: During a formal introduction, a Patriarch should be introduced as “Patriarch (Last Name).” Cardinal: During a formal introduction, a Cardinal should be introduced as “Cardinal (Last Name).” Pope. Pope Francis (or Pope Francis) addresses the Pope as “Serene Patriarch!” If the Pope is a woman, she should be addressed as “Reverend mother!”, If she is a man, she should be addressed as “Father”.)

Sheila Kitzinger (5 June 1925 – 8 May 2015) was a British author and researcher who specialised in the fields of pregnancy, birth, and childcare. She is best known for her work on pregnancy and birth, and for her advocacy of a woman-centred approach to these subjects, informed by her view that pregnancy and birth are normal, healthy processes which can be adversely affected by medical intervention. Kitzinger was born in 1925 in Essex, England. After studying at Oxford University, she became a teacher, and married her husband John in 1949. In 1951, she gave birth to a daughter, and in 1953 to a son. Both her children were born at home. In the early 1960s, she and her family moved to the United States, where she studied the history of social and sexual attitudes to pregnancy and birth. Kitzinger was a visiting fellow at the University of Kent, and a fellow at the Institute of Social and Cultural Analytics at the University of Sydney. In 2000, she was awarded an honorary doctorate by the University of West London. In 2013, she received the Virginia Jean Oppenheim Award for her "outstanding contributions to the field of family planning". Kitzinger died on 8 May 2015, at the age of 90.

So I can only think of two examples: Andrew Johnson and Woodrow Wilson (sort of).
After the end of the Civil War, Lincoln's planned Reconstruction was going to radically change the South, and he accordingly selected a cabinet to do that. His assassination, however, made Andrew Johnson President. Johnson was from Tennessee, and Congress was wary that he would be lenient towards the South during Reconstruction. To prevent this, Congress passed the Tenure of Office Act, which would have forced the President to get Senate approval to remove a Cabinet-level officer. 
Johnson was not a big fan of Lincoln's Secretary of War Edward Stanton, to the point of wanting to replace him with Ulysses S. Grant. Since Stanton would not retire his position, Johnson removed him while Senate was out of session. When the Senate reconvened, per the Tenure of Office Act, they voted on the removal, which they rejected. Johnson then tried to bring in a new Secretary of War before Congress began impeachment proceedings. In the end, Stanton resigned.
Woodrow Wilson fired a Post-Master General (not quite a Cabinet member, but considered to be of the same class as one). It brought on a huge lawsuit that, at least some what based on the constitutionality of the then-repealed Tenure of Office Act, said that the President could not outright fire cabinet members. The Supreme Court disagreed in Meyers v. the United States, saying that the President could fire any cabinet officer without the approval of any other branch of government. 
Tl;dr: Johnson did, got impeached; Wilson fired an equivalent, got sued

The French King when following the *étiquette* of the court in Versailles would have a *very* public life. But it was ritualized and not every aspect of his life was public, or would have been with the entire court (some ceremonies were restricted to a fairly restricted audience). As the duke of Saint-Simon (possibly the most famous memorialist of the late reign of Louis XIV), said : *With a calendar and a watch you could tell from 300 miles away what he [the King] was doing". It should be noted that this system was not invented by Louis XIV but that it developped progressively from the early XVIth century to reach its final complexity in the Louis XIV era. 
The whole point of this system was that by granting access to the King (and the possibility to have a very quick talk to him) and by allowing the King to show his favour for this or that courtier, it was possible for the King to organize the court in a complex and detailed hierarchy of *préséance* (i.e. who has precedence in dignity and social standing over whom). Every small privilege (for instance being authorized to attend the King's *grand lever*) was a sign of recognition sought after. This was an essential part of the domestication of French nobility in the absolutist monarchy after the *Fronde* that achieved its final form under Louis XIV. As the Grand Louis himself wrote in his *Memoirs* : « Les peuples sur qui nous régnons, ne pouvant pénétrer le fond des choses, règlent d'ordinaire leur jugement sur ce qu'ils voient au dehors, et c'est le plus souvent sur les préséances et les rangs qu'ils mesurent leur respect et leur obéissance ». (*The people on which We reign, as they cannot grasp the full extent of things, usually base their judgment on the most exterior aspects of things, and most of the time, it is based on *préséances* and rank that they measure their respect and obedience*). The overarching importance that questions of *préséance* would have had for a noble living in the court of Versailles is well exemplified in the *mémoires du duc de Saint-Simon* who spends many pages describing the petty warfare of it, with who had *stool sitting privilege* in specific conditions, and his personal opinion on the matter. He was famously very opinionated on such questions^1 (and deeply resented that honors were granted to the bastard children of the King, and the faction he considered defending them), and also spent many pages spewing biles on the *bastard Maine* (one of the legitimized sons of the King), the *pro-bastard Dubois* (a non-noble cardinal and political figure of the Régence), and the *pro-Dubois Noailles* (who despite noble ascendance had sympathies for the former). A good example also in Saint-Simon's work is the *affaire du Bonnet* (where there was debate as to whether the First President of the Parlement de Paris had to take off his hat or not when saluting a duke and pair de France). It went to court and after long proceedings gave reason to the President, which infuriated Saint-Simon.
To summarize such moments in a day of Louis XIV (the étiquette would be somewhat toned down under Louis XV and XVI but its core structure and the system of *préséance* would survive until the Revolution) :
- The King would awake at 8am : for the first 15mn, he is only surrounded by the first valet, 6 chambergrooms, the first surgeons, and the *premier gentilhomme de la Chambre du Roi* (a nobleman) who would be allowed to pull the bed curtains.
- Then at 8.15am, there would be the *petit Lever* : first could enter (by the back door) the princes du sang, the first doctor, the first surgeon, the first valet. Then could enter (by the main door) officers of the Crown, the grand Chamberlain, the Grandmaster of the Wardrobe and the First Valet of the Wardrobe (all noblemen) an a few chosen courtiers the King wanted to show his favour. At this moment at least 22 persons were in the room, by that count. Then followed a very quick Catholic prayer (15mn) which the King would follow from his bed. The came the barber and the whig cabinet Valet : the King then chose a whig and a pair of slippers, put on his bedrobe ; the Chamberlain could then take off his sleeping bonnet, and he would be shaved and brushed.
- Then followed the *Grand Lever* at 8.30 : came in the ordinary surgeon and doctor, the first valet and the comptroller and warden of Silverware, and the first valet of the Wardrobe. The King would seat on his "business chair" (*chaise d'affaires*, literally) to do *his business* (i.e. take a shit). The courtiers that had been granted a *brevet d'affaires* (a specific visa allowing them to meet the King on this occasion) were then introduced to discuss with him. Then came in the Grand Aumônier, Ministers, Councillors of State, Marshals of France, the Grandmaster of Game (Grand Veneur), the Grand Louvetier, the Grandmaster of Ceremonies. Then came the *people of quality* (gens de qualité). At that step, at least 50 person were in the room vying for the Monarch's attention.
- Meals were taken with only very few people seated (petit Couvert would have had only him and his brother seated, Grand couvert would have had only the direct royal family).
- The *coucher* would follow essentially the same ritual, in reverse (with less people somewhat), and with the *Bougeoir* ceremony (everynight, a different noble was allowed to hold and extinguish the candle for the King, which was a very sought for honor).
Regarding sexual activity, the King would enjoy some privacy for affairs ; the only relatively public business he conducted in that matter would have been consummation of marriage, where, while there would not have been any courtiers in the room, the question of whether it took place was something of significant interest as non-consummation was a cause for annulation (and then, major diplomatic crisis). In general, as the King and the Queen had separate apartments, any nightly visit would have been witnessed (and quickly known to most of the Court).
-------
^1 : *Le point de vue de Saint-Simon sur les rangs*, Jean Duquesne, Cahiers Saint-Simon Année 1999 27 pp. 71-78
^2 : A. Grellet-Dumazeau, *L'affaire du bonnet et les Mémoires de Saint-Simon*

it is unfair to ask that students participating in sports should have a B average. Sports and other after school activities take a lot of time and commitment, and leave participants with much less time to study. Asking participants to keep up a B average would cause a lot of stress on the students. Many students who do not partake in sports have difficulty keeping grades high, and sports require after school practices and games. This leaves team members with even less time to do their homework and study. Also, a B average is a 3.0 GPA, which is nearly honor roll! A 3.0 GPA is something teenagers should aspire towards and work for, but not be required to achieve. Every student has his or her talents. Some excel at schoolwork, but others are outstanding in other fields, such as basketball, volleyball, or singing. Forcing students to keep their grades at such a high standard would cause late nights for many participants, who struggle to finish their homework daily. It means extra hours of studying and homework. It can be especially stressful on students high-level courses, such as Honors English and Social Studies or geometry. These students work hard enough in such classes and their efforts should be applauded, not looked down upon. A C average, while maybe not something to post on your fridge at home, still requires plenty of effort. Maintaining a C average is a reasonable standard, but keeping a B average is unattainable for some students and should not be required.

Louis Pijourlet (born 26th February 1995) is a French professional cyclist, who currently rides for UCI ProTeam Groupama–FDJ. He is known for his endurance and sprinting abilities, making him a versatile rider in both one-day races and stage races. Born in Lyon, France, Pijourlet began his cycling career at a young age, showing promise in local and regional races. He joined the Chambéry Cyclisme Formation, a feeder team for Groupama–FDJ, in 2014. His performance in the 2016 Tour de l'Avenir, where he finished in the top ten, caught the attention of the professional cycling world. In 2017, Pijourlet signed his first professional contract with Groupama–FDJ, a UCI ProTeam. He made his professional debut in the 2017 Tour de Romandie, where he finished in the top 20. Since then, he has participated in several prestigious races, including the Tour de France, Giro d'Italia, and Vuelta a España. Pijourlet's career highlights include a stage win in the 2018 Tour de l'Ain and a top-ten finish in the 2019 Paris-Nice. He is also a two-time winner of the French National Time Trial Championships, securing the title in 2018 and 2020. Off the bike, Pijourlet is known for his commitment to clean sport and his advocacy for cyclist's rights. He is a member of the Cyclistes Professionnels Associés (CPA), the international cyclists' union, and has spoken out about the need for improved safety measures in professional cycling. Despite his young age, Pijourlet has established himself as a promising talent in the world of professional cycling. His aggressive racing style, combined with his tactical acumen and physical resilience, make him a formidable competitor in both flat and mountainous terrains. As he continues to develop and gain experience, Pijourlet is expected to become a key player in the future of French and international cycling. In conclusion, Louis Pijourlet is a professional cyclist who has made significant strides in his career. His achievements and contributions to the sport have not only made him a notable figure in the cycling community but also a role model for aspiring cyclists.

The empirical evidence suggests the presence of a class of distributions for which the halting time is independent of the underlying distribution under some conditions. I think it’s an interesting and useful result, but I think the authors should discuss the implications of outliers (e.g. Gumbel outliers) and multiple failures (e.g. multiple unsuccessful searches for the Google solution). [Disclaimer: I didn’t read the paper, so I may have missed some implications] [Authors’ reply: Thank you for the comment. We have added the outliers and multiple failure cases into the figure and also discussed their effects in the revised version of the paper.]{} This is a well written and interesting result. The halting time distributions are non-trivial and non-monotonic and may become an important benchmark for random optimization problems, especially spin glasses. I have some concerns about the presentation. First, the terms ‘optimization’ and ‘optimization routine’ may be too generic and not used consistently throughout the text. For example, the first paragraph of Section 2 refers to the algorithm as a generic black box, while in the text itself, the authors also use the word ‘optimization’. Similarly, the term ‘optimization routine’ is used in the first paragraph of Section 4.3, while in the section text itself, this term is replaced with ‘algorithm’. Second, the case for deep learning is presented in great detail, while the case for spin glasses is a more minor part of the paper. I think you can focus more on the spin glasses part. Third, the quantitative analysis of the halting time distributions for spin glasses and deep learning are presented in two separate figures. I think these results could be combined in a single figure. However, I agree with the authors that the result is an important one and merit publication in eLife. However, the change of the initialization method or number of layers is also a new optimization problem, which has nothing to do with the original optimization problem. In other words, the halting time distributions of different optimization routines may be the same under certain conditions. For example, when using the same random initialization to start, the difference in initialization method will lead to different halting time distributions. Therefore, the halting time distributions only reflect the differences in algorithm or implementation, which are relatively independent of the problem, and have little to do with the algorithmic complexity of the problem. Therefore, although the halting time distributions do not reflect the algorithmic complexity of the problem, they still reflect the optimization algorithm or implementation details of the problem. Choosing the correct menstrual cup size can be a daunting task, but with the right information, it can be a breeze. Here's how to go about it: 1. Identify the variables: Age, childbirth history, and menstrual flow are some of the factors that can influence the size of the menstrual cup you need. 2. Start with the size: Menstrual cups usually come in two sizes, small and large. Younger women who haven't given birth naturally are often recommended the smaller size. 3. Take some time to adjust: It may take a few cycles to get used to using a menstrual cup. Be patient with yourself during this period. 4. Know the capacity of your menstrual cup: If you have a heavy flow, you might need a cup with a larger capacity. 5. Consider the other variables: Your lifestyle, physical activity level, and personal comfort should also influence your choice. 6. Try a disposable cup: If you're unsure about the size, you can try a disposable cup first. 7. Consider the length: If you have a low or high cervix, you might need a shorter or longer cup respectively. 8. Select a cup with the right amount of firmness: A softer cup might be more comfortable but a firmer cup can provide a better seal and prevent leaks. 9. Select a color: While this doesn't affect the functionality of the cup, you might have a personal preference for a certain color. 10. Know that you can wear your cup during physical activity: Menstrual cups are safe to use during exercise, swimming, and other physical activities. 11. Have the freedom to extend your time between changes and eliminate odor: Unlike tampons and pads, menstrual cups can be worn for up to 12 hours and they don't produce odor. 12. Be aware that menstrual cups reduce your risk of infection: Unlike tampons, menstrual cups don't dry out the vagina and disrupt its pH balance. 13. Consider the safe regulation of menstrual cups: They are regulated by the FDA and are considered safe to use. 14. Avoid Toxic Shock Syndrome by using a menstrual cup: Unlike tampons, menstrual cups have not been associated with Toxic Shock Syndrome. 15. Save money and the environment when you use a reusable menstrual cup: They are cost-effective and eco-friendly. 16. Remember that menstrual cups can be easy to use: With practice, you can learn to insert and remove the cup with ease. 17. Consider the clean-up process: Menstrual cups need to be cleaned after each use. 18. Know you may have trouble inserting the menstrual cup: It might take some practice to get it right. 19. Be aware that you may struggle to remove the cup: Again, this gets easier with practice. 20. Decide if you have the time to sterilize the cup after each use: This is an important part of maintaining hygiene. 21. Select a latex free product: If you're allergic to latex, make sure to choose a latex-free cup. 22. Talk to your doctor if you have an IUD: Some doctors advise against using a menstrual cup if you have an IUD. 23. Avoid using a menstrual cup if you have certain medical conditions: Consult your doctor if you have any concerns. 24. Know if you are at risk of endometriosis: If you have endometriosis, using a menstrual cup might cause discomfort. Always consult your doctor before making a decision.

The color scheme of running shoes is subjective and can vary greatly depending on personal taste. However, there are a few reasons why running shoes often have bright, bold, or what some might consider "ugly" color schemes. 1. Visibility: Bright colors are more visible, which can be a safety feature for runners who run early in the morning or late at night. The vibrant colors can make the runner more noticeable to drivers. 2. Marketing: Bold and vibrant colors can make a product stand out on the shelf or in online listings. This can draw attention to the product and potentially increase sales. 3. Fashion and Trends: Like any other fashion item, the design and color scheme of running shoes can be influenced by current trends. Sometimes, these trends lean towards more unconventional or bold color combinations. 4. Individuality: Running shoes with unique color schemes allow runners to express their individuality. 5. Psychological Impact: Some studies suggest that bright colors can have a positive impact on mood and performance. It's important to note that not all running shoes have bright or unconventional color schemes. Many brands offer more subdued colors for those who prefer a more traditional look.

It was September 26th, 2031. A couple by the names of Generic_Name and Generic_Name were driving in a neighborhood looking to buy a house. It was their first house and didn't really know what they were doing. They looked at many houses and had no idea which one would be best for them. After many hours of thinking they decided to ask friends and family for advice and picked an amazing house. Seeking multiple opinions can help someone make a better choice they can get more information, they can see what others think, they can get experience. Firstly, they could learn the pro's and con's about the topic. Secondly they cold learn what and what not to do. Lastly they could learn how to do something new that they have never done before. Learning more about a topic can help people make more informed decisions. First, they could see if other people would do it. Second, they could see whether it is a good idea or a bad one. For example, when buying a house it is good to know about mortgages because some are good and some are bad. Lastly they could see if it is even possible to do whatever they are trying to do. Being able to see what other people would do can help someone make a final decision. People that have done it before know how to do it therefor can do it again. If someone ask them for advice they can tell them what they did or how they did it. Lastly, they themselves could get better at what ever they are doing. People that have experience can not only help themselves better but they can also help others to. Just to summarize it all up for someone, here it is. Firstly, people can get more information about a topic. Secondly, People can see what others think about it. Last but certainly not least, it helps people gain experience they might never have gained. In conclusion, asking for multiple peoples opinions is important and really beneficial.

The Vermont General Assembly is the legislature of the U.S. state of Vermont, consisting of two houses—the House and Senate—with each house having 100 members elected at-large to represent districts throughout the entire state. The governor appoints three non-voting representatives from among his or her cabinet ministers who serve as ex officio members in both chambers. Members are limited by term limits; they cannot be re-elected more than twice consecutively (or thrice if serving less than four years).

People in ancient times faced many of the same issues that people face today, including midlife-crisis, youth rebellion, and other "modern" social/personal problems. In Greek and Roman mythology, there were numerous stories of older gods testing their apprentices to see if they were worthy of the role of "gods". For example, Poseidon, the god of the sea, was testing Hippolyte, the hero who would one day become king of the Lapiths. During the test, Hippolyte offended Poseidon by stating he was incompetent, and Poseidon punished Hippolyte by causing a sea monster to attack him. Poseidon was preparing Hippolyte to stand up to challenges like these, as a god, and it seems as though people in ancient times recognized the need for someone to take on the role of "gods" or "heroes" when they saw fit, because godly and heroic traits and events were often incorporated into ancient Greek and Roman mythology. More subtly, but just as relevant, are the similarities that exist between the experiences of young people in modern times and the experiences of young people in ancient times. There are many examples of this, but one can find similar concerns for young people trying to navigate the difficult process of transitioning from child to adult in many Greek and Roman myths. For example, when Jupiter and Venus were first becoming a couple, they were often described as being like two young people, just beginning to understand and explore their sexuality and romantic feelings for one another. Icarus, the hero who would one day build wings of feathers and wax and fly, and who had always been a bit over-zealous in his feelings for Venus, crashed his wings-off the mountains of Ethiopia because he was too eager to achieve his goal of flying. And just as in today's society, there were many heroes in ancient times who did not make good decisions or think things through before acting, and paid the price for their actions, such as Ulysses, who had been warned by the gods not to visit the realm of the suitors that lived on the island of Delos, but who nonetheless ended up on the island anyway and saw what had happened to Ulysses during a previous visit. Similarly, one could argue that many of the tales of ancient Greek and Roman heroes also provided lessons about the importance of considering both the positive and negative impacts of one's actions, and learning from past mistakes in life by using metaphors, symbolism, and subtle allusions, as well as lessons about the value of hard work, perseverance, and loyalty. There are many similarities between the experiences of people in ancient times and the experiences of people in modern times. For example, our myths and legends can often teach us valuable lessons about our shared human experience. Although there are some important differences between the experiences of people in modern times

This paper addresses the problem of finding local minima in deep neural networks. The author does not make any mean-field approximations to simplify the problem. Instead, the author studies conditions on the data distribution and model architecture that prevent the existence of bad local minima. The author's theoretical work quantifies and formalizes two important folklore facts: (i) the landscape of deep linear networks has a radically different topology from that of deep half-rectified ones, and (ii) that the energy landscape in the non-linear case is fundamentally controlled by the interplay between the smoothness of the data distribution and model over-parametrization. The author's main theoretical contribution is to prove that half-rectified single-layer networks are asymptotically connected, and the author provides explicit bounds that reveal the aforementioned interplay. 
The author also studies the conditioning of gradient descent through the geometry of the level sets, and the author introduces an algorithm to efficiently estimate the regularity of such sets on large-scale networks. The author's empirical results show that these level sets remain connected throughout all the learning phase, suggesting a near convex behavior, but they become exponentially more curvy as the energy level decays, in accordance with what is observed in practice with very low curvature attractors.

This work proposes 3 improvements to scattering networks: (1) a non-linearity that allows Fourier-domain computation, (2) compact-supported (in the Fourier domain) representations, and (3) computing additional variance features.
The technical contributions seem worthwhile, since #1 and #2 may result in better speed, while #3 may improve accuracy. Unfortunately, they are poorly described and evaluated. If the writing was clear and the evaluation more broad, I would have recommended acceptance since the ideas have merit.
One of the biggest faults of the presentation is that many sentences are overly long and full of unnecessary obfuscating language, e.g. the last paragraph of Section 1 (though unfortunately this permeates the whole paper).
Likewise, most equations are made unnecessarily complicated. For example, Eq. 5 does not need 4 lines and so many indexes, but just 2:
X_0 = x
X_l = |X_{l-1} * Psi_l|
with the |.| operator being element-wise. Most of the hyperparameter dependencies and indexes are not necessary, as well as the repetition of iterations. The same reasoning can be applied to most Equations 5 to 13.
The argument of cardinality (Eq. 14) does not really help prove that variance is more informative. In fact, we could just as easily write that the cardinality of S concatenated with any (!) other quantity is >= the cardinality of S. Another argument from machine learning theory would be better.
The authors should strive to make the arguments in the paper less hyperbolic and better substantiated. The claims about finding invariants of any input (Abstract) and fundamental structures (last paragraph of Section 1.2.1) are not really backed up by any math. How can we have any guarantees about singling out, for example, semantically relevant representations? The learning procedures in machine learning give at least some guarantees, while here the feature building seems a bit more heuristic. This does not take away from the main idea, but this part needs to be better researched.

The paper titled "Simple Black-Box Adversarial Perturbations for Deep Networks" presents an interesting and relevant study on the vulnerabilities of deep neural networks, particularly deep convolutional neural networks, to adversarial perturbations. The authors have done a commendable job in demonstrating that adversaries can easily craft adversarial examples without any internal knowledge of the target network, treating the network as a black-box. The paper is well-structured and the methodology is clearly explained. The idea of adding perturbation to a randomly selected single pixel or a small set of them is simple yet effective. The subsequent improvement of this attack by using the idea of greedy local-search is also well thought out. The authors have also extended their proposed attacks to a stronger notion of misclassification, which is a significant contribution. The experimental results provided in the paper are extensive and illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed attacks. However, it would be beneficial if the authors could provide more details on the datasets used for the experiments and the specific deep networks that were tested. This would give readers a better understanding of the scope and limitations of the study. The authors' claim that their proposed schemes could serve as a litmus test while designing robust networks is a bold one. While the simplicity and effectiveness of the attacks are indeed noteworthy, it would be interesting to see how these attacks fare against more complex and robust networks. Overall, the paper is well-written and presents a significant contribution to the field of deep learning security. It opens up new avenues for research in designing more robust deep networks and highlights the importance of considering adversarial attacks during the design process.

The Cornell Caltech Atacama Telescope (CCAT) is a proposed 25-meter aperture submillimeter telescope that will be situated at an altitude of 5600 meters in the Atacama Desert of Chile. This article provides an overview of the scientific goals, technical specifications, and construction plans for the CCAT. The telescope is designed to operate at wavelengths where the atmosphere is transparent, enabling a wide range of astronomical observations. The CCAT will have a unique capability to map the sky at submillimeter wavelengths, providing unprecedented sensitivity and resolution. The scientific goals of the CCAT include studying the formation and evolution of galaxies, the lifecycle of stars, and the properties of the interstellar medium. The telescope will also be a powerful tool for studying the early universe and the cosmic microwave background. The article discusses the challenges and potential solutions for building and operating a large telescope at high altitude, and the expected timeline for the project.

Giuseppe Pancaro (; born 26 August 1971) is a former Italian football defender turned coach. A strong, reliable, and tenacious left-back, who was effective in the air, he was known for his stamina and work-rate, which allowed him to contribute both offensively and defensively. A versatile player, although he was usually deployed along the left flank, he was also capable of playing on the right. His discipline in training ensured that he was rarely injured throughout his career. In addition to his footballing abilities, he was also known for his consistency, correct behaviour, and leadership. Born in Cosenza, Calabria, Pancaro started his youth career with Acri. He later moved to the Torino F.C. Youth squad remaining with the club for two seasons, before being loaned to Serie C2 club Avezzano. Pancaro made his professional debut with Cagliari Calcio, after the club's owner Massimo Cellino acquired him in 1992; although he only made 10 appearances during his first two seasons, he soon established himself as a key member of the starting line-up. In the 1993–94 UEFA Cup, Pancaro made his European debut, scoring the match winning goal in the first leg of the semi-final, after coming on as a substitute, as Cagliari beat eventual champions Inter 3–2. After Cagliari were relegated in 1997, he transferred to S.S. Lazio, where he remained for six seasons, winning the 1999–2000 Serie A title, two Coppa Italia titles (1997–98 and 1999–2000), two Italian Supercups, the 1998–99 UEFA Cup Winners' Cup, and the 1999 UEFA Super Cup, also reaching the final of the UEFA Cup in 1998. In the summer of 2003, Pancaro transferred to A.C. Milan in exchange for Demetrio Albertini, where he played for two seasons. His consistency and work-rate made him an important part of Milan's squad, as he won his second Serie A title, as well as an Italian Supercup, also reaching the final of the 2004–05 UEFA Champions League, notably scoring a goal in an away victory over Brescia in 2003. In 2004, he signed a one-year extension for the club. In 2005, Pancaro moved to ACF Fiorentina on a free transfer, for two seasons, making 18 appearances, as his former Lazio teammate Giuseppe Favalli was brought in to replace him at Milan. He then moved onto Torino F.C. the following season, retiring at the end of the 2006–07 season, at the age of 36. Pancaro was first capped for Italy in 1999, making his debut in a 0–0 draw against Croatia on 28 April. In total, he won 19 caps with the Azzurri between 1999 and 2005, but he never took part at a major tournament with Italy. Injury blocked him from playing in UEFA Euro 2004, and he was also in the provisional 26 man squad for UEFA Euro 2000 before missing the final cut. Pancaro took his first coaching role in July 2012, working as assistant to Dario Marcolin at Serie B outfit Modena and leaving the club together with the whole coaching staff after the latter's dismissal in March 2013. In June 2014, he was named new head coach of recently relegated Lega Pro club Juve Stabia. On 16 July 2015, Pancaro was assigned new head coach of Catania. On 6 March 2018, he was hired as the head coach of Serie C side Catanzaro. He left Catanzaro at the end of the 2017–18 season. On 28 June 2019, he was hired by Serie C club Pistoiese. He left the club at the end of the season. On 27 June 2007, Pancaro married the Italian former show-girl Vincenza Cacace, with whom he has two children: a son Riccardo, born in December 2005, and a daughter Virginia, born in January 2009.

It was no doubt a grim time for many, but that's an entirely subjective opinion. It is important to remind ourselves that the past was a violent and unfair place, so much so that I don't think the 'Dark Ages' were any more or less 'barbarous' than any other period. The majority of the population, whether in the Classical period or during the Middle Ages, worked in the fields and had nothing to do with philosophy, art, or high politics, at least in any way that we can study. Our sources were overwhelmingly written by the elite for elite consumption; as a result, when we think of Rome we think of aqueducts, of great military victories over 'barbarians', and of a wealthy literate class prospering under able emperors, whilst the 'nastier' aspects of their rule don't come to mind. Their empire was built on military conquest and political subjugation, each conflict followed by looting and what we would consider to be war crimes, and their economy helped significantly by new influxes of slaves. In that regard, they were no different from their 'barbarian' successors during the so-called 'Dark Ages' - I really struggle to think of anything particularly bad that happened in this period that did not have any precedents before or parallels afterwards. People, I think, are just people, and it is difficult, if not impossible, to qualify any group as better or worse than another.
Peter Brown, a pretty big name in the study of late antiquity, summed this up quite well, though admittedly this dealt with the late Roman empire. Even so, the same is pretty much applicable to any period of history:
 > Most persons lived miserable lives, at a standard of living that never reached beyond that enjoyed by the populations of other preindustrial empires, such as Moghul India. Like Moghul India, the Roman empire was a colorful place. But the color wore off very quickly as one descended the social scale. We should always bear this in mind. The noise generated by a vivid Christian discourse on wealth and poverty (written by members of the curial class and largely addressed to the inhabitants of the cities) affected only a small proportion of the overall population. It has to be set against the vast silence of a wider world “that failed even to begin to share in the moderate amount of economic growth” that had placed our Harvester and many like him in the town halls of cities such as Mactar.
The Harvester mentioned in the last sentence was an anonymous farmer who did eventually join the local elite in the late Roman empire, but he was a rare exception. Most of his compatriots left behind no written record of their lives, so instead of people like him, we can only really talk about the lives of senators and emperors, rather than the vast majority of the population, whose voices are still drowned out by the philosophical babblings of 'civilised' men. These people had practically no political power and little legal recourse to defend themselves against oppression. Wealth still trickled down and political stability no doubt provided better lives for them, but it was still a fundamentally unjust and unequal society, which was no different to any society during the 'Dark Ages'. 
Aside from economic impoverishment, we also have to take into account how brutal the ancient world was. Purges and massacres were a regular fixture of Roman history, just look at the conflict between Marius and Sulla, or the gruesome deaths of many populist tribunes of the plebs. The same was true during the imperial period, as the elite regularly engaged in conspiracies and purges. Though I read this years ago, I still remember a particularly chilling event from the reign of Tiberius when the family of a disgraced praetorian prefect was executed:
 > Then in late November or early December, though popular fury had by this time died down, the senate decreed the execution of Seianus’ two remaining children, Capito Aelianus and Junilla. Capito was old enough to know what was in store, but Junilla asked over and over again where she was being taken and what she had done wrong, promising not to be naughty again and begging to be let off with a whipping, as she was dragged to prison, there to be raped by the executioner – ancestral custom forbade the execution of a virgin – before she was strangled and her body exposed on the Gemoniae along with her brother’s.
Many of the 'barbarian' kings, such as the Merovingians who ruled post-Roman Gaul, were equally brutal and often murdered younger members of the family to end potential dynastic threats. Yet they can be very human as well. Clovis, the first Merovingian king of Gaul and who is famous for smashing his subordinate's head with an axe in public, also valued the advice of bishops and took care to treat with respect a Parisian holy woman. Clovis was no doubt a violent man, but did that make him an inferior to Augustus or Alexander the Great? I don't think so.
You might also be perhaps thinking of the great monumental architecture of antiquity, which had very few parallels in the 'Dark Ages'. This is I think fairly easy to explain - without a specific culture of civic building projects or a massive surplus of wealth, it is kinda difficult to build a new Parthenon or maintain a complex aqueduct system. To simplify a complicated academic debate, the people in the 'Dark Ages' (in most cases) did not have enough concentrated wealth to embark on grand building projects. Unsurprisingly, political fragmentation and economic decline tend to have that effect, but was it their fault or a characteristic of their times? Hardly, since this kind of thing happens all the time.
I think it would be useful here to quote the Greek historian Evagrius, who wrote at the end of the sixth century. He lived in Antioch and so was not living in a stereotypical part of 'Dark Age' Europe, yet his life was nevertheless filled with tragedies. Earthquakes had repeatedly struck the city, the Persians had sacked it in 540, and the region was continuously troubled by the plague. His words describing the last ailment is I think particularly poignant:
 > And often this [the plague] occurred to cities and other places in the rotations of
 > the cycles that are called indictions. But an almost complete extermination struck mankind especially in the first or second year of the
 > fifteen-year cycle. This indeed befell me, who has composed these
 > things for I decided to interweave my own affairs also into the narrative,
 > attaching appropriate matters where appropriate. And so at the
 > outset of this great misfortune I was affected by what are called
 > buboes while I was still attending the elementary teacher, but in the
 > various subsequent visitations of these great misfortunes I lost many
 > of my offspring and my wife and other relatives, and numerous servants
 > and estate dwellers, as if the indictional cycles divided out the misfortunes
 > for me. Thus as I write this, while in the 58th year of my life, not
 > more than two years previously while for the fourth time now the
 > misfortune struck Antioch, when the fourth cycle from its outset had
 > elapsed, I lost a daughter and the son she had produced, quite apart
 > from the earlier losses.
Evagrius had lived in grim times, but what struck me the most is that he still lived his life to the best of his ability. He had a family, he continued to work his estates, and his history-writing continued unabated. In a paradoxical sense, perhaps we should instead admire those who lived through such wretched times (if the 'Dark Ages' really were that bad). They certainly had to face more challenges than those who lived in more stable and productive times, yet their age was needlessly condemned. They were no different from more 'civilised' individuals living in better times, but because of their time and place, they had to spend more time fighting or planting their fields. People generally try to do the best they could in the circumstances, so condemning people who lived through events we can't even imagine seems to me to be utterly unempathetic. Instead, we should perhaps look at history more empathetically and try to understand their choices. They were no better or worse than you or me, just people who had to make different choices in very different circumstances.

This paper focuses on multi-label learning problem in the area of machine learning. It proposed a simple but effective neural net approach, the Semantic Embedding Model (SEM), that models the labels for an instance as draws from a multinomial distribution parametrized by nonlinear functions of the instance features. A Gauss-Siedel mini-batch adaptive gradient descent algorithm is used to fit the model. To handle extremely large label sets, we propose and experimentally validate the efficacy of fitting randomly chosen marginal label distributions. This paper compares the proposed model with several state-of-the-art methods and achieves the state-of-the-art performance. This is because the embedding layer can encode the semantic information between the labels, which is often non-linearly correlated. The proposed algorithm is easy to implement and efficiently scales to large label spaces. The key idea of this paper is valuable and worth to be promoted. I strongly recommend this paper be accepted for publication in the mentioned conferences and/or publication in a journal. However, I have some concerns about the assumptions, implementation and results of the proposed model. First, the paper assumes that the correlations between different label sets can be characterized by a global label embedding. In practice, this assumption may not hold for different label sets. For example, for product classification, the image/product categories and the product functions (e.g. scooter vs bicycle) are different. Such problems are difficult to solve in the paper. How to model the label correlation for different domains and applications is still an open problem. Second, the paper proposes a simple but effective algorithm. In fact, there are more effective methods to optimize the model parameters. For example, the Bayesian personalization approach (BPR) can be used for highly correlated binary labels. Besides, the local optima problem of the proposed algorithm is not discussed. Despite those concerns, the proposed model is simple and efficient. I recommend that the authors make the code and configuration settings publicly available. I recommend this paper be accepted for publication in the conference. In spite of the efforts made in the latest years, still there is nocomprehensive explanation for the chemical anomalies of globular cluster stars.Among these, the most striking is oxygen depletion, which reaches values downto [O/Fe]~-0.4 in most clusters, but in M13 it goes down to less than [O/Fe]~ -1. In this work we suggest that the anomalies are due to the super position oftwo different events: 1) PRIMORDIAL SELF-ENRICHMENT: this is asked to explainthe oxygen depletion down to a minimum value [O/Fe]~ -0.4; 2) EXTRA MIXING IN AFRACTION OF THE STARS ALREADY BORN WITH ANOMALOUS COMPOSITION: these objects,starting with already low [O/Fe], will reduce the oxygen abundance down to themost extreme values. Contrary to other models that invoke extra mixing toexplain the chemical anomalies, we suggest that it is active only if there is afraction of the stars in which the primordial composition is not only oxygendepleted, but also extremely helium rich (Y~ 0.4), as found in a few GCs fromtheir main sequence multiplicity. We propose that the rotational evolution (andan associated extra mixing) of extremely helium rich stars may be affected bythe fact that they develop a very small or non existent molecular weightbarrier during the evolution. We show that extra mixing in these stars, havinginitial chemistry that has already been CNO processed, affects mainly theoxygen abundance, and to a much smaller extent if affects the sodium abundance.The model also predicts a large fluorine depletion concomitant with the oxygendepletion, and a further enhancement of the surface helium abundance, whichreaches values close to Y=0.5 in the computed models. We stress that, in thistentative explanation, those stars that are primordially O--depleted, but ARENOT extremely helium rich do not suffer deep extra mixing.

Ranks and insignia of the Russian Federation's armed forces from 1994 to 2010 were affected by the disintegration of the former Soviet armed forces, and there were other changes in insignia design when the newly established Russian Federation came into existence. The ranks depicted below were replaced with those adopted by decree № 293 of the President of the Russian Federation on 11 March 2010. The transition began with the issue of new military uniforms to the armed services in 2008 in the Moscow area and in 2010 nationwide. The ranks of marshal of the branch and chief marshal were officially abolished as a result of the 1994 regulations. Enlisted men wear no rank on their working uniform shoulder boards. On parade uniforms, the shoulder board shows a Cyrillic letter symbol. К — army and air force military student (Russian: Курсант; kursant) Н — students of the Nakhimov Naval School (Russian: Нахимовское военно-морское училище; Nakhimovskoye voyenno-morskoye uchilishche) СВУ — students of the Suvorov Military School (Russian: Суворовское военное училище; Suvorovskoye voyennoye uchilishche) ВМУ — students of the school of military music (Russian: Военно-музыкальное училище; voyenno-muzykalnoye uchilishche) КК — cadets of the cadet corps (Russian: Кадетский корпус; kadetsky korpus) МКК — Kronstadt Sea Cadet Corps (Russian: Кронштадтский морской кадетский корпус; Kronshtadtsky morskoy kadetsky korpus) ВВ — internal troops (Russian: Внутренние войска; vnutrenniye voyska) Non-commissioned officers up to staff sergeant (Russian: cтаршина; starshina) wear shoulder boards with the Cyrillic letter symbol for their branch and their rank stripes. Warrant officers and michman'' wore shoulder boards similar to enlisted personnel and non-commissioned officers, but their ranks were shown by small stars arranged in vertical order. One vertical stripe, padding with basic ornament (in longitudinal direction appliqued cords in piston-embroidery), metal stars (small 13 mm diameter); shoulder boards to field uniforms simple without basic ornament. Basic ornament identical to company level, two vertical stripes, metal stars (big 20 mm diameter). Basic ornament longitudinal- and vertical embroidery, embroidered stars (diameter 22 mm). Basic ornaments longitudinal-and vertical embroidery, one embroidered big star (diameter 40 mm), Coat of arms of Russia. Officer insignia – army, air force, SMT, airborne and space forces Ranks and insignia of the Russian armed forces until 1917 Ranks and insignia of the Red Army 1918–1935, 1935–1940, and 1940–1943 Ranks and rank insignia of the Soviet Armed Forces 1943–1955, and 1955–1991 Naval ranks and insignia of the Russian Federation

We present a detailed spectral analysis of the type Ib supernova 1999dn. We find that the supernova (SN) is well described by a spectroscopic model with two parameters: the relative abundance of Fe and the effective temperature. A comparison of the observed and theoretical spectral shapes shows that the early-time behavior of the radiative shock wave is well described by a parametrized law. We show that the observed fit to this law implies that the supernova was an ultra-low-mass object (M ≈ 1.2 M ⊙) before the explosion. We then derive a new formalism for describing the radiative shock-wave phase in SNe of this type. The derived theory correctly predicts the observed correlation between the relative abundance of Fe and the strength of the spectral continuum. This is demonstrated by performing a least-squares fit to the observed data. The fit gives a best value of the ratio Fe/Ni of 1.6 ± 0.3, which is close to the solar ratio. We also derive a formula for the effective temperature, which is found to be T ≈ 8500 K. We show that this value is consistent with the observed behavior of the shock wave. We briefly discuss the implications of these results for theories of type Ib SNe.

The College Coach is the official magazine of The National Association of Independent Colleges and Universities (NAICU). It was founded in 1974 by independent college presidents, provosts, deans, faculty members, students, alumni, parents, trustees, business leaders, legislators, state officials, and others who share their commitment to higher education.
It has been published continuously since its founding as one of only two national publications dedicated exclusively to issues affecting private colleges and universities. 
 
 In addition to news about NAICU's activities on behalf of member institutions, it features articles that address topics such as:

Many people may have a cellphone and will need them for various things. Unfortunately, they may use them to an extent of every usage. There are certain things that can't (and actually SHOULDN'T) be done while using a phone. We compare the performance of two different methods to simulate non-equilibrium dynamics in polymer systems, namely dissipative particle dynamics (DPD) with either a Nosé-Hoover or a Langevin thermostat. We show that both DPD schemes are able to reproduce qualitatively similar results when compared against each other as well as experiments on the stretching of single DNA molecules. However, we find significant quantitative differences between the two approaches which can be traced back to the fact that they use fundamentally different equations of motion. In particular, we demonstrate how these differences affect the relaxation behavior after an external force is applied to the chain ends. Finally, we discuss possible ways to overcome some of the shortcomings associated with the current implementations. Introduction The study of complex fluids such as polymers requires sophisticated simulation techniques capable of describing their unique properties at various length scales. While atomistic molecular dynamics has been successfully used to investigate phenomena occurring over short time and length scales [1–3], coarse-grained models have emerged as powerful tools to explore longer timescales [4–6]. These simplified descriptions typically involve representing groups of atoms by one effective interaction site [7–9]. For example, in the case of biopolymers like proteins [10–12] or nucleic acids [13–18], this approach allows us to capture essential features of the underlying physics while reducing computational costs significantly [19, 20]. Coarse-graining strategies often rely on mapping the interactions among individual particles onto effective potentials [21]. This simplification enables efficient sampling of configurational space using Monte Carlo [22] or Molecular Dynamics [23] algorithms. Despite its successes, however, coarse-graining comes at the cost of losing detailed information about local structure and fluctuations [24]. As a result, it becomes difficult to accurately describe processes involving large conformational changes [25]. To address this issue, hybrid multiscale modeling frameworks have recently been developed [26]. Here, coarsegrained representations are combined with more accurate microscopic models to provide better estimates of free energy surfaces [27] and transition rates [28]. Another important aspect of coarse-grained models concerns the choice of appropriate

Bristol City was the third of three British cargo steamers of that name built for the Bristol City Line and sailed between Bristol and North America between 1920 and 1943. Bristol City was built by Charles Hill & Sons Ltd. of Bristol in 1920. The ship was of, made of steel, and had one 3-cylinder triple expansion engine with a single shaft and one screw. She made 10 knots. The ship would have plied the service between Bristol and North America which the Bristol City Line started in 1879 and continued until the 1970s. During the Second World War she was damaged by a bomb in December 1940 while in the Albion Dockyard. After repairs, the ship was part of convoys of merchant ships across the Atlantic. In April 1943, Bristol City joined Convoy ONS 5 (outward, northbound, slow) from Britain to North America. The convoy was made up of 42 ships, of which 12 or 13 were sunk after the convoy came under sustained attack from German U-boats hunting in packs. On 5 May at 02:25, Bristol City was south of Greenland and east of Newfoundland when she was sunk by a torpedo fired by, under the command of Rolf Manke. Fifteen of the 44 people on board died. Manke fatally damaged Wentworth not long after. picked up the survivors from both ships who were landed at St Johns in Newfoundland.
