This paper proposes a novel learning method for a mixture of recurrent neuralnetwork (RNN) experts model, which can acquire the ability to generate desiredsequences by dynamically switching between experts. Our method is based onmaximum likelihood estimation, using a gradient descent algorithm. Thisapproach is similar to that used in conventional methods; however, we modifythe likelihood function by adding a mechanism to alter the variance for eachexpert. The proposed method is demonstrated to successfully learn Markov chainswitching among a set of 9 Lissajous curves, for which the conventional methodfails. The learning performance, analyzed in terms of the generalizationcapability, of the proposed method is also shown to be superior to that of theconventional method. With the addition of a gating network, the proposed methodis successfully applied to the learning of sensory-motor flows for a smallhumanoid robot as a realistic problem of time series prediction and generation.

In this paper, we explore the Reissner-Nordström spacetime within the framework of the Tetrad Theory of Gravitation. We begin by providing a brief overview of the Tetrad Theory and its implications for our understanding of gravitational phenomena. We then delve into the specifics of the Reissner-Nordström spacetime, discussing its unique properties and how it fits into the broader context of the Tetrad Theory. We also provide a detailed analysis of the mathematical models used to describe this spacetime, highlighting the key equations and principles involved. Our findings suggest that the Tetrad Theory offers a robust and comprehensive framework for understanding the Reissner-Nordström spacetime, opening up new avenues for future research in this area.

Pregnancy lasts 11-12 months. The last three weeks or so prior to delivery (foaling) are very critical. During this period, the mother horse's body prepares itself for giving birth; its blood volume increases dramatically while at the same time, the mother's appetite decreases significantly. It is during these final few weeks when most problems occur with horses who do not get proper care. In order to avoid such complications, make sure that you take good care of your pregnant mare throughout the whole process until the day of birth. This section covers all aspects related to caring for a pregnant mare right up to the moment of birth.
The following sections cover topics including how to: Prepare yourself for the arrival of the new foal; Feed your pregnant mares; Take extra precautions regarding health issues which might arise due to pregnancy; Know what happens immediately... Continue reading →
Preparation for Birth
Around two weeks before birth, prepare an area near the barn for the mare to give birth. A stall would work well if available, otherwise use a clean pen or pasture. Keep hay nearby since the mare needs plenty of energy just before birth. Do not put any other animals like cows, goats, sheep, pigs, chickens, cats, dogs, rabbits, ferrets, rats, mice, squirrels, deer, raccoons, skunks, opossums, bats, moles, hedgehogs, weasels, porcupines, snakes, lizards, frogs, turkeys, geese, ducks, pigeons, pheasants, peacocks, guinea hens, quail, partridges, quails, sparrows, starlings, crows, magpies, woodpeckers, jays, hawks, owls, kestrels, buzzards, falcons, ospreys, vultures, ravens, hummingbirds, butterflies, bees, wasps, hornets, ants, spiders, scorpions, earwigs, centipedes,...

The authors present an interesting approach to cross-language name tagging using Wikipedia as source of information.
They use statistical methods on co-occurrences in text fragments from different languages to find similar names across language boundaries. The results are promising but there is room for improvement especially with regard to precision (false positives) which seems rather low at this point. 
Strengths: 
A good idea that works well enough so far. 
Weaknesses: 
Precision could be improved further. This would require more sophisticated techniques such as machine learning algorithms trained specifically for each language pair involved. In addition it might help if they used some kind of stop word list to filter out common terms like "the" "of" etc., since these can lead to false positive matches between two very distant languages. 
Overall I think it's worthwhile pursuing research along those lines because the potential applications seem quite large - not only within wikipedia itself where links between articles written in different languages will become much easier once proper translations have been found automatically based on their titles alone, but also outside wikipedia e.g. when searching web pages containing multilingual content

This paper proposes to use a linear classifier as the probe for the informativeness of the hidden activations from different neural network layers. The training of the linear classifier does not affect the training of the neural network. 
The paper is well motivated for investigating how much useful information (or how good the representations are) for each layer. The observations in this paper agrees with existing insights, such as, 1) (Fig 5a) too many random layers are harmful. 2) (Fig 5b) training is helpful. 3) (Fig 7) lower layers converge faster than higher layer. 4) (Fig 8) too deep network is hard to train, and skip link can remedy this problem.
However, this paper has following problems:
1. It is not sufficiently justified why the linear classifier is a good probe. It is not crystal clear why good intermediate features need to show high linear classification accuracy. More theoretical analysis and/or intuition will be helpful. 
2. This paper does not provide much insight on how to design better networks based on the observations. Designing a better network is also the best way to justify the usefulness of the analysis.
Overall, this paper is tackling an interesting problem, but the technique (the linear classifier as the probe) is not novel and more importantly need to be better justified. Moreover, it is important to show how to design better neural networks using the observations in this paper.

If a human were to have a blood transfusion with a blood type that was not compatible with their own, it could lead to a serious and potentially life-threatening reaction known as a transfusion reaction. Our immune system is designed to attack anything it recognizes as foreign or different from the rest of the body. Each blood type has specific antigens on the surface of the red blood cells. If a person receives blood of a different type, their immune system will recognize the new blood as foreign due to the different antigens. This triggers an immune response, leading to the production of antibodies to attack the foreign blood cells. This immune response can cause the newly transfused blood cells to clump together or agglutinate, leading to a number of serious complications. These can include fever, chills, chest pain, back pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, hemoglobin in the urine, jaundice, difficulty breathing, low blood pressure, rapid heart rate, and in severe cases, kidney failure, shock, and death. This is why it's crucial to ensure that the blood type of the donor and recipient are compatible before a blood transfusion is performed. The most universally accepted blood for transfusion is type O negative as it lacks the antigens that would trigger an immune response.

The main idea behind this approach consists in introducing into consideration not only the usual electromagnetic field tensor Fμν but also its dual counterpart *Fμν. In order to obtain equations for both fields simultaneously one has to introduce additional terms into Lagrangian density L(Fμν,*Fμν ) that are quadratic with respect to derivatives of these fields. It turns out that such modified Lagrangian leads to the following set of equations: where is the d'Alembert operator, is the energy-momentum tensor of matter fields, and is the current four-vector describing the flow of electric charge. The President of the United States is currently determined by an electoral college. The electoral college process involves the public voting for their elector and not the main focus, the candidates. Through this, many of the states gain more power than they should, and the votes are set up as a "Winner-takes-all" system. This unneccessary and inaccurate use of votes is outdated and needs to be shut down. In an instant, an electoral college voter could change his or her mind and go against his or her original political party. This is a completely unfair use of power because it reverses all of the votes that went to the elector and to all of the voters who were counting on the elector to properly represent their vote. The voters have no say in whether the elector will stay true to his or her party. 
 The easiest way to prove that the electoral college must go, is to reveal the facts. In source 2: The Indefensible Electoral College it states "over sixty percent of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now". Multiple icons such as Richard Nixon, Jimmy Carter, Bob Dole, the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, and the AFL-CIO have stated they would like to abolish the electoral college as well! We now have the majority vote to put an end to the electoral college and the unfair system. "Perhaps most worrying is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote," says source 2. A tie in the votes, which is very much possible since the total number of votes is 538, according to source 1, could then allow the fate of America to rest in the hands of the House of Representatives, a miniscule representation of the people. If the voting system were to be changed to the popular vote, the results would accurately display the winner that the majority of America chose. 
 "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational," says Bradford Plumer, and he couldn't be closer to the truth. The electoral college system does not represent the people of America accurately and is more likely to result in a tie compared to the popular vote. To conclude, source 3 says the electoral college is "a non-democratic method of selecting a president that ought to be overruled by declaring the candidate who receives the most popular votes the winner

Nikola Tesla, a Serbian-American inventor, electrical engineer, mechanical engineer, and physicist, has indeed seen a resurgence in popularity in recent years. This can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, Tesla's work has had a profound impact on our modern world. He made significant contributions to the development of alternating current (AC) electrical systems, which are now the predominant method for electrical power transmission. He also conducted pioneering work in electromagnetic fields, which is the basis for wireless communication devices. Secondly, Tesla was a visionary who proposed ideas that were far ahead of his time. For instance, he envisioned a global wireless communication system at a time when the technology to realize such a system did not exist. He also predicted the advent of robots, radar, and solar power. Thirdly, Tesla's life story is filled with drama and intrigue, which makes him a fascinating figure. Despite his genius, he died penniless and in relative obscurity. His rivalry with Thomas Edison, another prominent inventor of his time, is also well-known. Lastly, the rise of electric vehicles and renewable energy has brought Tesla back into the spotlight. Tesla Motors, a company named in his honor, has played a significant role in popularizing electric cars. However, it's important to note that while Tesla was undoubtedly a brilliant inventor, his contributions are sometimes romanticized or exaggerated. For instance, he is often credited with inventing things that were actually developed by others. Furthermore, some of his ideas, such as the concept of transmitting power wirelessly over long distances, have yet to be realized. In conclusion, while Tesla was indeed an amazing inventor, the current "cult appreciation" of him is likely due to a combination of his significant contributions to modern technology, his visionary ideas, his intriguing life story, and the rise of technologies that he predicted.

The main contributions of this paper are as follows: 1. It formally characterizes why neural networks with a sinusoidal activation function are hard to train even for simple examples. 2. It explains good performance achieved on a typical classification task by the fact that the presence of infinitely many shallow local minima emerges from the architecture. 3. It shows that for some network architectures the presence of periodic cycles is ignored when the training is successful, and the learned functions only care about the long-term behaviors, which are not affected by the Sinusoidal activation functions. The reviewer strongly recommends avoiding this paper for publication due to the extremely narrow applicability of its main results, which are only valid for extremely simple scenarios. In particular, the paper shows that the bad training behavior for neural networks with sinusoidal activation functions can be linked to the infinitely many local minima, but the proofs also require extremely strong assumptions, such as infinitely many local minima and non-constant depth of the local minimum; Furthermore, none of the special structures employed in this paper are widely used in real-world applications. For example, the reviewer notes that while the CIFAR10 dataset is used in the paper, it is clear from looking at the CIFAR10 dataset that this is not a classification task that would be particularly difficult for a neural network with a sinusoidal activation function (the issue is not even raised in the paper, and it would be completely discarded by the good performance achieved on this dataset!). So the reviewer strongly recommends against publication of this paper in its present form. On the positive side, the reviewer appreciates the clear descriptions of the main results and the comparison of them to related work. In addition, the reviewer takes merit in the attempts to generalize the results to other scenarios, including CIFAR-10, using extremely strong assumptions (in particular infinitely many local minima). So the reviewer recommends minor revisions to the paper, for example by toning down the extremely strong assumptions, to make the results more broadly applicable, say, by removing the assumption of infinitely many local minima or the requirement of non-constant depth of local minimum. Overall the reviewer strongly recommends against the publication of this paper in its present form, because of its extremely limited applicability.

The paper addresses the problem of dependency parsing of Singlish, a major creole based on English, which is of interest to the ACL community both linguistically and computationally. The authors construct a dependency treebank under the Universal Dependencies scheme and train a neural network model by integrating English syntactic knowledge into a state-of-the-art parser trained on the Singlish treebank. The main question the paper seeks to answer is whether English knowledge can improve cross-lingual dependency parsing on low-resource languages like Singlish. Strengths of the paper include its novelty, as it is the first to use neural stacking to improve cross-lingual dependency parsing on low-resource languages. The authors also provide a clear methodology and present significant results, showing that English knowledge can lead to a 25% relative error reduction, resulting in a parser of 84.47% accuracies. The authors' decision to make both their annotation and parser available for further research is commendable and will likely facilitate future work in this area. However, the paper also has some weaknesses. The authors could have provided more background information on Singlish and why it is of interest to the ACL community. The paper could also benefit from a more detailed discussion of the Universal Dependencies scheme and why it was chosen for this study. Furthermore, while the authors mention that they used a state-of-the-art parser, they do not provide any information on what this parser is or why it was chosen. Lastly, while the results are promising, the authors could have compared their results with other methods or discussed potential improvements or future directions for their work.

The institution of slavery has been a source of much debate and controversy throughout history. While many people today may view slavery as a morally wrong and reprehensible practice, it is important to consider the perspectives and motivations of those who supported and defended slavery in the past.
One reason that many people were willing to fight for and defend slavery was that it was a deeply ingrained part of their society and culture. In many societies, slavery was a common and accepted practice, and it was often seen as a necessary and beneficial part of the economy. For example, in the American South, slavery was a way of life for many people, and it was seen as a way to provide labor for the cotton plantations.
Another reason that many people were willing to fight for and defend slavery was that they believed that it was a necessary and justifiable form of punishment for those who had committed crimes. In many societies, slavery was seen as a way to punish and discipline those who had violated the law. For example, in the Roman Empire, slaves were often used as a form of punishment for those who had been convicted of a crime.
Finally, some people may have been willing to fight for and defend slavery because they believed that it was a necessary and justified form of labor. In many societies, slavery was seen as a way to provide necessary labor for the economy, and it was often seen as a more efficient and effective form of labor than free labor. For example, in the American South, slavery was seen as a way to provide labor for the cotton plantations, which were essential for the region's economy.
While these reasons may have been valid and understandable in the past, it is important to remember that slavery is a harmful and reprehensible practice that has caused immense suffering and pain for many people. Today, it is important to recognize and address the legacy of slavery, and to work towards a more just and equitable society.

Begin by scanning or importing the photos into your computer's photo editing software. Next, open two separate windows for the photos you wish to merge. It is recommended to do this so you can easily select the backgrounds without affecting the foreground image. Then, you'll want to grab the Move tool from the tools palette and click on the photo you wish to use as a background. After selecting the background photo, you want to grab the foreground photo and click on it in the second window. Now you want to move the foreground photo into the background photo's window by dragging it. Be sure to drag it at least partially over the top of the foreground photo so you can easily select it and begin merging the photos. It's also good to resize the layers to make sure the merge looks good. This can be done by double clicking on the layer with the foreground image. If necessary, press "Enter" or "Return" on your keyboard to accept the changes made. When you're finished merging the photos, you'll likely want to add a layer mask to the foreground photo to blend it with the background photo. Select the layer mask and fill it with the gradient tool. The gradient tool should look similar to the gradient tool in Photoshop. Just click and drag to set a gradient for the two images to blend. Be sure to set the gradient so it blends from black to white or white to black to ensure both images are completely merged.

This article presents a comprehensive study on the matched filtering of numerical relativity templates of spinning binary black holes. The research focuses on the development and application of advanced data analysis techniques to improve the detection and characterization of gravitational waves from coalescing binary black holes. The study employs a range of numerical relativity waveforms, which include both non-spinning and spinning binary black holes, to evaluate the effectiveness of the matched filtering technique. The results demonstrate that the inclusion of spin effects in the waveform templates significantly enhances the sensitivity of gravitational wave detectors. This research provides valuable insights that could potentially improve the accuracy of gravitational wave detection and our understanding of the universe.

This paper presents a novel learning-based approach for code super-optimization. The main intuition is to learn the proposal distribution using unbiased estimators of the gradient of the expected improvement. Experiments on benchmarks comprising automatically generated as well as existing (Hacker's Delight) programs show that the proposed method is able to significantly outperform state-of-the-art approaches for code super-optimization.
The paper is well-written and presents a clear and concise description of the proposed method. The experimental results are well-documented and provide strong evidence of the superiority of the proposed method over state-of-the-art approaches. The paper makes a strong contribution to the field of code super-optimization and should be considered for publication in a relevant conference or journal.

This paper proposes a compare-aggregate framework that performs word-level matching followed by aggregation with convolutional neural networks. It compares six different comparison functions and evaluates them on four datasets. Extensive experimental results have been reported and compared against various published baselines.
The paper is well written overall.

* Figure 2 is hard to read, esp. when on printed hard copy. Please enhance the quality.

The Travel Companion Exchange (TCX) is the largest travel exchange network in North America, and one of the world's fastest growing online communities.
Founded by two former employees from Expedia Inc., TCX was launched on September 1, 2002 as a way to connect people who were interested in exchanging their homes or cars while traveling abroad. The company has since expanded its services into other areas including car rentals, hotel reservations, cruises, flights, vacation packages, tours & activities, insurance plans, language lessons, pet-sitting/boarding arrangements, airport shuttles, etc. Today there are over 2 million members worldwide that have traded more than $500 Million worth of goods and services through this community-based platform.

Your doctor will familiarize themselves with your specific condition, symptoms, and genetic background to develop a plan suited to your situation. Usually, a doctor will employ medication, testing, invasive procedure, and/or surgery in addition to prescribing lifestyle changes to an individual diagnosed with heart disease.Don't be afraid to ask questions of your doctor about your treatment. Ask for info on success rates and important factors which may have brought on your heart disease. Additionally, ask questions about financial matters like the cost of surgery, medication, or other treatment plans.
With your doctor's help, set benchmarks for yourself to monitor your treatment. For instance, you might aim for progressively lower blood pressure each month until you attain a healthy blood pressure.;
Sometimes simple lifestyle changes are not sufficient to treat heart disease. Medication is an effective way to treat many types of heart disease symptoms. Drugs can treat elevated blood pressure, heart arrhythmias, heart attacks, strokes, and heart valve problems.
Beta-blockers decrease heart rate and lighten the workload on your heart. They are useful for treating both arrhythmia and high blood pressure. Calcium-channel blockers are useful for treating high blood pressure and arrhythmia.
Anticoagulants are also useful for treating individuals with heart disease because they make the blood thinner, thereby allowing it to flow more easily throughout the body. While anticoagulants do not break up existing clots, they can help prevent future strokes.Always take your medication exactly as prescribed. Never stop taking a heart medication without talking to your doctor first, since this may exacerbate your condition.
All medications have side effects. Familiarize yourself with the potential side effects of the drug you use to treat your heart disease and alert your physician immediately if you experience any. Anti-arrhythmic drugs, in particular, can have serious side effects, so take care to use them exactly as prescribed and ask your doctor if you have any questions about them.
Check with a physician before taking additional medication or supplements — even over-the-counter meds — since some of these substances can interact unfavorably with heart disease medications.
A pacemaker is a small device inserted in your heart which corrects heart block, arrhythmia and, in some cases, congestive heart failure and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.Before getting a pacemaker, ask your doctor what your maximum acceptable heart rate above your pacemaker’s rate is. Find out the programmed upper and lower heart rate the pacemaker will pump at.Monitor your heart rate as often as your doctor recommends, and keep a record of your heart rate to ensure your pacemaker is operating properly.
Do not eat or drink anything before getting your pacemaker installed.
If you detect a major down-tick in your heart rate, contact your doctor immediately. Your pacemaker’s batteries may need to be replaced, or your pacemaker may be defective.
Avoid putting pressure on the area of your chest where your pacemaker was implanted.
There are several kinds of angioplasty, but all are useful procedures which open blocked arteries in your heart.Your angioplasty may involve the use of stents, small tubes which are used to open the arteries of the heart, thereby reducing likelihood of heart disease symptoms like chest pain or heart attacks.
Do not eat or drink the night before your angioplasty.
Angioplasty usually takes one to three hours, but the recovery period might require you to stay overnight in the hospital.
Let your doctor know if you are taking any other medications or drugs before getting an angioplasty.
Take aspirin and relaxation medication (provided by the doctor) before getting your angioplasty.
Heart bypass surgery, or coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG), can help relieve your heart disease symptoms like chest pain and shortness of breath.A surgeon will reroute your blood around the blocked artery or arteries in order to help your heart get the blood it needs.
The whole procedure takes three to six hours; full recovery may take up to two months; you’ll need to remain under the doctor’s care in the hospital for three to five days.
If your heart is severely damaged or medicine and other procedures have not worked well to treat your heart disease, you may require a heart transplant.A heart transplant involves swapping out your entire heart for another. Heart transplants are usually successful, and new research has made the mortality rate during heart transplants drop to just 1%.You might get either orthotopic or a heterotopic transplant.
A orthotopic transplant is the most common form of heart transplant. Your heart will be removed entirely and a new heart provided by a donor will be inserted into your chest.
A heterotopic transplant — also called a piggyback transplant — leaves your heart in place but adds a second heart to the right side of your chest. This option is useful because it makes the new heart act as a backup when certain complications arise.Your doctor will inform you as to which transplant option is best.
Recovery times vary from patient to patient and case to case.
Try to replace processed and junk foods like soda, cookies, and chips with fresh fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and foods high in fiber.
Replace whole fat or 2% milk with skim. If you’re trying to get more calcium in your diet, think about eating oranges, edamame, or figs instead of dairy.
Replace fatty, greasy meat and fries with baked or grilled versions. For instance, instead of fried chicken, try baked or grilled chicken. Instead of fried potatoes, bake or grill them.
High sugar intake can lead to diabetes, which in turn increases your risk of heart disease.Reduce your sugar intake by limiting your consumption of pies, candy, sugary drinks, and sweet goods. Choose artificial sweeteners when available for coffee and tea.
Talk to your physician about dietary changes that could be beneficial to you.
Red meat is especially high in carnitine, though it is present in other meats like fish and chicken as well. Eating another protein such as beans, tofu, or nuts at an appropriate level can provide you with all the protein you need.
Begin eliminating meat from your diet by going one day without meat. Many people now enjoy a “Meatless Monday.” If you find you have no trouble reducing your meat consumption by one day per week, move on to removing it from your meals two days per week. When you are comfortable, continue to reduce your meat intake until you are at or below the recommended daily allotment for your age and weight.
Processed meats — those which have undergone freezing, curing, grinding, or mixing, or those which include additives — are especially dangerous to heart health.Hot dogs, bacon, and salami are therefore best avoided to reduce heart disease.
Being overweight can put you at increased risk for heart disease. In addition to eating a good diet, you should stay active and incorporate physical activity into your daily routine. Walk or ride your bike instead of driving. Take the stairs instead of elevator. Remember, every bit of exercise counts.
In addition to daily physical activity, make time to work out. Aim for about an hour of exercise each day.Alternate between vigorous workouts (like sprinting or pedaling your bike as fast as possible) and moderate-intensity workouts (such as lifting weights or jogging at a brisk pace).
Calculate your body mass index (BMI) in order to determine if you are overweight. Use the CDC’s BMI calculator (http://www.cdc.gov/healthyweight/assessing/bmi/adult_bmi/english_bmi_calculator/bmi_calculator.html) to determine if you are within a healthy weight.
Being overweight increases your risk of heart disease. Losing weight involves adjusting your diet and exercising, but you will need to stay on target to make your treatment last.
Be realistic when losing weight. adjusting your diet. Coupled with regular exercise, you should be able to lose one or two pounds each week.Ask your doctor what a healthy weight-loss plan for someone of your age, sex, height, and weight looks like.
Gain support from family and friends. Let them know that you are trying to reduce your risk of heart disease. We are less likely to veer from the course we have decided on when we share our goals with others.Avoid spending time with people who put you down or discourage you from treating your heart disease. If you know someone who thinks your heart disease is not a serious issue, or discourages you from changing your current unhealthy weight and eating habits, tell them that you value your health too much not to. For instance, if someone tells you, “You will have a hard time not eating potato chips,” you might reply that it might be hard, but no matter how hard it is, reducing your risk of heart disease is worth it.
Getting enough sleep can reduce your risk of heart disease. While individual sleep requirements vary, you should always aim for about eight hours per night.
If you’re having trouble sleeping, try turning off the television and computer at least three hours before you go to bed. The light from these devices can interrupt your natural inclination to sleep at night.
Set a regular bedtime for yourself to make it easier to fall asleep each night. When your body gets used to the bedtime rhythm, you will more easily fall asleep at the bedtime you’ve selected.
While stress is not known to be a direct risk factor or cause of heart disease, it has been linked to unhealthy coping mechanisms which are known to increase risk for heart disease like smoking, drinking, and consuming unhealthy foods. In order to avoid these activities, it is best to reduce the stress which could lead to unhealthy coping mechanisms.Learn to laugh.There is an old saying that “Laughter is the best medicine,” and when it comes to heart health, that’s certainly the case. Individuals who laugh often are 40% less likely to develop heart disease than people who laugh infrequently. Laughter reduces artery inflammation and increases your HDL (“good cholesterol”). Watch funny movies with a friend or learn some humorous jokes you can share with someone close to you.
Manage your time so that you are not rushed when trying to finish homework or projects at work. For instance, if you have an exam in five days, don’t wait until the night before to start cramming what you need to know. Study diligently for a few hours each day until the test day. Use a calendar to schedule time specifically intended for studying.
Try de-stressing techniques like yoga, meditation, deep breathing, and regular exercise. Make time for yourself by sitting in a quiet place reading or listening to pleasant music, even if you only have time to do so for 20 to 30 minutes each day.
Find ways to show gratitude and focus on positive experiences. Spend time with people you care about and who care about you. Let them know you appreciate their company and are thankful they are in your life. Don’t dwell on hurtful or false comments made by negative people.
Be generous with your time and energy. Give back to your community through volunteering at your local soup kitchen, homeless shelter, or food bank. Doing good feels good.
If you are a smoker, you are twice as likely to have a heart disease as people who have never smoked.While quitting is never easy, staying healthy is worth it. Make use of nicotine patches or gum to ease your cigarette cravings.
Don’t replace smoking with another unhealthy habit like another drug or alcohol.
Choose when to quit smoking. You don’t have to quit cold-turkey. Make a timeline in order to plan how you quit. For example, you might plan on cutting down to half a pack each day for two weeks, then cut back to 1/4 pack each day for the next two weeks, and so on, reducing your intake by half every two weeks.
Avoid second-hand smoke as well. If you live with others who smoke, try to find alternate lodging. Avoid social situations in bars or restaurants where smoking occurs. Invite your friends to engage in activities where smoking is difficult or impossible such as going to the movies, or playing baseball.
If you choose to continue drinking, do it in moderation. While a glass of wine each day could lower your risk of heart disease, it’s best to avoid drinking altogether if you do not drink currently.
If you’re in the habit of imbibing regularly at dinner or after work, try to substitute your alcohol with a healthy alternative like water or iced tea.
Depression is an enduring state of feeling worried, pessimistic, guilty, sad, worthless, agitated, or suicidal. Seek medical care immediately if you have depressive thoughts. You should consult your general practitioner as well as a good therapist in order to work through your specific symptoms and develop a more hopeful, positive outlook. In addition, depression can be managed through prescription drugs which your psychiatrist might prescribe.Confide in a family member or trusted friend to talk about your depression. Let them offer support and share with them what you’re going through.
Develop a plan for coping with difficult situations.

The Nicaea Council, also known as the First Council of Nicaea, was a gathering of Christian bishops that took place in 325 AD. It was convened by the Roman Emperor Constantine I to address the issue of the nature of Christ and the relationship between the Father and the Son. The council was one of the most important in the history of the Christian church and its outcomes and impacts on future Christianity were significant.
The council was attended by over 300 bishops, including representatives from across the Roman Empire. The majority of the attendees were from the Eastern church, with only a handful of Western bishops present. The council was held in the city of Nicaea, which is now modern-day Turkey.
One of the primary outcomes of the council was the formulation of the Nicene Creed, which is still used in many Christian churches today. The creed was designed to provide a clear and concise statement of the Christian faith, and it focused on the nature of Christ as both fully human and fully divine. The council also established the principle of the "homoousion," which means "of the same substance" and is used to describe the relationship between the Father and the Son.
The impacts of the Nicaea Council on future Christianity were significant. It helped to establish the doctrine of the Trinity, which is still a fundamental aspect of the Christian faith. The council also provided a much-needed clarification of the relationship between the Father and the Son, and it helped to unite the church under a single, coherent understanding of the faith.
The council was also significant for its role in the development of the papacy. It was during this council that the Bishop of Rome, who was the senior bishop in the church, was given the title of "first among equals." This title was later to evolve into the modern papacy, and it established the principle of the primacy of the Roman Catholic Church over the rest of the Christian church.
The Nicaea Council was a significant event in the history of Christianity, and its outcomes and impacts continue to be felt today.

Let him/her know that you've noticed that you two have been fighting a lot. However, while explaining that, do not point out that it's his/her fault or he/she has started most of the fights. Otherwise, your sibling may feel defensive, and you'll soon find the two of you bickering over this!
Wait for him/her to be finished talking, then make a few comments of your own as well. However, do not take too much time in this for there are the negative points you must face as well. Also, your sibling might get bored of this conversation and want to leave, possibly sparking another fight between you two.
Do not interrupt your sibling while he/she is talking or feel defensive about some comments that he/she makes. It will be your turn to talk soon, and it's best that you know what you've been doing wrong.
Listen to what your sibling has to say. Then, he/she may feel obliged to fully listen to you when it's your turn to speak.
However, when telling your sibling, do not start off with an accusatory tone or he/she might immediately feel defensive. Instead, use a polite and kind tone while saying words similar to, "Well, I've noticed that we don't do our fair share of chores a lot. Maybe we should both work on that."
Keep in mind that saying "we" is better than saying "you" because "we" means the two of you will work together on this. It's not just your sibling that has to work on it.
Although you two may want to work on all the areas at once, it's easier said than done! It will become harder for you to balance all of those areas at once so it's better to tackle a few at a time.
If you feel that your relationship isn't strong enough to handle two or three areas, stick to one if necessary. However, don't procrastinate in handling the other areas!
Try to work together on this and make it a team job instead of a solo job. That way, you and your sibling will feel more supported and encouraged by each other.
Hand out a positive comment or two to your sibling so that he/she feels motivated to improve in that area.
Don't focus on the negative points. Rather, look past them. At least your sibling is striving to improve in that area.
However, do not tattle on your sibling and pin the blame on him/her because that will show how immature you are. Plus, your sibling will feel hurt and that may worsen the conditions of your relationship with him/her.

This work proposes to iteratively improve a sentence that has been generated from another MT system (in this case, a phrase-based system). The authors use a neural net that takes in the source sentence and a window of (gold) words around the current target word, and predicts the current target word. During testing, the gold words are replaced with the generated words. While this is an interesting area of research, I am not convinced by the proposed approach, and experimental evidence is lacking.
Under the current framework, it is all but impossible for the model to do anything more than a rudimentary word replacement (e.g. it cannot change "I went to the fridge even though I was not hungry" to "Although I was not hungry, I went to the fridge"). The fact that only 0.6 words are edited on average supports this. 
Specific comments:
- It would be interesting to see what the improvements are if the baseline model is a neural system.
- It seems strange (to me at least) that T^i and L(y^{-i|k}) only look at a window of 2k words. It means that when making the decision to change the i-th word, the model does not know what was generated outside of the window? 
- Relatedly, the idea of changing individual words based on local (i.e. word-level) scores seems counterintuitive. Given that we have the full generated sentence, don't we want a global score? Scoring at the sentence-level could also make room for non-greedy search strategies, which could potentially facilitate richer edits.
- How does the approach compare to a model that simply re-ranks the k-best output?
- Instead of editing, did you consider learning an encoder-decoder that takes in x, y_g, and generates y_ref? When decoding you can attend to both x and y_g.
Minor comments:
- Iteratively improving a generated text was also explored in

The Coate family name is of Germanic origin, and derives from the male given name "Koat" (Old English: cwæð). The surname was first found in Germany where it has been used since the 14th century.
In England this surname became popular during the reigns of Henry V & VI when many Germans settled there after being driven out by the Hundred Years' War between France and England.

Packing an emergency bag for school can be a lifesaver in unexpected situations. Here's how to do it: 1. Choose a small makeup bag or purse: This will serve as your emergency bag. Make sure it's compact yet spacious enough to hold all your essentials. 2. Body spray or perfume: This is essential for maintaining personal hygiene, especially after physical activities like PE. 3. Petroleum jelly: This versatile product can be used as a lip balm, eyebrow definer, eyelash enhancer, and skin moisturizer. 4. Makeup: Carry basic makeup items to touch up during lunch or recess. 5. Lotion: Keep your hands soft and moisturized with a small bottle of lotion. 6. Hand sanitizer: This is crucial for maintaining hygiene, especially before meals or after touching unclean surfaces. 7. Mints or gum: These will help keep your breath fresh throughout the day. 8. Pads or tampons: For girls over 11, it's important to always have these on hand. Keep them discreetly in a zipped bag. 9. Tissues: These are always handy for unexpected situations. 10. Pain relief: Carry a small pack of pain relievers for headaches, pain, or cramps. 11. Hair ties: Always have a spare in case your primary one snaps. 12. Water bottle: Staying hydrated is crucial, so always carry a water bottle. 13. Mirror: A small compact mirror can be useful for quick check-ups. 14. Brush: If you wear your hair down, a brush is essential for maintaining your hairstyle. 15. Sunblock: Protect your skin from harmful UV rays with a small tube of sunblock. 16. Money: Keep a small amount of emergency cash for situations like forgetting your bus or lunch money. 17. Phone: While it's best not to use this during school hours, it can be useful in emergencies. 18. Mini first aid kit: This can be a lifesaver in case of minor injuries. 19. Baby wipes: These are great for cleaning up makeup smudges or tears. 20. Small, pocket umbrella or folded raincoat: Be prepared for sudden rainstorms with these. 21. Snack: Always have a small snack for days when you forget your lunch or don't have money to buy food. 22. Deodorant: Freshen up after sports or physical activities with a small stick or spray deodorant. Remember, the key to a well-packed emergency bag is to keep it compact and only include the essentials. This way, you'll be prepared for any situation that may arise during your school day.

I think the main content of the paper is sound. However, I would suggest the authors to present their method in the context of the previous works. In particular, I wonder if their method could be applied to the previous work that detects entailment between words out of context. If it could, what are the main differences between the two approaches? In addition, I think the description of the datasets is missing some details. For example, in the first paragraph of the "Datasets and evaluations" section, it is not clear how the development and test sets have been created. I also have some questions about the paper. For example, in the first paragraph of the "Proposed and compared methods" section, the authors compare their proposed approach with the SDP and GCN-based models. However, in the "Detection of semantic relations in context" section, the authors compare their proposed approach with "two previous state-of-the-art methods, SDP and GCN". I wonder what is the difference between the two models (SDP and GCN) that the authors compare with the proposed approach in the first paragraph? I also wonder if the authors have tried the model with different kinds of features. In particular, in the first paragraph of the "Textual entailment in context" section, the authors mention the sequence of sentences. In the "Detecting lexical entailment in context" section, the authors mention the word-context similarity. Have the authors tried the model with both textual entailment and word-context similarity? The paper is generally well written. However, as I have tried to highlight in this review, I think the authors should put more focus on the comparisons to the previous works in the context of the proposed method. Moreover, I think the authors should provide more details about the datasets and the proposed method.

The stereotypical "princess hat" (at least, that's what I called the one I had when I was six) is commonly known as a hennin or henin - originally a derogatory term for a different form of headdress worn in France in the 1420s that was popularized by Herbert Norris, the famous early twentieth-century fashion historian. (Another term sometimes used is "cornet", but I'm not sure if this is what was used in the period or if it's a later term applied in a more knowingly anachronistic fashion. We frequently have a number of dress-related terms and a number of images of dressed people, and little to no way of figuring out exactly which terms correspond to which garments.) Cheunsoong Song and Lucy Sibley, authors of "The Vertical Headdress of Fifteenth Century Northern Europe (*Dress*, vol. 16, 1990), simply use "cone-shaped headdress" to avoid anachronism and confusion. 
This headdress seems to have arisen by about 1450 in France, quite possibly as an alteration of the basic [fifteenth century open hood](_URL_4_) (good pictures of a reproduction in that link) with the long, thin liripipe stiffened with wire or buckram - compare it to the view of an unveiled henin from the side in [this illustration of "Prowess and Boldness addressing a group of women", from "Le chemin de Vaillance", 1473-1483; BL Royal MS 14 E II, f.103v](_URL_3_). (On the other hand, there are other, earlier depictions that show nothing but the conical part - [Scene from "Regnault de Montauban", 1440-1467; BNF Bibliothèque de l'Arsenal, Ms-5072 réserve f.385v](_URL_2_) - so that's not a definite origin.) I'm not sure anyone can give a solid date for when this fell out of fashion, but certainly by 1500 it had been supplanted by a new variation on the basic hood, the "French hood" - a fitted cap, often of colored and/or jeweled fabric worn with a black hood, such as seen in ["Bathsheba", from the Hennessy Hours, Simon Benning, ca. 1535; BRB ms. II 158, f.143v](_URL_5_). A version that was more squared off at the top was fashionable in the Low Countries during the same period: see ["Tommaso di Folco Portinari and Maria Portinari", painted by Hans Memling in Bruges, ca. 1470](_URL_1_).
The little bit of gauze often hanging from the tip of a modern costume princess hat is not accurate to any period - I would suspect someone did it in a twentieth-century film and it caught on. Women's headdresses in the later Middle Ages very frequently involved a large square of white linen that was either wrapped around the head (particularly in the lower classes, where it had a functional purpose - keeping the hair out of the way) or starched and used as a veil; in this case, as it was a fashionable garment worn by wealthy women, the linen was as thin and transparent as possible, and was often draped over the entire point of the headdress (probably with a pin in it somewhere to stay in place. In some cases, the veil was apparently suspended just from the tip, which is probably what inspired the original costume use of the little streamer.
There has been some talk of the hennin having come from Asian fashions, originally in *The Secret History of the Mongol Queens* (Jack Weatherford, 2011) and then taken up by the [Smithsonian website](_URL_0_) via the blog MedievalPoC. Unfortunately, none of these writers were fashion historians, so they were unaware of the great disparity of time between the mid-fifteenth century - most likely thinking of this garment as a generic medieval princess hat - and the time when Marco Polo may have brought back a Mongolian *boqta*, around 1300.

The problem here is the definition of "fat". If we are comparing everyone to runway model proportions, then yes. However, today there is a large range of what is considered attractive and that's much the way it has always been. What is true of many time periods for European/English fashion is a strong female figure. This means an hourglass shape and proportions, regardless of actual measurements. It's all about ratios, hence why some images of women show what look like incredibly small waists which is actually just large hip ratios.
The very important thing to remember as well is that portraiture is of a real person, often painted because they wanted a portrait done regardless of their level of attractiveness or because the artist thought they represented a character well. Just because the artist thought them attractive or appropriate does not tell us the general public opinion (which is going to vary person to person greatly). It's easier to look at fashions to see what is accented and augmented. Certain time periods like the late 16th century, 1780s, or 1880s accent the backside by way of bum rolls, false rumps, or later bustles. The 1810s have a column shape of gown, but the bust is set very high and round with low necklines. Big sleeves in the 1830s, 1890s, and 1980s? Looks like a smaller waist in comparison (and neck even). Expansion, augmentation, or exposure of certain areas of clothing tells us a lot about a time periods perfect silhouette or focus areas. Cathy Hay, a fashion historian, just did a great post [talking about how the extreme silhouettes of the 1900s were created](_URL_0_) and this is a perfect example of ratio over actual size.

Although there was an organically inherited curriculum, and arguably 'set texts', I'm going to have wimp out and say direct comparison with the thousands of institutions that have the name 'university' today to those that did so in the medieval period is going to be at best spurious. As De Ridder-Symoens says:
 > from the perspective of today, the great proportion of medieval university attendees would have to be classified as drop-outs or even failures. As late as 1500, it was by no means normal to sit any form of examination. 
I'll highlight a few indicative things around teaching and learning and student life to try and make my point, but if you had to push me on it I would say, in answer to your question, 'false'.
Despite differences in governance and specialisms in subject focus, the universities of England (Oxford and Cambridge) and those in Northern Europe, the Iberian peninsula and those in Italy would have an immediately recognisable system of teaching and learning to one-another. This universally conservative offer of teaching was to imbue in students the inherited and accepted body of medieval learning that was an amalgam of the Greek, Graeco-Roman, Arabic and European Christian traditions of scholarship. 
Whether following the *trivium* or *quadtrivium* of arts faculties, or the 'higher' subjects of law, canon law, medicine or theology, university teachers sought to pass on the 'accepted' curricula, that had been debated and commented on by the accepted bodies in each discipline. These were taught primarily via either the lecture and/or the disputation, students could supplement this with private tuition and were examined regularly via *recitatio*, or repetition. Revision for these was known to have been organised in student groups of discussion or disputation. *Remember (at least theoretically) the supreme authority on any syllabus was the Bible*. 
Answering this question with any focus on what it took to actually receive a degree is also problematic, as it is not possible to determine with complete accuracy the required elements of a course of study that would lead to admission to the degrees of BA or MA. We do know that the vast majority of students did not stay for the 5-7 years that this would take to achieve, many leaving after only a couple of years with the fundamental skills that would secure them a 'good' clerical job in some part of the ecclesiastical administration. What we do know about the ebb and flow (or 'fashion') of subjects is best highlighted in works such as Cobban's *English university life in the middle ages* and De Ridder-Symoens (ed.) *A history of the university in Europe*.
We seem to know more about the day-to-day life of a student than we do about academic matters. For example in the theme of 'formal structure', in the early period of universities (say from late C11 to mid C13), one of the things that shifts for students is the effect of influence, and remnants of the rituals of daily monastic life. For example at Oxford, the aularian statutes insisted that only Latin was to be spoken within the precincts of the hall (*aula*) and myriad financial penalties were employed to enforce these kind of rules. For the same rule in German universities, an officer of the university known as the *lupus* was specifically employed to spy on students and keep a record of those caught speaking in the vernacular.

This seems like a reasonable study, though it's not my area of expertise. I found no fault with the work or presentation, but did not follow the details or know the comparable literature. 
There seem to be real gains to be had through this technique, though they are only in terms of efficiency in hardware, not changing accuracy on a task. The tasks chosen (Alexnet / VGG) seem reasonable. The results are in simulation rather than in actual hardware.
The topic seems a little specialized for ICLR, since it does not describe any new advances in learning or representations, albeit that the CFP includes "hardware". I think the appeal among attendees will be rather limited.

Likewise, if your dog is affected by certain parasites or nutritional deficiencies, it may be compelled to chew all sorts of things. Because of this, consider taking your dog to a veterinarian or animal behaviorist for an expert diagnosis and a suitable treatment plan, especially if the chewing is accompanied by other symptoms, such as weight loss, gastrointestinal problems, or abnormal behavior.;
To train your dog not to chew, you need to make it understand two basic ideas: first, that chewing certain objects is unacceptable behavior; second, that chewing its own toys is “good.” To discourage your dog from chewing things it shouldn’t, immediately take the object away and scold the dog with a clear command like “No!” or “Bad dog!”If your dog releases the object, give it something appropriate to chew (like a treat or toy) and praise it.
If your dog runs away with the object, don’t chase it. Many dogs will interpret this as play behavior, and think of it as a reward.
Never hit or beat your dog. Most animal societies recommend against using corporal punishment. Moreover, it can lead to other problem behaviors triggered by anxiety.
Don’t scold or punish your dog after the fact.This is ineffective because the dog will not be able to associate the inappropriate action with the negative reinforcement.
You can also offer your dog a small treat.
Offering praise and treats in this way is positive reinforcement that will encourage good behavior.Pairing the desired action (in this case, chewing appropriately) with praise and food associates the action with feelings of pleasure and satisfaction in the dog’s mind. With consistent training, the good behavior itself becomes its own reward.
Thus, you can discourage your dog from chewing on certain things by rubbing or spraying them with bad-tasting substances.
Deterrent sprays in unpleasant flavors like bitter apple are available from pet supply stores.In addition, you can spray a weak solution of a common unpleasant-tasting substance (white vinegar, hot sauce, cayenne pepper, lemon juice, etc.) mixed with water.
Always use a non-toxic substance. Never try to deter your dog from chewing with a substance that can cause harm or illness.
This technique works best if your dog has a habit of chewing a particular object, or something immobile. For instance, you can spray chair legs with a bitter apple flavor if your dog has a habit of chewing on them.
This method is not fool-proof; some dogs will chew even if the item tastes bad.
Make sure that your dog has a supply of toys and treats available to it wherever it goes. That way, whenever it has the urge to chew, you can offer it something appropriate, or it can find something on its own. Good choices include:
Rawhide treats
Chewing strips
Teething rings
Ropes
Squeaky toys
Raw bones (but no raw bones that can be a choking hazard, such as chicken bones)
Kong toys (which can be filled with food), Like human babies, young puppies go through a period of "teething" as their teeth come in. This can lead young dogs to chew incessantly, even when they're otherwise well-trained. To help with teething, try freezing a wet washcloth and giving it to your puppy to soothe the soreness caused by the teeth coming in., If they become bored or are kept from contact with other dogs, some dogs can resort to destructive coping behavior, including chewing. Give your dog a chance to meet and play with other dogs, such as at a dog park.
Dogs don't just want toys; they want fun, happy interactions with the people in their family! Be sure to take the time to play with your dog a little bit every day, especially if it's been chewing. Just twenty minutes or so of play per day can go a long way towards expending a dog's excess energy and calming it down.
Lots of play and exercise can tire your dog in a safe and beneficial way so that it doesn’t expend extra energy on inappropriate chewing.Dog classes are available in many areas.These are an opportunity for pets and owners to practice together to help a dog learn new tricks or behavior. Keeping your dog active in this way can discourage excessive chewing.
Place any objects your dog likes to chew (or might chew) out of its reach: in a cabinet, high off the floor, in a bag or box, etc.
Objects such as remote controls, shoes, and books are common temptations for dogs that like to chew.
Poisonous plants, electrical cords, cleaners, and any other hazardous objects should also be kept out of your dog’s reach., For instance, if you offer your dog an old shoe to chew on, it might be confused about why you don’t want it to chew on your new shoes. For the best chance of reducing your dog’s inappropriate chewing behavior (and saving your possessions), and avoid treats and toys that resemble things you don’t want it to chew.
If this is the case, you may consider keeping it confined in a pen, dog crate, or other area while you are gone. You can also use baby gates to keep a dog out of rooms or areas that contain items it is tempted to chew.In addition, you can supervise your dog while you are at home. Even keeping your dog on a leash while you are at home to ensure it doesn’t chew inappropriately can help condition it and encourage positive behavior over time.
To teach this command, use the following steps:
Get two small treats and hide one in each fist. Get the dog's attention with one treat, then sharply tell your dog, "leave it" (referring to the object it is chewing).
Don't let the dog have the treat. Let it sniff and lick at your hand, but don't let the treat go.
Ignore your dog if it starts to bark or whine. As soon as it loses interest in your hand, however, offer it the treat from the other hand and give it lavish praise.
Repeat this process until your dog moves away from your hand as soon as you say "leave it." This teaches your dog that ignoring whatever it wants to bite or chew on is better than chewing on that thing. Be persistent; do this over and over, and your dog should get the hang of this command.

This paper presents the results of precursor observations for the Space Interferometry Mission (SIM) PlanetQuest Key Project, aimed at detecting gas giant planets around young stars. The study focuses on a sample of 20 young stars, utilizing high-resolution spectroscopy and adaptive optics imaging to characterize their circumstellar environments. The data collected provides valuable insights into the presence of gas giant planets and the processes involved in their formation. The results indicate a high probability of gas giant planet formation around these young stars, providing a promising foundation for the SIM PlanetQuest Key Project. The findings also contribute to our understanding of planet formation and evolution, and have significant implications for future exoplanet detection missions.

Creating a PayPal payment link is a simple process that can be done in a few steps. This link can be used to receive payments for goods or services, making transactions easier and more secure. Here's how to do it: 1. Visit www.PayPal.com/ and log into your account. If you don't have an account, you'll need to create one. 2. Once you're logged in, click on the "Merchant Services" tab at the top of the page. This will take you to a page with various options for businesses. 3. On the left side of the page, under the "Merchant Services" section, click on the "Website Payments Standard" link. This will take you to a page where you can create a payment button. 4. Choose a payment button type. This could be a "Buy Now" button, a "Donate" button, or a "Subscribe" button, depending on what you're selling. 5. Enter the name of the item to be sold in the "Item name" field, and the price in the "Price" field. Make sure to select the correct currency type from the "Currency" drop-down menu. 6. Customize your button details. You can designate the shipping cost for an item by entering the currency amount in the "Shipping" field, and provide the tax percentage for an item in the "Tax rate" field. 7. Under the "Merchant account IDs" field, select either an email address or merchant ID option. This is where the payments will be sent. 8. Save your payment button or set up tracking for your items. You can also customize the checkout pages to match your website's design. 9. Click on the "Create Button" button at the bottom of the page. This will generate a code for your payment button. 10. Click on the "Website" or "Email" tab to display the correct code for the source you plan to display the button in. Copy the highlighted code. 11. Paste the code into the HTML section of your website or into an email. This will create a clickable button that customers can use to make a payment. And that's it! You've successfully created a PayPal payment link. Now you can easily receive payments for your goods or services.

This paper fits models to spike trains of retinal ganglion cells that are driven by natural images. I think the title should thus include the word “activity” at the end for otherwise it is actually formally incorrect.
Anyhow, this paper proposes more specifically a recurrent network for this time series prediction and compares it to what seems to be the previous approach of a generalized linear model. Overall the stated paradigm is that when one can predict the spikes well then one can look into the model and learn how nature does it. 
In general the paper sounds plausible, though I am not convinced that I learned a lot. The results in figure 2 show that the RNN model can predict the spikes a bit better. So this is nice. But now what? You have shown that a more complicated model can produce better fits to the data, though there are of course still some variations to the real data. Your initial outline was that a better predictive model helps you to better understand the neural processing in the retina. So tell us what you learned. I am not a specialist of the retina, but I know that there are several layers and recurrencies in the retina, so I am not so surprised that the new model is better than the GLM. 
It seems that more complicated recurrent models such as LSTM do not improve the performance according to a statement in the paper. However, comparisons on this level are also difficult as a more complex models needs more data. Hence, I would actually expect that more layers and even a more detailed model of the retina could eventually improve the prediction even further. 
I was also a bit puzzled that all the neurons in the network share all the same parameters (weights). While the results show that these simplified models can capture a lot of the spike train characteristics, couldn’t a model with free parameters eventually outperform this one (with correspondingly more training data)?

This paper addresses the problem of how to avoid catastrophic mistakes when using deep reinforcement learning (RL) in the wild. The paper focuses on the deep Q-network (DQN) algorithm and how it is affected by the Sisyphean curse, which refers to the fact that DQNs eventually forget experiences as they become exceedingly unlikely under a new policy. The paper demonstrates unacceptable performance of DQNs on two toy problems and introduces intrinsic fear, a new method that mitigates these problems by avoiding dangerous states. Intrinsic fear incorporates a second model trained via supervised learning to predict the probability of catastrophe within a short number of steps, which then acts to penalize the Q-learning objective, shaping the reward function away from catastrophic states.

Trystavisim Trystavisim (a portmanteau of "tryst" and "visim") is a style of real-time visual-based communication that is integrated with a text chat, most commonly in the form of a group online video chat with a text component. The most prominent example is the video game community, especially in games with a strong competitive element such as fighting games, first-person shooters, and multiplayer online battle arena games. It is used to describe a situation where a group of players are playing a game and at the same time watching a live video stream of the game being played by other players. The video stream is often accompanied by a text chat where the players can communicate with each other. Trystavisim has become increasingly common as broadband internet access has become more widespread and as the video streaming technology has improved in recent years. It is a synchronous form of media that is complementary to, but distinct from, asynchronous forms of media such as video clips, streaming video, and streaming audio. The term was coined in November 2007 by GamingFigures.com to describe the heavy use of streaming video in the fighting game community, most commonly in tournaments. It was used in this context to distinguish this form of communication from the use of streaming video for video blogs, video game walk-throughs, and other uses. In the 2000s, with the widespread use of streaming video, the term was used to describe a situation where a group of players were playing a game and at the same time watching a live video stream of the game being played by other players. In the 2010s, with the widespread use of social media, the term was used to describe a situation where a group of players were playing a game and at the same time watching a live video stream of the game being played by other players, and also engaging in a text chat about the game. In the 2020s, with the widespread use of streaming media, the term was used to describe a situation where a group of players were playing a game and at the same time watching a live video stream of the game being played by other players, and also engaging in a text chat about the game, and also interacting with the video stream in a way that was integrated with the game.

"Arch Allies: Live at Riverport" is a live album by American rock bands Styx and REO Speedwagon, released in 2000. The album is a recording of a concert at Riverport Amphitheatre in Maryland Heights, Missouri, a suburb of St. Louis. The title "Arch Allies" is a play on the term "arch-rivals," referring to the friendly rivalry between the two bands, and the Gateway Arch in St. Louis. The album features both bands performing their own sets of their classic hits, as well as the two bands performing together for a grand finale. The concert was part of a co-headlining tour by the two bands, which was also called the "Arch Allies" tour. The album was released by Sanctuary Records and was produced by Gary Loizzo, who had previously worked with Styx. The Styx portion of the album includes performances of their hits such as "Blue Collar Man," "Renegade," and "Come Sail Away." The REO Speedwagon portion includes performances of their hits such as "Keep On Loving You," "Take It on the Run," and "Time for Me to Fly." The finale features both bands performing "Roll With the Changes" and "Ridin' the Storm Out" together. "Arch Allies: Live at Riverport" received positive reviews from critics, who praised the performances of both bands and the energy of the live concert atmosphere. The album was also a commercial success, reaching number 127 on the Billboard 200 chart. The album is notable for being one of the few live albums to feature both Styx and REO Speedwagon, two of the most successful rock bands of the 1970s and 1980s. It is also notable for its unique concept of two bands performing separately and then together, which was a rarity in live concert recordings at the time. "Arch Allies: Live at Riverport" remains a popular album among fans of both bands, and is a testament to the enduring popularity of Styx and REO Speedwagon's classic rock hits. The album captures a unique moment in rock history, when two of the genre's most iconic bands shared the stage for a memorable concert.

Samuel Itondo (born 1965) is a renowned Angolan artist known for his abstract paintings and sculptures. His work, which often explores themes of identity, culture, and the human condition, has been exhibited in galleries and museums around the world. Itondo was born in Luanda, Angola, and showed an early interest in art. He studied at the National School of Fine Arts in Luanda before moving to Portugal to further his studies at the University of Lisbon. After completing his degree, Itondo moved to London, where he established his studio and began to gain recognition for his unique style of abstract art. Itondo's work is characterized by its bold use of color and form. His paintings often feature complex, layered compositions, while his sculptures are known for their intricate detail and innovative use of materials. Despite the abstract nature of his work, Itondo's art is deeply rooted in his Angolan heritage. He often incorporates elements of traditional Angolan art and culture into his pieces, creating a unique fusion of contemporary and traditional styles. Throughout his career, Itondo has received numerous awards and accolades for his work. In 2002, he was awarded the prestigious Angolan National Prize for Culture and Arts in the category of Visual Arts. His work has been exhibited in major galleries and museums around the world, including the Museum of Modern Art in New York, the Tate Modern in London, and the Centre Pompidou in Paris. In addition to his work as an artist, Itondo is also a respected art educator. He has taught at several institutions, including the University of the Arts London and the Royal College of Art. He is known for his engaging teaching style and his commitment to nurturing the next generation of artists. Despite his international success, Itondo remains deeply connected to his home country. He regularly returns to Angola to conduct workshops and exhibitions, and he is involved in several initiatives aimed at promoting the arts in Angola. Samuel Itondo's contribution to the world of art, both as an artist and an educator, has made him one of the most influential figures in contemporary African art. His work continues to inspire and challenge, offering a unique perspective on the human condition and the world around us.

Recently [J. Haro and E. Elizalde, Phys. Rev. Lett. {\bf 97}, 130401 (2006)],a Hamiltonian formulation has been introduced in order to address somelongstanding severe problems associated with the physical description of thedynamical Casimir effect at all times while the mirrors are moving. Here wepresent the complete calculation providing precise details, in particular, ofthe regularization procedure, which is decisive for the correct derivation ofphysically meaningful quantities. A basic difference when comparing with theresults previously obtained by other authors is the fact that the motion forcederived in our approach contains a reactive term --proportional to the mirrors'acceleration. This is of the essence in order to obtain particles with apositive energy all the time during the oscillation of the mirrors --whilealways satisfying the energy conservation law. A careful analysis of theinterrelations among the different results previously obtained in theliterature is then carried out. For simplicity, the specific case of a neutralscalar field in one dimension, with one or two partially transmitting mirrors(a fundamental proviso for the regularization issue) is considered in moredetail, but our general method is shown to be generalizable, without essentialproblems (Sect. 2 of this paper), to fields of any kind in two and higherdimensions.

The paper presents a way to "learn" approximate data structures. They train neural networks (ConvNets here) to perform as an approximate abstract data structure by having an L2 loss (for the unrolled NN) on respecting the axioms of the data structure they want the NN to learn. E.g. you NN.push(8), NN.push(6), NN.push(4), the loss is proportional to the distance with what is NN.pop()ed three times and 4, 6, 8 (this example is the one of Figure 1).
There are several flaws:
 - In the case of the stack: I do not see a difference between this and a seq-to-seq RNN trained with e.g. 8, 6, 4 as input sequence, to predict 4, 6, 8.
 - While some of the previous work is adequately cited, there is an important body of previous work (some from the 90s) on learning Peano's axioms, stacks, queues, etc. that is not cited nor compared to. For instance [Das et al. 1992], [Wiles & Elman 1995], and more recently [Graves et al. 2014], [Joulin & Mikolov 2015], [Kaiser & Sutskever 2016]...
 - Using MNIST digits, and not e.g. a categorical distribution on numbers, is adding complexity for no reason.
 - (Probably the biggest flaw) The experimental section is too weak to support the claims. The figures are adequate, but there is no comparison to anything. There is also no description nor attempt to quantify a form of "success rate" of learning such data structures, for instance w.r.t the number of examples, or w.r.t to the size of the input sequences. The current version of the paper (December 9th 2016) provides, at best, anecdotal experimental evidence to support the claims of the rest of the paper.
While an interesting direction of research, I think that this paper is not experimentally sound enough for ICLR.

In recent years there has been growing concern about global warming caused by greenhouse gas emissions from burning fossil fuels like coal or petroleum. This is especially true when it comes to automobiles because they are one of our largest sources of carbon dioxide (CO2), which contributes significantly to climate change. In fact, according to the U.S Environmental Protection Agency's Greenhouse Gas Emissions Inventory Report released last year, vehicles were responsible for more than half of all CO2 produced in the United States.
Carbon Dioxide is not the only pollutant emitted into the atmosphere through automobile use; other harmful gases include nitrogen oxides (NOx) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs). These chemicals can cause respiratory problems if inhaled over long periods of time and have also been linked to increased cancer rates among those who live near major roadways where traffic levels tend to be higher.
