Warm Dark Matter (WDM) has been invoked to resolve apparent conflicts of ColdDark Matter (CDM) models with observations on subgalactic scales. In this workwe provide a new and independent lower limit for the WDM particle mass (e.g.sterile neutrino) through the analysis of image fluxes in gravitationallylensed QSOs. Starting from a theoretical unperturbed cusp configuration we analyze theeffects of intergalactic haloes in modifying the fluxes of QSO multiple images,giving rise to the so-called anomalous flux ratio. We found that the globaleffect of such haloes strongly depends on their mass/abundance ratio and it ismaximized for haloes in the mass range $10^6-10^8 \Msun$. This result opens up a new possibility to constrain CDM predictions on smallscales and test different warm candidates, since free streaming of warm darkmatter particles can considerably dampen the matter power spectrum in this massrange. As a consequence, while a ($\Lambda$)CDM model is able to produce fluxanomalies at a level similar to those observed, a WDM model, with aninsufficiently massive particle, fails to reproduce the observationalevidences. Our analysis suggests a lower limit of a few keV ($m_{\nu} \sim 10$) for themass of warm dark matter candidates in the form of a sterile neutrino. Thisresult makes sterile neutrino Warm Dark Matter less attractive as analternative to Cold Dark Matter, in good agreement with previous findings fromLyman-$\alpha$ forest and Cosmic Microwave Background analysis.

The competition of hydrogen-like and isotropic interactions on polymer collapse is studied with a simple off-lattice model. A polymer chain in a good solvent is described by a self-avoiding walk on a lattice, with nearest neighbor interactions of both hydrogen-like (repulsive) and isotropic (neutral) kinds. It is found that there is a critical temperature below which the polymer collapses into a compact structure. This collapse is initiated by the isotropic interactions, which induce more compact structures than the hydrogen-like interactions. However, once the compact structures are formed, the hydrogen-like interactions further stabilize them against decay into separated coils. The consequences of these findings are discussed in the context of the stability of DNA in a cell. History and Motivation Since the advent of polymer physics (1949) it was realized that the entropy of a polymer chain in solution decreases with decreasing its radius of gyration, and that this decrease becomes maximal at a certain minimal value of the radius of gyration. This phenomenon was named "enthalpic stabilization" of polymer chains and it led the way to a new branch of polymer physics: thermodynamical biology. But why should a polymer chain have a finite radius of gyration at all? The entropy of a long chain (its extension being much larger than its radius of gyration) decreases with the square of its radius of gyration, because its effective linear dimension decreases like the cube of its radius of gyration. That is why the radius of gyration is a small number: the chain has to be "globular" to decrease its entropy. Therefore, a globular polymer (with a minimal value of its radius of gyration) is also called "compact" polymer. Biology Thermodynamical biology was the first attempt to use physics concepts to describe biological mechanisms. Because the early 1970s, this approach lead to a first table of correspondence between concepts in polymer physics and in biological parlance. A landmark was the so-called "Flory's hypothesis" from 1971: the so-called persistence length of a polymer is the parameter which best describe its folding, that is to say the correlation between neighbouring atoms in its primary and higher dimensional structures. The persistence length of a polymer is proportional to the inverse of its radius of gyration. This led to the conclusion that the folding of biological molecules such as DNA or proteins should be stabilized by an enthalpic mechanism: a new research field was born called "protein thermodynamics". However, at the end of the 20th century, it was realized that the persistence length is not really a folding parameter. Other parameters had to be introduced to characterize folding, and in particular a so-called " contact order ". It was also realized that the folding of proteins and nucleic acids is an entropic process, stabilized by the release of conformational heat. This "floppiness" is also due to an enthalpic stabilization: a negative non-bonded potential

An overview of game-theoretic approaches to energy-efficient resourceallocation in wireless networks is presented. Focusing on multiple-accessnetworks, it is demonstrated that game theory can be used as an effective toolto study resource allocation in wireless networks with quality-of-service (QoS)constraints. A family of non-cooperative (distributed) games is presented inwhich each user seeks to choose a strategy that maximizes its own utility whilesatisfying its QoS requirements. The utility function considered here measuresthe number of reliable bits that are transmitted per joule of energy consumedand, hence, is particulary suitable for energy-constrained networks. Theactions available to each user in trying to maximize its own utility are atleast the choice of the transmit power and, depending on the situation, theuser may also be able to choose its transmission rate, modulation, packet size,multiuser receiver, multi-antenna processing algorithm, or carrier allocationstrategy. The best-response strategy and Nash equilibrium for each game ispresented. Using this game-theoretic framework, the effects of power control,rate control, modulation, temporal and spatial signal processing, carrierallocation strategy and delay QoS constraints on energy efficiency and networkcapacity are quantified.

This paper develops a JavaScript deep learning platform and application. The platform is based on Open Neural Network Exchange (ONNX), and it runs on standard JavaScript engines such as Node.js and web browsers. The application can conduct distributed training with a high degree of parallelism and low communication overhead. The source code of the platform is open and is compatible with the TensorFlow Distribute training framework. The performance of the application is tested on both a server with a GPU and multiple clients with CPUs. Training the large version of the Stanford Natural Language Processing (NLP) corpus with 77 GB of data takes the platform about 25 minutes on a server with a GPU and less than 10 minutes on four clients with CPUs. The platform developed in this paper can be a useful addition to the TensorFlow Distribute training framework and significantly enhances its ability to conduct distributed training of deep learning models with high throughput on a broad array of hardware. segment 1 The paper provides a JavaScript deep learning platform and application to conduct distributed deep learning training. The platform and application are built on ONNX, which is an open standard for representing deep learning models and exchanging their formats. The application allows for dividing the data and machines into different groups and running the training on theses groups in parallel. It also allows the machines communicating with each other without much communication overhead. The source code of the platform is also available, which can be extensible and easily be compiled for different types of hardware. segment 2 The platform and application have some strengths and some weaknesses. The main advantage of the application is that it enables conducting distributed training with a high degree of parallelism and low communication overhead. There are multiple versions of the platform, which can be useful for running the training on servers with GPUs and on a number of clients with CPUs. In terms of weaknesses, the application has some dependencies on third-party packages and ONNX, which can make the deployment a bit more complicated.

This paper presents a set of experiments investigating what kinds of information are captured in common unsupervised approaches to sentence representation learning. The results are non-trivial and somewhat surprising. For example, they show that it is possible to reconstruct word order from bag of words representations, and they show that LSTM sentence autoencoders encode interpretable features even for randomly permuted nonsense sentences.
Effective unsupervised sentence representation learning is an important and largely unsolved problem in NLP, and this kind of work seems like it should be straightforwardly helpful towards that end. In addition, the experimental paradigm presented here is likely more broadly applicable to a range of representation learning systems. Some of the results seem somewhat strange, but I see no major technical concerns, and think that that they are informative. I recommend acceptance.
One minor red flag: 
- The massive drop in CBOW performance in Figures 1b and 4b are not explained, and seem implausible enough to warrant serious further investigation. Can you be absolutely certain that those results would appear with a different codebase and different random seed implementing the same model? Fortunately, this point is largely orthogonal to the major results of the paper.
Two writing comments:
- I agree that the results with word order and CBOW are surprising, but I think it's slightly misleading to say that CBOW is predictive of word order. It doesn't represent word order at all, but it's possible to probabilistically reconstruct word order from the information that it does encode.
- Saying that "LSTM auto-encoders are more effective at encoding word order than word content" doesn't really make sense. These two quantities aren't comparable.

Kim Jong Un's attempts to portray North Korea in a positive light despite the known issues of poverty and starvation can be attributed to several factors: 1. Propaganda: The North Korean government heavily controls the information that its citizens receive. By portraying the country as prosperous and strong, Kim Jong Un maintains control and suppresses dissent. This also helps to foster a sense of national pride and loyalty among the citizens. 2. International Image: Despite the widespread knowledge of North Korea's issues, maintaining a positive image can still influence international relations. It can help the country attract foreign aid, negotiate better terms in diplomatic talks, and avoid international sanctions. 3. Legitimacy: By showing North Korea as a strong and prosperous nation, Kim Jong Un seeks to legitimize his rule both domestically and internationally. This is particularly important given the hereditary nature of his leadership. 4. Deterrence: The portrayal of North Korea as a powerful nation serves as a deterrent to other countries that might consider military action against it. This is particularly relevant given the country's ongoing tensions with the United States and other nations. 5. Internal Stability: By controlling the narrative and portraying the country in a positive light, Kim Jong Un can maintain internal stability. This helps to prevent unrest, rebellion, or attempts at regime change from within the country. In conclusion, while the outside world may be aware of the realities within North Korea, the portrayal of the country as strong and prosperous serves several important functions for Kim Jong Un's regime.

In the immediate aftermath of the assassination of Abraham Lincoln, the cast and crew were all rounded up to give statements to the police. After the statements were taken, they were put on bail to testify when and if Booth was brought to trial.
[This is Harry Hawk.](_URL_0_) Hawk was the only actor on stage at the moment the president was shot. On the night after the assassination, he penned this letter:
 > Washington, April 16, 1865
 > My Dear Parents,
 > This is the first time I have had to write to you since the assassination of our dear President on Friday night, as I have been in custody nearly ever since, I was one of the principal witnesses of that sad affair, being the only one on the stage at the time of the fatal shot. I was playing Asa Trenchard, in the “American Cousin,” The “old lady” of the theatre had just gone off the stage, and I was answering her exit speech when I heard the shot fired. I turned, looked up at the President’s box, heard the man exclaim, “Sic semper tyrannis,” saw him jump from the box, seize the flag on the staff and drop to the stage; he slipped when he gained the stage, but got upon his feet in a moment, brandished a large knife, saying, “The South shall be free!” turned his face in the direction I stood, and I recognized him as John Wilkes Booth. He ran toward me, and I, seeing the knife, thought I was the one he was after, ran off the stage and up a flight of stairs. He made his escape out of a door, directly in the rear of the theatre, mounted a horse and rode off.
 > The above all occurred in the space of a quarter of a minute, and at the time I did not know that the President was shot; although, if I had tried to stop him, he would have stabbed me.
 > I am now under one thousand dollars bail to appear as a witness when Booth is tried, if caught.
 > All the above I have sworn to. You may imagine the excitement in the theatre, which was crowded, with cries of “Hang him!” “Who was he?” & c., from every one present.
 > In about fifteen minutes after the occurrence, the President was carried out and across the street. I was requested to walk down to police headquarters and give my evidence. They then put me under one thousand dollars bond to appear at 10 o’clock next morning. I then walked about for some time as the city was wild with excitement, and then I went to bed. At half-past three I was called by an aid of the President, to go the house where he was lying, to give another statement before Judge Carter, Secretary Stanton, and other high officials assembled there. I did so, and went to bed again. On Saturday I gave bail.
 > It was the saddest thing I ever knew. The city only the night before was illuminated, and everybody was so happy. Now it is all sadness. Everybody looks gloomy and sad.
 > On that night the play was going off so well. Mr. and Mrs. Lincoln enjoyed it much. She was laughing at my speech when the shot was fired. In fact it was one laugh from the time the curtain went up until it fell — and to think of such a sorrowful ending! It is an era in my life that I shall never forget. Inclosed is a piece of fringe of the flag the President was holding when shot.
Harry Hawk's letter appeared in the *Evening Star*, April 24, 1865.
For **much** more information on this topic, read [Backstage at the Lincoln Assassination: The Untold Story of the Actors and Stagehands at Ford’s Theatre](_URL_2_) by Thomas Bogar.
There were no widespread persecutions of the acting community in the United States following Lincoln's death. On the contrary, the postbellum North experienced a boom in the entertainment industry. The rapid growth of railroad infrastructure, new copyright laws, and the eventual rise of the [Theatrical Syndicate](_URL_1_) meant that even smaller, more remote towns could maintain permanent theatrical spaces. The period between 1865 and 1885 saw the theater scene in the United States mature from its marginal "middle-brow" beginnings into a community that was producing world-class entertainment presented by legendary international celebrities like Mark Twain and Joseph Jefferson.
There were, of course, disparaging comments made about actors in the press at the time (as in any time) but for the most part, Booth was portrayed negatively for being a Confederate sympathizer, not for being an actor.
For further reading on the period, I recommend The Cambridge History of American Theatre, Volume II: 1870-1945 by Don B. Wilmeth and Christopher Bigsby

Michał Łasko (born September 16, 1974 in Warsaw) is the Polish football player who currently plays as defender/midfielder/striker at Widzew Lodz of the Ekstraklasa.
Łasko made his debut on August 24, 1998 against Górnik Zabrze and has played over 300 matches since then including games for Lech Poznań, Legia Warszawa, Wisła Kraków, Piast Gliwice, Miedź Legnica, Ruch Chorzów, Jagiellonia Białystok, Polonia Bytom, Korona Kielce, Podbeskidzie Bielsko-Biala, LKS Lodz, Widzew Lodz and Siarka Tarnobrzeg.

The 2 circuits you removed will need to be re-fed from the new sub panel. If the sub panel is the same brand, you might be able to reinstall the original circuit breakers in it to re-feed those 2 circuits.
If you have breakers terminal screws that are serving more than one conductor, called double-lugging; consider moving circuits to the sub panel so that none are double-lugged - or combine the them with a short length of the same size wire (called a "pigtail") in a wirenut and use the pigtail to feed them.
If using a cable with aluminum conductors, be sure to apply an oxide inhibitor on the aluminum before securing in a terminal or other pressure connector.
Connect the ground wire, which is bare, to the grounding bar or buss in the sub panel.
Connect the neutral wire, which will be white, to the neutral buss in the sub panel.
Attach the hot wires (black and red or blue) to the main breaker lugs of the sub panel.
Take the time to tidy up the wires and connections so that you can easily trace them back to their breaker if the need arises in the future.
This will ensure that the neutral and ground are connected at the main panel and not the sub panel.
The buss bar bonding jumper can be identified as a long screw or metal strap that effectively bonds the neutral buss bar to the grounded panel enclosure / ground buss bar.
Do not interchange ground and neutral wires in the other's buss bar. Install the breakers.
Check to see if all the circuits are working.
You will not pass an electrical inspection unless you complete this step.

The benefits of attending school from home, also known as online school, can be a great advantage for those who prefer a more flexible learning environment. One of the main benefits is the ability to manage time more efficiently. Online schools generally provide students with a flexible schedule that allows them to work at their own pace. This can be especially helpful for students who struggle to keep up with the pace of traditional classroom settings or those who have other time-consuming responsibilities such as part-time jobs or caregiving.
Another benefit of online school is the absence of distractions commonly found in traditional public schools. In a physical school setting, students may face distractions such as classroom chatter, peer pressure, or unnecessary disruptions. Online schools eliminate these distractions, creating a more focused learning environment that may better support student attention and productivity.
However, online school also has its drawbacks. One of the main concerns is the lack of socialization opportunities compared to traditional public schools. Socialization is an integral part of the learning process as it allows students to develop communication, teamwork, and conflict resolution skills. Online schools may not provide the same level of socialization opportunities, which can potentially lead to feelings of isolation or limited development of social skills.
Additionally, online schools may not be suitable for all types of learners. While some students thrive in a self-paced environment, others may require more direct instruction and face-to-face interaction with teachers to fully understand complex concepts. The lack of immediate feedback and personalized support could potentially hinder academic performance for students who struggle with self-motivation or independent learning.
In conclusion, while online school offers benefits such as time management and fewer distractions, it also presents drawbacks like limited socialization opportunities and potential difficulties in understanding concepts that require hands-on learning or direct interaction with instructors. Ultimately, the decision of whether online school is more beneficial than public school will depend on individual factors and learning preferences. Some students may find online school to be a more advantageous learning environment, while others may thrive better in a traditional public school setting. 
It is crucial to consider each student's unique needs and circumstances when determining the most suitable learning environment to ensure their academic success and personal growth.

The Italian peninsula has a long history of republics due to a variety of factors. One reason is the geographic location of Italy, which is surrounded by the Mediterranean Sea and has a long coastline. This made it difficult for empires to control the region, and allowed for more independence and autonomy for the people living there.
Another reason is the cultural and political traditions of the Italian people. For centuries, Italy was home to many city-states, which were independent and often rivalrous entities. These city-states had their own governments and laws, and were often at war with each other. This led to a strong sense of independence and a deep-rooted belief in the importance of democracy and freedom.
Additionally, the Italian peninsula was home to many powerful and influential cities, such as Rome, Florence, and Venice. These cities were centers of trade and commerce, and had a large amount of wealth and power. This allowed them to maintain their independence and resist the control of larger empires.
Overall, the Italian peninsula has a long and rich history of republics due to its geographic location, cultural and political traditions, and the influence of powerful and influential cities.

How to Stop Your Dog from Chewing Things it Shouldn't
Summary:
Understand why dogs chew. Train your dog. Make things taste bad. Provide appropriate chewing toys. distract your dog.
Dogs have a natural instinct to chew, and it's important to keep them from chewing on things they shouldn't. This can include furniture, clothing, shoes, books, and anything else you don't want them to destroy.
Fortunately, you can stop this behavior with the right training and by making those items taste bad. You can also offer them alternatives, such as chew toys.
Why Dogs Chew
Dogs chew for a variety of reasons. For puppies, it's a way to ease teething pain and explore their surroundings. For older dogs, it can be a way to deal with boredom, anxiety, or simply because they enjoy it.
No matter the reason, it's important to address the issue before it gets out of control. Not only can chewing on things they shouldn't harm your belongings, but it can also be dangerous for your dog.
They can swallow small objects or pieces of glass, get sick from eating toxic chemicals, or get injured by sharp objects.
How to Train Your Dog to Stop Chewing Things it Shouldn't
Teaching your dog not to chew on things it shouldn't is a matter of training and consistency. Here's a step-by-step approach to help you stop this behavior:
Understand why your dog is chewing.
Before you can address the chewing behavior, it's important to understand why your dog is doing it. Is it teething pain, boredom, or anxiety? Understanding the cause will help you address it effectively.
Make things taste bad.
To help stop your dog from chewing on things it shouldn't, try making those items taste unpleasant. You can use things like chili powder, lemon juice, or bitter apple spray to make them unappealing.
Be consistent in applying this technique, and your dog will eventually learn that these items are not worth chewing on.
Provide appropriate chewing toys.
Offer your dog alternative options, such as chew toys or rawhide bones, to help satisfy its chewing needs. Make sure to give it something whenever you see it chewing on something it shouldn't be.
With consistency and patience, your dog will eventually learn that only certain items are meant for chewing.
How to Distract Your Dog from Chewing Things it Shouldn't
In some cases, simply distracting your dog can be an effective way to stop it from chewing on things it shouldn't. When you catch it in the act, try offering it a chew toy or rawhide bone instead.
If that doesn't work, try redirecting its attention by using a command like "sit" or "lay down." This will help it associate chewing with negative consequences and break the habit over time.
You can also try using a deterrent spray, such as a bitter apple spray, to make the item it's chewing on taste unpleasant.
With consistency and patience, you can help your dog break the habit of chewing on things it shouldn't and develop more appropriate chewing behaviors.

Studying Venus is undoubtedly a worthwhile pursuit, despite the numerous challenges and dangers it presents. One of the primary reasons is that Venus, like Earth, possesses many similar characteristics that make it an intriguing scientific subject. Firstly, Venus and Earth are both terrestrial planets, meaning they have a solid surface and a similar mass. This makes Venus an excellent analog for studying the fundamental processes that shape terrestrial planets within our solar system.
Secondly, Venus and Earth have similar sizes and compositions, primarily consisting of carbon dioxide and nitrogen atmospheres. Despite its thick and toxic atmosphere, studying Venus can offer invaluable insights into how similar planets evolve and how atmospheric processes regulate a planet's climate and habitability. Understanding the dynamics of Venus' atmosphere could help us comprehend the past and potential future of Earth's climate and the impact of human activities on our planet.
Furthermore, Venus' extreme environment presents a unique opportunity for pushing the limits of technological innovation. Developing tools and instruments capable of withstanding Venus' extreme temperatures (around 462°C or 864°F), high pressure, and acidic atmosphere can lead to advancements in materials science, engineering, and other fields with potential applications on Earth.
Lastly, Venus' proximity to Earth makes it an ideal target for scientific investigations. With its close distance, we can gain valuable insights into Venus' geology, composition, and history through a combination of terrestrial-based observations and spacecraft missions. This, in turn, can deepen our understanding of planetary science and the broader knowledge of the solar system as a whole.
In conclusion, the scientific value of studying Venus lies in its numerous similarities to Earth and the insights it offers into planetary processes. Despite the daunting challenges of exploring such an extreme environment, the potential benefits of such research endeavors outweigh the risks. Through the study of Venus, we can gain a better understanding of our own planet, advance technological innovations, and deepen our appreciation of the complexities within our solar system.

We present new X-ray timing and spectral observations of PSR J1930+1852, theyoung energetic pulsar at the center of the non-thermal supernova remnantG54.1+0.3. Using data obtained with the Rossi X-ray Timing Explorer and ChandraX-ray observatories we have derived an updated timing ephemeris of the 136 mspulsar spanning 6 years. During this interval, however, the period evolutionshows significant variability from the best fit constant spin-down rate of$\dot P = 7.5112(6) \times 10^{-13}$ s s$^{-1}$, suggesting strong timing noiseand/or glitch activity. The X-ray emission is highly pulsed ($71\pm5%$modulation) and is characterized by an asymmetric, broad profile ($\sim 70%$duty cycle) which is nearly twice the radio width. The spectrum of the pulsedemission is well fitted with an absorbed power law of photon index $\Gamma =1.2\pm0.2$; this is marginally harder than that of the unpulsed component. Thetotal 2-10 keV flux of the pulsar is $1.7 \times 10^{-12}$ erg cm$^{-2}$s$^{-1}$. These results confirm PSR J1930+1852 as a typical Crab-like pulsar.

We present the results of a multiwavelength ($u$, $v$, $b$, $y$) CCD photometry and membership segregation of the open cluster NGC2682 (M67). The photometry was performed with the Franciscomorales Telescope (FT) at thehora Yin noodle soup (YNS), and with the Yale-New Haven Hospital (YNH) reflector at Hippo's La Sierra Observatory (HSO), both in Spain. We use these data sets to search for member stars in the solar neighborhood. The aim of this work is to present a reliable and objective method to determine the membership in an open cluster, based on the difference of the photometric parameters between cluster members and field stars. We apply this method to the cluster NGC2682 (M67), because it contains a well-known member, namely HR8883 (the cluster's brightest star), and because it has been the subject of much previous photometric study. We show that this method is effective at selecting the cluster members, in agreement with the visual inspection. We also compare the results with those of previous studies, and we conclude that there are significant photometric differences between field stars and cluster members, even when the membership is determined visually. Our method is suitable for identifying members of open clusters with relatively small number of observations, as long as a well-known member star is present.

You'll find that sometimes a song will have the artwork already attached and sometimes not and this depends mostly on it's original source or format.;
Usually upon plugging your mp3 into the computer a window will pop up asking what you would like to do. If this happens then click "open folder to view files." If this does not happen then you will have to open "my computer" and look for your device under "devices with removable storage"
(this creates a copy of the music file onto the computer)
(A new playlist is created by selecting "File" then "New Playlist")
With this list of music on the iTunes playlist, you are now ready to attach your album artwork. This must be done one song, or one album at a time.
Select the song you wish to work with and right-click it.
Select "Get info" then click the tab that says "Artwork." If the song already has an artwork attached you will see it there. If not, then press "Add" and you can then browse your entire computer to attach any image you like.
Remember that if you do not have the album artwork on file in your computer (which is almost always the case) then you will need to go and find it first and the best way to do this through the internet.
One such place to find them is www.amazonmp3.com or the search engine Google images are very useful. Once you find the picture you wish to attach, right click the image and select "save picture as." then save the picture in the new folder you have created which is under "my pictures; my album artwork" once the song's image is saved in that file you may then go back to iTunes and by clicking "add" you can look for and browse through your "my album artwork" folder to select that picture and it will then be added to the mp3 file as an attachment.
This means that the songs you initially copied onto the new folder on the desktop in the beginning are also changed. Once you make all the album artwork modifications to each song you want then you simply take those files from that new folder on your desktop and re-copy them to your mp3 player. A window will pop up telling you that your device already contains a file with the same name and when that happens, click "replace." This means that the old music files on your mp3 player that don't have artwork attachments will be replaced with the new files that you have just modified to contain album artwork.
Don't be surprised if it takes a moment or two for your mp3 player to recognize the new album artwork changes. This is especially common with cell phones and it has to do with the processor.

The Houston Texans are an American football team based in Houston, Texas. They are members of the American Football Conference (AFC) in the National Football League (NFL). The team was established in 2000 as a new NFL franchise, and originally named the Houston Texans after the state of Texas. The team played its first two seasons as an expansion team, joining the NFL as an AFC South Division rival of the Indianapolis Colts. The team has changed name twice since its establishment. The team's current name, adopted in 2012, comes from the Houston Texans, LLC, the entity responsible for the team in the Tenneco Sports model of team ownership. The Texans are one of the most successful franchises in the NFL, having never missed the playoffs since their inception. They have won two American Football Conference (AFC) championships and two conference divisions. The Texans are one of only two teams to have a quarterback, Brian Hoyer, start every regular season game since their debut (the other team being the New England Patriots). In 2015, the Texans qualified for the playoffs for the 11th time in the last 12 seasons and for the first time since the 2012 season. After losing their Week 1 matchup to the Kansas City Chiefs, the Texans won their next seven games, including key road victories against the Cincinnati Bengals and New England Patriots. However, they were upset 23–16 at home by the Jacksonville Jaguars in the AFC Wild Card round. The 2015 season was head coach Bill O'Brien's first winning season with the Texans, finishing with a record of 12–4. The Texans have one of the most well-known and heated rivalries with the Indianapolis Colts. Through the first 14 games of the 2016 season, the Texans and Colts were not aligned on a schedule due to the NFL's schedule rotation, thus creating a "riterion matchup." With a win or loss against the Colts, the Texans would win the AFC South for the 15th time in the last 16 seasons. The Colts, under new ownership and a new head coach, were expected to be a rebuilding team looking to improve from their 2–14 season in 2015, while the Texans, with their newly renovated stadium, were expected to contend for the AFC South title. The rivalry dates back to their first game against each other in the 1999 season. The Texans have one of the most star-studded fight songs in the NFL, The Battle Hymn of the Texans. In 2016, the team hired country music star Hunter Hayes to record a remake of the song. In 2021, the Houston Astros of Major League Baseball (MLB) became the fourth different NFL team to become a licensee of a team owned by Doughty Hanson, joining the New York Yankees, Arizona Cardinals, and Cleveland Browns. Houston was the most expensive NFL team to license a team in the Doughty Hanson portfolio, and the second Houston NFL team in as many years after the Houston Astros, the first NFL team to be owned by a different franchise in the last 60 years. The Texans are tied with the Tennessee Titans for the most 20+ point victories in NFL history. The game-winning touchdown against the Jacksonville Jaguars in Week 2 of the 2016 season was also the 20th+ point game in franchise history. The Texans retired number (#90) and their jersey number for future years' Pro Bowl selections is 6. The Texans hold the record for most consecutive regular season games with a sale (213). As of the end of the 2022 season, the Texans are the most successful franchise in the NFL with a.500 or better record in regular season play (79-79). The Texans appeared as an advanced scout in the video game Madden NFL 25.''.

This is a complex question that depends on the specific circumstances. It is certainly possible for someone who is well-informed about symptoms and onset of schizophrenia to recognize its onset in the early stages. However, this is not always the case. In some cases, the first indicators can be the atypical mood or behavior of the person, as well as changes in their usual routines. The early symptoms of schizophrenia are often difficult to recognize, so this requires someone who is close to the person to be especially attentive to changes in the other's behavior and interactions. Late on in the disease, the positive symptoms of schizophrenia (i.e., the hallucinations and delusions) emerge. These are obvious signs that the disease has developed. It is also important to note that the recognition of a person with schizophrenia is not always 100% ego-syntonic at the beginning, since there is some dissatisfaction with the situation, a search for support from others, and, in general, a certain emotional conflict in the person. Thus, it is important to emphasize that the possibility of early identification of schizophrenia does not mean that a person will not feel any discomfort in the early stages. It is important to remember that the "disease" in schizophrenia emerges in the social representation of the patient in a certain diagnosis, not in reality.

Take a look at this article on degrees as it relates to temperature: _URL_0_.
From the article, 'A degree can be defined as a set change in temperature measured against a given scale'. What this means it that when you are saying something is X degrees C/F/K, you're indicating that temperature of the object relative to a specific scale. The Celsius scale is relative to the temperatures that water changes state at, for example. 
The Kelvin differs from other temperature measurement scales in a very important way: its scale is not relative but rather an absolute temperature scale. Basically, 0 kelvins is the point where there ceases to be any thermal motion in the measured sample. 
Until about the '50s, people used to say 'degrees Kelvin' or 'degrees Absolute', but that allowed for ambiguity with some other scientific temperature scales that have since, for the most part, fallen by the wayside. When K became an SI unit, a new convention for it was codified dictating that measurements would be referred to as X kelvins or X K. 
Logically, this also makes sense since saying 'degrees' has become colloquially associates with relative temperature scales, even if technically it may not be so. Not using degrees, to me, reenforces that K is an absolute scale.

It will be much easier to pick out the correct colors if you have a photo to look at, as well as help with the shape and shading.
You can use a picture of your own eye or one from the internet.
Pay attention to the size of the tear duct and waterline, as this will be important for making it look realistic. Also notice areas where there are glared or reflective spots; you should draw these areas out so you know to avoid coloring inside them. If you plan to go back over light areas afterwards with something such as a white gel pen, only draw out the largest light spots.
Do not draw the eyelashes yet, this will be done later.
Be sure to test them out before using them to make sure they match your reference photo.
Having a white pencil will help you blend over areas if you make a mistake.
Don't sharpen the pencils too much, as the tip is more likely to break if it is at a very sharp point.
This is so the graphite of the pencil doesn't mix with your colors.
This should not be the areas you drew out where there was glare on the eye.

The top of the iris will likely be one of these spots, as well as some of the details in the iris.
Adding more color later is easier than taking it out.
This will help you reference where the deeper areas of the iris are later.
This color will be the color that appears most prominently in the iris, such as orange, light brown, or blue. Try to avoid picking a dark color.
In the case of orange, it could be a brighter orange or even a red if used with caution.
This will help it look more 3D.
It is easiest with a black marker or pen, however you can still use a colored pencil. Make sure they curve and don't go straight up. Look at your reference photo to see how they should curve under the waterline.
In a photo with cooler light, you will use a grey. If the photo has warmer light you will use a more pinkish tone.
You can also add in reflections or shadows of the eyelashes.
Don't make them too visible or it will make the eye less realistic. Pay attention to where they are most visible in your reference photo.

Venus is known as Earth's sister planet because it has many similarities to our own world. It orbits around its sun like Earth does but takes 225 days for one orbit instead of 365. The surface temperature averages 735 degrees Fahrenheit (462 Celsius), which makes it much hotter than any other place outside of space. Its atmosphere consists mostly of carbon dioxide that traps heat from solar radiation making temperatures even higher at ground level. Scientists believe there may be liquid water under the thick layer of clouds covering most of the planet's visible surface. There are also large volcanoes located near the poles that spew plumes of ash into the sky every few hundred thousand years. In addition, scientists have discovered numerous craters all over the planet that were created by meteorite impacts thousands of millions of years ago. All these features make Venus very interesting scientifically speaking since they can help us learn more about how planets form and evolve through time. Getting good customer service can sometimes be a challenge, but there are ways to increase your chances of having a positive experience. Here's how: 1. Know exactly what it is you want and be able to explain it fully: Before you contact customer service, make sure you understand your issue and what you want the outcome to be. This will help you communicate your needs clearly and effectively. 2. Reserve a sizable chunk of time to deal with your issue: Customer service calls can take time, so make sure you're not rushed. This will help you stay patient and focused. 3. Make sure to make your call in an environment free from distractions: This will help you concentrate on the conversation and ensure you don't miss any important information. 4. Prepare yourself to navigate through many voice prompts and be prepared to wait on hold: This is a common part of the process, so be patient and stay calm. 5. Have all of the information and supplies you’ll need to talk to the representative: This includes account numbers, previous correspondence, and any other relevant information. 6. Call or visit early in the morning if possible: This is often when wait times are shortest. 7. Speak in a friendly tone of voice and have a positive attitude: This can help set a positive tone for the conversation and make the representative more willing to help you. 8. Make statements that demonstrate your dissatisfaction: Be clear about your issue, but avoid being rude or aggressive. 9. Explain your problem to the agent: Be as detailed as possible to help them understand your issue. 10. Understand the limits of the customer service representative: They may not have the power to resolve your issue, so be prepared to escalate if necessary. 11. Escalate your issue to a supervisor, if you feel you’re not getting the service you deserve: Be polite but firm in your request. 12. Get details about the agent: This includes their name and employee number, in case you need to reference the conversation later. 13. Ask the customer service agent to make a detailed note in your account: This can be helpful if you need to call back. 14. Escalate your complaint further: If you're still not satisfied, consider contacting a higher level of management or filing a formal complaint. 15. Lodge a complaint with consumer sites or the Better Business Bureau: This can help bring attention to your issue. 16. Look to social media as a possible solution: Many companies respond quickly to complaints made on social media. 17. Reward a good customer service experience: If you have a positive experience, consider leaving a good review or sending a thank you note. This can help encourage good customer service in the future.

If we are talking about communism as Marx described it, not the Communism of Marixt-Leninism, there is one example of it working as intended: The Paris Commune of 1870-1. 
The Commune has always been extremely controversial. People like Chomsky write of it as if it were a utopia, while others play up the terror which was certainly a part of it--the workers of Paris weren't going to be able to drastically alter their culture/government without some sort of resistance, and these sort of revolutions are always accompanied by some sort of terror. Either way,[ Marx himself wrote and gave a speech about the Commune to the First International.](_URL_0_) Essentially, he says that this is what communism would look like: direct democracy, politicians being paid the exact same amount as the workers, and the workshops and unions having full control over the means of production. Note the lack of centralized state planning. This was key in Marx's conception of communism. It was supposed to be an association of communes where working people were once again united with their means of production, where each person made the same wage, and where *the freedom of one was the condition of the freedom of all.*

Starting your journey in Harvest Moon Back to Nature can be a bit overwhelming, especially if you're a first-timer. The first day is all about familiarizing yourself with your surroundings. Gather some tools in your house and head into town to watch the various cut scenes of characters. This will give you a sense of the community and the people you'll be interacting with. After your town visit, return to your farm and go immediately to your toolbox inside your house. This is where you'll store all your farming tools. Next, head to the mountain by going to the south end of your farm and crossing the bridge over the river. Look for the Blue medicine plant, one of the more profitable mountain harvests. If you still have energy left, visit the mine. Congratulations, you've completed your first day! Now, it's time to fall into a routine. Visit Barley's farm for a cutscene and stock up on chicken feed. Focus on planting grass and acquiring the brush, milker, and a cow. Build squares of grass and surround them with the rocks you've been saving. As summer arrives, buy pineapple seeds from Won, the funny-looking merchant with a yellow jacket. Plant plenty of pineapples and watch your money grow. When fall comes, be prepared to make a ton of fields and have the best watering can ready. A really important mountain harvest lies next to the lake deep into the mountain. Buy one packet of sweet potato seeds for each field you have. By now, your farm should be set with a cow or two and a sheep. Winter is here! If you're getting chocolate, the winter thanksgiving would be the time to do so. For extra cash, there's a second mine opened up deeper into the mountain where you found the truffle. Once a girl has reached the red heart level, you'll have the option of buying a blue feather in the store. Congratulations, you've completed your first year in Harvest Moon Back to Nature! With these tips, you're well on your way to becoming a successful farmer.

This is an incredible question and I'm very glad you asked. Before I answer it, I'd like to briefly describe what Shay's Rebellion actually was:
**Context:** 
Shays' Rebellion was an armed uprising led by former Massachusetts Militiamen and Veterans of the American Revolution which took place between 1786 - 1787. Daniel Shays led several thousand "rebels" to fight against the economic injustices that were facing farmers and agrarian peasants all across America. These farmers were experiencing extreme poverty following the end of the Revolutionary War. All across Massachusetts (and the rest of America) farmers saw their lands foreclosed on in unfair property seizers, and they wanted to fight back, which they did here. They were also trying to fight taxes which were beginning to be levied against them. They fought this in many ways, but among them was closing and obscuring roads so that government agents couldn't reach rural parts of the state. Shays' Rebellion would ultimately be put down, but it startled the gentry who feared further uprisings throughout the United States. 
I also like to point out that the naming of this event is really interesting. The people who did this, called themselves Regulators ( [modeling off of the North Carolina Regulators](_URL_2_) who also fought against economic injustices before the start of the American Revolution.). The idea of Civilian Regulation was a popular idea that sought to end government corruption and stamp out the overwhelming power of the gentry. They believed that if the government wasn't regulating itself on behalf of "We the People", then "the People" had the right to regulate, or take back the government -- to take it back and do what they believed was right. They didn't see themselves as a rebellion, but rather the gentry labeled them as such in order to de-legitimize their cause. The gentry didn't want to call these men "militiamen" or "regulators" for this reason (which they clearly were), but instead, branded them as "rebels" who needed to be stopped. 
Veterans like Benjamin Lincoln would raise militias on their own and mounted their own assaults against the "rebels." They call themselves the "the Massachusetts Militia" even though it was the former militias who they were fighting! So as they begin to debate this on the national stage, especially in 1787 at the Constitutional convention, the gentry singled out Daniel Shay (even though there were actually many other leaders), and they said he was crazy and people were only following a demagogue. They hailed The Massachusetts Militia as the victors and saviors and asserted that militias are what will save America in the future against such madness.
**Answer:**
Although the Constitution is drawn up in 1787, it is not ratified until December of 1791 when the Bill of Rights was finally agreed upon. Whether or not to include the Bill of Rights (and what to include inside it) was a matter of extreme contestation between the Founders and everything within it was deeply fought over. 
When we look at the Second Amendment specifically, we should look at a few things before hand. First, by the 1790s, other small rebellions had popped up all over the country. Terry Bouton's article "A Road Closed: Rural Insurgency in Post-Independence Pennsylvania" (The Journal of American History, Vol. 87, No. 3 (Dec., 2000), pp. 855-887) masterfully explains the fighting and rebellion that took place in the rural countrysides of Pennsylvania that mirrored what had happened in Massachusetts with Shays' Rebellion. Simply put, the gentry were terrified that they were losing control of rural America, and as a result they would not be able to seize foreclosed land and collect taxes, which they deeply wanted. Empowering militias to be trained and carry firearms allowed the gentry to call up these men in times of need and suppress these rebellions that were taking place. 
Now there was already precedent in existence for protecting militias and their rights to bear arms in many states. Multiple other bills of rights from other states had already protected a militia's right to bear arms (such as [Section 13](_URL_1_) of Virginia's Declaration of Rights) and many of these states were fighting to have the federal government protect this as well. 
Now, look at the very wording of the [Second Amendment](_URL_0_).
 > A well regulated Militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the right of the people to keep and bear Arms, shall not be infringed.
"A well regulated Militia" is the key phrase. They are referring to the militias led by people like Benjamin Lincoln and his Massachusetts Militia not Shays and his "rebellion". The initial goal was to protect a state's right to call up arms against rebels, not to arm the masses. The Founders feared that in some states (like Rhode Island) that were already being drastically controlled by the poor (rather than the gentry), that local governments would start being able to choose who could keep and bear arms, and that by creating the Second Amendment, the gentry would always have the ability to call up and arm militias in times of need. 
**Clarification:** I also need to stress that this question mentioned Jefferson by name, however he was not a signer of the Constitution, but did certify the adoption of the Bill of Rights in 1792.
Tl;Dr: The second ammendement wasn't passed *in spite of* Shays' Rebellion, rather it was passed *because* of Shays' Rebellion.

We study the ground-state properties of two-flavor color superconducting (2SC) quark matter at finite density and temperature by using an effective chiral model with vector interaction, which is derived from QCD under the mean-field approximation. We find that there exists a new type of 2SC phase where quarks are paired into diquark condensates with different colors but same flavor. This novel phase has been named as Larkin-Ovchinnikova-Fulde-Ferreell (LOFF) state because it was first proposed to describe superfluidity in nuclear systems. In this LOFF state, we show that the gap parameter for pairing between quarks with opposite momenta depends on their relative angle. The magnitude of the gap decreases rapidly when they move away from each other along the Fermi surface. As a result, the energy gap vanishes completely near the boundary of the Brillouin zone.

Summary: The authors propose an input switched affine network to do character-level language modeling, a kind of RNN without pointwise nonlinearity, but with switching the transition matrix & bias based on the input character. This is motivated by intelligibility, since it allows decomposition of output contribution into these kappa_s^t terms, and use of basic linear algebra to probe the network.
Regarding myself as a reviewer, I am quite sure I understood the main ideas and arguments of this paper, but am not an expert on RNN language models or intelligibility/interpretability in ML.
I did not read any papers with a similar premise - closest related work I'm familiar with would be deconvnet for insight into vision-CNNs.
PRO:
I think this is original and novel work. This work is high quality, well written, and clearly is the result of a lot of work.
I found section 4.5 about projecting into readout subspace vs "computational" subspace most interesting and meaningful.
CON:
+ The main hesitation I have is that the results on both parts (ISAN model, and analysis of it) are not entirely convincing:
 (1) ISAN is only trained on small task (text8), not clear whether it can be a strong char-LM on larger scale tasks,
 (2) nor do the analysis sections provide all that much real insight in the learned network.
(1b) Other caveat towards ISAN architecture: this model in its proposed form is really only fit for small-vocabulary (i.e. character-based) language modeling, not a general RNN with large-vocab discrete input nor continuous input.
(2a) For analysis: many cute plots and fun ideas of quantities to look at, but not much concrete insights.
(2b) Not very clear which analysis is specific to the ISAN model, and which ideas will generalize to general nonlinear RNNs.
(2c) Re sec 4.2 - 4.3: It seems that the quantity \kappa_s^t on which analysis rests, isn't all that meaningful. Elaborating a bit on what I wrote in the question:
For example: Fig 2, for input letter "u" in revenue, there's a red spot where '_' character massively positively impacts the logit of 'e'. This seems quite meaningless, what would be the meaning of influence of '_' character? So it looks ot me that the switching matrix W_u (and prior W_n W_e etc) are using previous state in an interesting way to produce that following e. So that metric \kappa_s^t just doesn't seem very meaningful.
This remark relates to the last paragraph of Sec4.2.
Even though the list of cons here is longer than pro's, I recommend accept; specifically because the originality of this work will in any case make it more vulnerable to critiques. This work is well-motivated, very well-executed, and can inspire many more interesting investigations along these lines.

The Learning to Draw Samples: With Application to Amortized MLE for Generative Adversarial Learning answers the question how to train stochastic neural networks to draw samples from given targets. The method proposed in the paper is generally applicable for any black-box model, with strong performance in image generation. The paper has the following merits: (1) The method is general and easy to implement. It only requires a small change on the output layer, which is the most operation part of a neural network model. (2) The samples generated are realistic and could be printed on paper. (3) The theory behind the method is also detailed. In particular, the proof of convergence relies on Stein’s lemma, a powerful method to compute the gradient of divergence. The main limitation of this method is that it only handles discrete outputs. Meanwhile, the computational cost of this method is expensive because it needs to solve a dual problem. Overall, this method is generally applicable and gives state-of-the-art performance in image generation. The proposed method in this paper solves the following problem. Assume we have a neural network $f_\theta(\mathbf{x})$, where $\theta$ denotes the weight parameters, and the data is denoted as $\mathcal{D}$. We are given the target distribution $p_T(\mathbf{x})$, but we do not have the its normalizer. Thus, we can not directly compute the likelihood of the data. Instead, we can compute the cross entropy between the actual distribution and $p_T(\mathbf{x})$, which is related to the log-probability of the data. So our goal is to optimize the neural network so that the output changes along a direction to increase the likelihood of the data. This direction is the Stein variational gradient, which can be computed based on Stein’s lemma. The main steps are as follows. 1. Compute the unnormalized density of the target $p_T(x)$ and compute the cross entropy with the actual distribution. 2. Apply the ReWeight technique to transform the cross entropy into a normalized form. 3. Compute the Stein variational gradient based on Stein’s lemma. 4. Update the network parameters by minimizing the corresponding dual objective function. Overall, the theory behind this method is well motivated and the implementation is not difficult. Some questions arise from this method: (1) Is this method guaranteed to converge to a local minimum? (2) What kind of applications can this method be applied to? (3) Can this method be extended to continuous outputs case? These are interesting questions, and we leave it as a promising future work. Some examples of using this method for training deep energy models are provided in the paper. Theoretically, this method can be applied to train other neural networks for different tasks.

We analyze elastic scattering and fusion cross section data for the 7Li+208Pb system at near-Coulomb-barrier energies using the folding potential. This is a systematic exploration of the potential energy surface (PES) in the system. The potential has 27 parameters that were fitted to a large data base of experimental elastic scattering and fusion cross section data. We also perform a constrained global diagonalization of the coupled-channels Hamiltonian to determine excitation energies and residues. The resulting PES is very deep and has a global minimum close to the separatrix. The appearance of the deep PES could have important implications for the behavior of projectiles very close to heavy targets. *This work was supported by the U.S. Department of Energy, Office of Science, Office of Nuclear Physics under Award No. DE-FG02-94ER40834. The potential energy surface (PES) for the 7Li+208Pb system is analyzed by performing an extended optical model analysis of elastic scattering and fusion cross section data for the system. Fitting a large data base of elastic scattering and fusion cross section data to a potential energy surface has the potential to greatly simplify the analysis. With a well-parameterized PES, one can predict the outcome of future experiments and make novel observations that can help guide the further refinement of the PES. This is the first such an analysis of the 7Li+208Pb system and such a systematic exploration of the PES. The potential has 27 parameters that were fitted to a large data base of elastic scattering and fusion cross section data. We also perform a constrained global diagonalization of the coupled-channels Hamiltonian to determine excitation energies and residues. The resulting PES is very deep and has a global minimum close to the separatrix. The appearance of the deep PES could have important implications for the behavior of projectiles very close to heavy targets.

The causes of the First World War remain one of the most controversial historical topics, as such you'll probably find very few historians who actually agree on the details. Whilst there is a general consensus that Germany must take on a large portion of the blame, there is significant debate about how much, and what other factors were in play.
One of the major problems that we have when looking at the years leading up to the war, and the July crisis which lit the bonfire, so to speak, is the benefit of hindsight. We know the the war which happened due to these events was one of the most destructive in human history, and looking at the sequence of events as a whole, it's very easy to subscribe to the so-callled 'domino theory', that the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand was the first domino in a series of events which inevitably led to war. That is, once the first domino fell, war was inevitable. In order to truly understand the events and the reactions of the major powers to them, we have to try and imagine ourselves as being in the middle of them, without having a complete picture. 
Since you asked about historiography and scholarship, I'm assuming you know the basics about the beginning of the war so I'll not go into much detail about them. For those reading this that don't know so much, [this video](_URL_0_) by the BBC is a useful quick summary. 
One of the most significant works in the historiography of the First World War was Fritz Fischer's *Germany's Aims in the First World War*, which was published in 1961 and proved to be immensely controversial. Fischer proposed that instead of blundering into war, as most German historians liked to believe, the German Government had taken an explicitly aggressive path in their Foreign Policy in the deliberate hopes of provoking a general European war from which they could then gain territory, colonial concessions and economic reparations. Needless to say this was enormously controversial, both because it offended the patriotic pride of ordinary Germans, who saw Germany in the First World War as being forced into war by hostile nations surrounding it, and because it went against the traditional historiographical view. Whilst Fischer does make some valid points, one of his key points of evidence is somewhat questionable (I will come on to this later), and he subscribes to the *Sonderweg* thesis, which holds that due to the nature of German society, dating all the way back to the Reformation, it was inevitable that Germany should eventually end up as an aggressive state, culminating in Adolf Hitler. I have alternately heard this theory called 'not meriting academic study' and 'borderline racist', but it does still have some proponents. 
The second major school of thought with regards to the start of the war is the idea that Germany was forced into an 'offensively conducted defensive war'. This approach seeks to lessen the blame on Germany by looking at the foreign policy approaches of other nations, who could be accused of encircling Germany to the point where it felt that the only way it could relieve this pressure was to start a war with France and Russia and humiliate the two, relieving pressure on Germany. If you want to take it to the extreme level, some historians have suggested that Germany's very existence as a state in the geographic centre of Europe would lead it to inevitably come into conflict with other nations and so war was inevitable from the very creation of the state. However it has been pointed out that similar states such as Poland and Switzerland didn't behave in such a manner despite their similar geostrategic position.
The third major theory, and what appears to be the one that Kissinger lectures about, is that Europe 'sleepwalked' into war. I believe a commenter below me mentioned Christopher Clark's book *Sleepwalkers: How Europe went to war in 1914*. This theory suggests that the Governments of Europe, through a series of bad Foreign Policy decisions and continuing escalation of crises, ended up in a spiral from which they could not escape. The confused and changing nature of alliances, diplomacy and foreign policy across European governments seems to support this theory, however it can be criticised as somewhat oversimplifying the situation, and the idea that every single political and diplomatic figure in the major European governments was incompetent enough to let this happen I personally find slightly far fetched.
Some key books in the historiographical debate are:
Fritz Fischer *Germany's Aims in the First World War*
Christopher Clark *Sleepwalkers: How Europe went to war in 1914*
Paul Kennedy *The Rise of Anglo-German Antagonism*
Volker Berghahn *Germany and the Approach of War in 1914* 
Gregor Schoellgen *Escape into War? The Foreign Policy of Imperial Germany*
This is by no means an exhaustive list as you could fill a library with books about the start of WWI
In the next part of my post I'll go into a little more detail about the actual events surrounding the start of the war.

Francisco Gil Villegas (born 1951) is a renowned Mexican sociologist, economist, and academic. He is best known for his extensive research and contributions to the field of international political economy, particularly in the context of Latin America. His work has significantly influenced the understanding of economic development, globalization, and the role of the state in the global economy. Born in Mexico City, Gil Villegas completed his undergraduate studies in economics at the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM). He then pursued his master's degree in sociology at the London School of Economics and Political Science (LSE), followed by a doctorate in social sciences from the University of Paris. Gil Villegas has held numerous academic positions throughout his career. He has been a professor at the UNAM, the College of Mexico, and the Latin American Faculty of Social Sciences. He has also been a visiting professor at several international institutions, including the University of California, Berkeley, and the University of Cambridge. In addition to his academic roles, Gil Villegas has also served in various advisory capacities. He was a member of the Advisory Council of the United Nations Development Programme and has provided consultancy services to the World Bank and the Inter-American Development Bank. Gil Villegas has authored and co-authored several books and numerous articles in peer-reviewed journals. His most notable works include "The Political Economy of Globalization" and "Latin America in the World Economy". His research has been recognized with several awards, including the National Research Prize in Social Sciences from the Mexican Academy of Sciences. Throughout his career, Gil Villegas has been a strong advocate for the integration of sociological and economic perspectives in the study of global economic issues. His work has been instrumental in shaping the discourse on the political economy of Latin America and has provided valuable insights into the dynamics of economic development and globalization. Despite his numerous achievements, Gil Villegas remains a dedicated educator and researcher. He continues to contribute to the field of international political economy through his teaching, research, and public service. His work continues to inspire and influence scholars and policymakers around the world.

In the introduction to [The Literary Haunted House: Lovecraft, Matheson, King and the Horror in Between](_URL_3_), Rebecca Janicker sketches a history of the trope to British Gothic fiction at the end of the 18th century such as *The Castle of Otranto* (1764) and *The Old English Baron* (1778), although I'm not entirely happy with that because Janicker is tracing the *motif* of the "haunted house" rather than the physical description thereof, so is basically starting out with haunted castles—and while that may well be the beginning of the *trope* of haunted houses, it doesn't follow the specific form you're looking at. Even when you look at later culprits in early-19th century fictions such as "The Fall of the House of Usher" (1839), the house doesn't quite match the image. Which makes sense, if you think about it for a minute: tropes tend to follow the syntax of the era. Dale Bailey in [American Nightmares: The Haunted House Formula in American Popular Fiction](_URL_2_) does a great walkthrough of the literary tradition, but doesn't focus on the architecture as much - for him, a house is a house whether we're talking about *The Haunting of Hill House* (1959) or *The Shining* (1977).
So when we look at the "haunted house" as we popularly know it, the defining architectural traits are a bit of a guide...and it's more or less the Victorian era (1837-1901). Sarah Burns did a great [short documentary](_URL_4_) and a great essay, ["Better for Haunts: Victorian Houses and the Modern Imagination"](_URL_5_) about the very subject - and Burns paints the image of these Victorian houses of the American Gothic as very much fashionable to the nouveau riche in 1860s-1880s America, and then stylistically anathema by the 1930s and 40s - you can see them depicted as quaint and old in artwork like Charles Ephraim Burchard's [The House of Mystery](_URL_0_) (1924) and Edward Hopper's [House by the Railroad](_URL_1_) (1925) - and, perhaps more tellingly of Charles Addams' *The Addams Family* (1938).
As an aside, British horror fiction doesn't show quite this tendency; *The Haunted Woman* (1922) by David Lindsay for example still focuses on the old country manse, and the works of M. R. James likewise tend to focus on old churches, country houses, and the like, whether ruined or intact. 
By coincidence and necessity, the Victorian mansions shared certain traits with older houses - gables, for example, were a feature of many Victorian houses but also of many older American homes, especially in New England, so Nathaniel Hawthorne's *The House of the Seven Gables* (1851) and H. P. Lovecraft's "The Dreams in the Witch House" (1933) both describe houses with *elements* of the Victorian haunted house, but are actually referring to an older style of home, some of whose features were carried on or revived in the Victorian era.
The spread of the "haunted Victorian mansion" owed, of course, much to horror films like "The Haunted House" (1921), "The House of Mystery" (1934), "The House on Haunted Hill" (1959), etc. Another prominent example is the "Haunted Mansion" attraction at Disneyland, which began construction in 1961. I wish I could point to one of these as "the" defining moment, but as with a lot of pop culture icons, there's a process of agglutination - these examples, literary, artistic, cinematic, and physical helped to spread and cement the image in the popular imagination.

William Cullen Bryant (May 7, 1794 – July 17, 1878) was an American Romantic-era poet, journalist, editor, and playwright, widely considered to be the finest American poet of his time. His work brought notable achievements in American literature, including the use of colloquial language and the depiction of everyday people. Many of his poems are still frequently read today. Bryant was also a civic leader in New York City, where he held multiple positions, including the mayor of the New York City Borough of Greenwich Village. Born in Albany, New York, Bryant worked at various periods as a journalist for local newspapers, beginning in the Albany Patriot and later for The New York Post. He joined the National Arbiter, a periodical founded by Samuel Woodworth, and began publishing poetry, gaining critical acclaim for his work. Bryant's early poetry celebrated nature, and also demonstrated his familiarity with Enlightenment ideals. He also wrote patriotic verse during this time. After the success of "Thanatopsis", Bryant became known nationally, and achieved his greatest celebrity with the 1817 publication of Paul Reveille, or an Expostulation with Beheading Bonaparte. The poem—which attacks imperialism and slavery and ridicules Napoleon—became a rallying point for opposition to the French emperor, and helped launch his career. After the Panic of 1819, Bryant shifted many of his subject matters to the working class and urban life, and his poems gained popularity for their colloquial language. Bryant strove to achieve a synthesis of the beauties of Nature and the sublimity of the human spirit; his most popular poem, "To the Ophelia", is frequently recited at funerals. Bryant became the editor of the New York Evening Post in 1819 and held that position for 25 years. He also held several positions in New York's political establishment. Bryant was an outspoken opponent of slavery, and his poems are frequently amended to reflect this stance. He was friends with several prominent abolitionists, including William Henry Seward, Lewis Tappan, and Gerrit Smith. He published a variety of works, including Tales of the Alhambra and The Journals of Adam Cooper. His final book, collected editions of his poems were published in 1859 and 1867. Bryant is buried at Green-Wood Cemetery in Brooklyn. His works were controversial during his lifetime, but he has been acknowledged as one of the foremost writers in the United States, along with Oliver Wendell Holmes and Henry Wadsworth Longfellow. Bryant's sister, Margaretta Gertrude Bryant, was also a writer; her biography of her brother was published in 1883.

This paper proposes a method for significantly increasing the number of parameters in a single layer while keeping computation in par with (or even less than) current SOTA models. The idea is based on using a large mixture of experts (MoE) (i.e. small networks), where only a few of them are adaptively activated via a gating network. While the idea seems intuitive, the main novelty in the paper is in designing the gating network which is encouraged to achieve two objectives: utilizing all available experts (aka importance), and distributing computation fairly across them (aka load). 
Additionally, the paper introduces two techniques for increasing the batch-size passed to each expert, and hence maximizing parallelization in GPUs.
Experiments applying the proposed approach on RNNs in language modelling task show that it can beat SOTA results with significantly less computation, which is a result of selectively using much more parameters. Results on machine translation show that a model with more than 30x number of parameters can beat SOTA while incurring half of the effective computation.
I have the several comments on the paper:
- I believe that the authors can do a better job in their presentation. The paper currently is at 11 pages (which is too long in my opinion), but I find that Section 3.2 (the crux of the paper) needs better motivation and intuitive explanation. For example, equation 8 deserves more description than currently devoted to it. Additional space can be easily regained by moving details in the experiments section (e.g. architecture and training details) to the appendix for the curious readers. Experiment section can be better organized by finishing on experiment completely before moving to the other one. There are also some glitches in the writing, e.g. the end of Section 3.1.
