Often, making a difficult decision is instrumental to a successful outcome. By seeking advice from others, you not only gain new perspectives on a problem, but you also gain a better understanding of different solutions and their implications. Inevitably, making a difficult decision without the advice of others can produce less than desirable outcomes. Consider, for example, the global financial crisis that began in 2007. Despite the hindsight provided by today, there were no clear warning signs that the global economy was starting to weaken. Without the input of experts in the fields of economics and finance, the Federal Reserve and other global financial institutions made the decision to leave monetary policy untouched, which in turn fueled the initial acceleration of the recession. Had the government acted more swiftly and increased regulations on mortgage lending, the severity of the crisis could have been mitigated. Another example of an important decision made without advice from others is the selection of a new President of the United States. During the 2016 US presidential election, Hilary Clinton and Donald Trump were the two candidates favored to win. In the days leading up to the election, both campaigns tried to sway public opinion through interviews and personal appearances. Despite these efforts, many voters remained undecided. Ultimately, the election was decided by the very slim margin of victory for Hilary Clinton over Donald Trump. Had Hillary Clinton’s campaign taken advice from others, she may have been better able to predict and prepare for a potential loss, and may have been better positioned to encourage undecided voters to support her. Ultimately, seeking advice from others can lead to a better outcome by gaining a more in-depth understanding of different viewpoints and alternative solutions, and by providing an unbiased assessment of your own decision-making.

Middle English is a language that evolved from Old English, so it is natural to expect that speakers of Middle English would have an easier time understanding Old English texts than those who do not speak Old English. However, the degree of difficulty in understanding Old English texts can vary depending on several factors, including the specific dialect of Old English being used and the level of education and literacy of the speaker.
For example, a speaker of Middle English who is also educated in Old English literature and language may have an easier time understanding Old English texts than someone who is not familiar with Old English. On the other hand, a speaker of Middle English who is not educated in Old English may have a harder time understanding Old English texts, even if they are familiar with some of the vocabulary and grammar.
In general, speakers of Middle English would have a better understanding of Old English than speakers of modern English, but the level of understanding would still vary depending on the individual's education and familiarity with Old English.
Here is an example of a passage from Beowulf in Old English and Middle English:
Old English: "Thu waes on heofenum aetcyndes, þaet is gefyldelic gearu, oþþ þu waes gefyldelica hæleða. þu waes se mægna þeah þu waes ylda, þu waes Drihtenes Sunu, aethelum fæderum. þu wæs æghwaes god, þu wæs gefyldelica gæst, þu wæs gefyldelica hæleða, þu wæs gefyldelica wælfræetha, þu wæs gefyldelica drihten, þu wæs gefyldelica god."
Middle English: "Thou wert on hevene a lively creature, that is a blessed thing, and thou wert a lively leach, the son of God, of a lively father. Thou wert ever good, and thou wert a blessed ghost, a lively leach, a lively warrior, a lively king, a lively god."
As you can see, the Old English passage is more difficult to understand than the Middle English passage, as it uses more archaic vocabulary and grammar. However, a speaker of Middle English who is educated in Old English literature and language would likely have an easier time understanding the Old English passage than someone who is not familiar with Old English.

The Greeks for the most part did not use our modern algebraic notation, which is much newer. For much of the history of mathematics, an equation we write as x^2 - 7x - 2 = 0 would be something like "Determine the quantity whose square is equal to seven of itself and two units." (Notice the avoidance of negative numbers: They would have first rewritten this as x^2 = 7x +2.) Diophantus' *Arithmetic*, a work on what we would call finding integer solutions of polynomial equations, had some symbolic notation for these equations, but it was pretty limited compared to modern notation.
Despite this, in some ways the *Elements* looks very much like a modern geometry textbook: there are diagrams with points labelled by letters, lines called AB and angles ABC (or ABΓ) and statements and proofs of theorems. The difference is that there is no symbolic algebraic notation. There are also no coordinates; points are not represented by pairs (or triples) of numbers. While we might state the Pythagorean theorem as "the squared distance from the origin to the point (a,b) is a^2 + b^(2)", Euclid would say that the sum of the (areas of the) squares on the two shorter sides is equal to the square on the hypotenuse. (Compare the diagram [here](_URL_0_).) Things like the Law of Cosines get more complicated to state without algebra, but it's still possible.
A lot of this came down to the Greek conception of numbers. While we freely talk about not only rational (2/3) but also irrational (the square root of 3) and even transcendental numbers (π, e) in elementary math education, you'll notice that there isn't a good, general definition of what a real number *is*. This is because the definition is actually pretty tricky, and because it only becomes really important when you start doing rigorous calculus, which is something normally only taught to mathematics majors.
The Greek mathematicians were quite concerned about rigorous proofs and definitions, so they stuck to what they knew. They were ok with whole numbers (1,2,3...), and they were ok with length and areas, but generally speaking those were different things. They would not say "Line AB has a length of 2," but instead "Line CD is twice the length of AB." This idea is retained somewhat in expressions like "Line AB has a length of 2 units;" the "unit" is some fixed length we are using for comparison.
Similarly, the Greek mathematicians did not prove that the square root of two is an irrational number, they proved that the ratio of the diagonal of a square to its side is not expressible as a ratio of whole numbers. The point of stating things this way is that you don't have to worry about what an "irrational number" is, but instead rely on geometric concepts like length. Another statement is "the ratio of the area of a circle to the square on its radius is equal to the ratio of a circle to its diameter," which we write as area = πr^(2). π was defined to be the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter, and it was a theorem that this was the same for all circles.
You shouldn't think of this as "primitive" or "inferior:" the Greek mathematicians had a rigorous concept of quantity that worked for their purposes. The *Elements* has sections defining exactly what it means for lengths or proportions to the same that closely resemble what we now call Dedekind cuts, which are a way of constructing real numbers. Archimedes even figured out how to approximate irrational ratios like π using rational ones, by using inscribed and circumscribed polygons.
Nonetheless, there were some problems with this approach. One objection to early algebraic notation in early modern Europe was drawn from Greek ideas about quantity: things like x^2 + x don't make sense, because you're adding an area to a length. Physics students might recognize this as a dimensionality issue, and the solution was similar to the way we deal with it, namely an implicit or explicit coefficient for x with units of length. There were a number of related conceptual issues that had to be worked out to make sense of algebraic notation.
Sources: *The History of the Calculus* by Carl B. Boyer. This was the textbook for an undergraduate mathematics class I took on the history of calculus, but I've now realized it's probably rather dated. If a real expert shows up I defer to them.

The paper proposes a convolutional neural network approach to model the
coherence of texts. The model is based on the well-known entity grid
representation for coherence, but puts a CNN on top of it. 
The approach is well motivated and described, I especially appreciate the clear
discussion of the intuitions behind certain design decisions (e.g. why CNN and
the section titled 'Why it works').
There is an extensive evaluation on several tasks, which shows that the
proposed approach beats previous methods. It is however strange that one
previous result could not be reproduced: the results on Li/Hovy (2014) suggest
an implementation or modelling error that should be addressed.
Still, the model is a relatively simple 'neuralization' of the entity grid
model. I didn't understand why 100-dimensional vectors are necessary to
represent a four-dimensional grid entry (or a few more in the case of the
extended grid). How does this help? I can see that optimizing directly for
coherence ranking would help learn a better model, but the difference of
transition chains for up to k=3 sentences vs. k=6 might not make such a big
difference, especially since many WSJ articles may be very short.
The writing seemed a bit lengthy, the paper repeats certain parts in several
places, for example the introduction to entity grids. In particular, section 2
also presents related work, thus the first 2/3 of section 6 are a repetition
and should be deleted (or worked into section 2 where necessary). The rest of
section 6 should probably be added in section 2 under a subsection (then rename
section 2 as related work).
Overall this seems like a solid implementation of applying a neural network
model to entity-grid-based coherence. But considering the proposed
consolidation of the previous work, I would expect a bit more from a full
paper, such as innovations in the representations (other features?) or tasks.
minor points:
- this paper may benefit from proof-reading by a native speaker: there are
articles missing in many places, e.g. '_the_ WSJ corpus' (2x), '_the_ Brown...
toolkit' (2x), etc.
- p.1 bottom left column: 'Figure 2' -> 'Figure 1'
- p.1 Firstly/Secondly -> First, Second
- p.1 'limits the model to' -> 'prevents the model from considering...'?
- Consider removing the 'standard' final paragraph in section 1, since it is
not necessary to follow such a short paper.

The paper Rule Mining in Feature Space proposes a novel approach to rule mining that operates directly in the feature space. This is an interesting and potentially useful approach, as it bypasses the need to first transform data into a boolean format.
One potential issue with this approach is that it may be less efficient than traditional rule mining algorithms that operate on boolean data. This is because the proposed approach requires more complex boolean operations, which could potentially be slower. However, the paper does show that the proposed approach is competitive with traditional rule mining algorithms in terms of both time and space complexity.
Overall, the paper Rule Mining in Feature Space presents an interesting new approach to rule mining that has potential to be competitive with traditional rule mining algorithms.

Identity theft is a serious crime that can cause significant financial and emotional distress. Here are some steps you can take to protect yourself and avoid becoming a victim. 1. Regularly request your free credit report from TransUnion, Experian or Equifax. It's recommended to do this at least once a year, but preferably every six months. This will allow you to monitor any suspicious activity on your accounts. 2. Place an “Initial Fraud Alert” on all three of your credit reports. This lasts for 90 days and can help protect your information from being misused. 3. If you want extended protection, you can obtain a 7-year fraud alert. This requires writing to each of the credit companies, providing the initial fraud alert confirmation number and some additional information. 4. Make it a habit to personally read through your financial report. Ensure that all loan and credit card requests are from you and not an unknown entity. 5. If you notice a missing financial statement or bill, contact your financial institution immediately. It could have been stolen. 6. Don't rely solely on paper statements. Visit your bank to check your accounts in person. 7. Establish a personal security code with your banks and credit card institutions. This adds an extra layer of security to your accounts. 8. Pick up new checks from the bank instead of having them mailed to you. This reduces the risk of them being stolen. 9. Never have your Social Security Number printed on your checks. This is a common way for thieves to steal your identity. 10. Promptly notify your credit institutions of any change of address. This avoids confusion and ensures your statements are sent to the correct location. 11. Be cautious with emails. Even if an email looks legitimate, close it and contact the business or organization directly. Never give out your private information over the phone unless you initiated the call. 12. Be reluctant to give out personal information, especially your social security number, mother’s maiden name, and date of birth. 13. Don't carry your social security card or pin number in your wallet. If you lose your wallet, immediately cancel your credit cards. 14. Regularly clean out your computer hard drive and use reliable software to detect and remove spyware. 15. Limit the number of credit cards you use. It's easier to monitor two cards than several. 16. Be aware of your surroundings when using an ATM. Make sure no one is looking over your shoulder and that there are no additional devices attached to the machine. 17. Secure your mail. Always send and receive mail through a secure and locked mailbox. 18. Keep sensitive documents in a safe and secure place. This includes ID, passport, and financial accounts. 19. Shred private receipts, junk mail, and CDs before throwing them away. 20. If you suspect something, question the people who have access to your personal information. 21. Consider hiring a reputable Identity Theft Protection Company, such as LoudSiren, LifeLock or TrustedID, if you don't have the time or knowledge to take these steps. By following these steps, you can significantly reduce your risk of becoming a victim of identity theft.

Although a popular vote is generally preferred, there are certain advantages to using the Electoral College. One important aspect is the presence of electors in each state that are not directly elected by the citizens. These electors are bound to cast their votes in favor of the candidate who won the popular vote in their state. This means that even if a highly populated state goes to one candidate, the candidate with the most votes still has the majority of electoral votes, allowing him or her to win the presidency. Additionally, the Electoral College protects state boundaries and respects regional differences, ensuring that each state is equally represented. This ensures a balanced system that does not favor any region of the country or any group of people. Another advantage of the Electoral College is the protection of smaller states. Since each state has equal representation, large population states do not have the same power as small states. Finally, the Electoral College has existed since the Constitution was established and is well-established. Thus, any changes should be easy to implement and do not cause any unexpected repercussions. Overall, the Electoral College is a more equitable, balanced, and mature voting system than the popular vote. Therefore, I strongly advocate that the Electoral College should continue to be used for electing the President of the United States. The Electoral College offers a number of advantages over the popular vote. First, it preserves state boundaries and protects smaller states. Large populous states don’t disproportionately influence the outcome the way they do with the popular vote system. Additionally, this allows for more balanced power distribution among the states. Second, the Electoral College ensures that each state is equally represented. This provides balance and prevents any one region from being overrepresented or underrepresented in the government. Third, the Electoral College provides a way to protect minority interests. If a state has a very popular opinion or perspective, they should be able to have a voice in the election through their electoral votes.

Before the invention of glasses, people with poor vision indeed had to deal with it. However, it's important to understand that the need for glasses is not solely due to genetic factors, but also environmental factors and age. For example, reading small print and staring at screens, which are common activities in modern society, can strain our eyes and potentially worsen our vision over time. These activities were not prevalent in our evolutionary past. In terms of evolution, natural selection favors traits that increase an individual's chances of survival and reproduction. In the past, having perfect vision might not have been as critical for survival as other traits. For instance, being able to see a predator from far away is certainly beneficial, but being able to run fast, having strength to fight, or being smart enough to create safe shelters could have been more important. Moreover, many vision problems do not appear until later in life, after the prime reproductive years. Therefore, these traits would not have been heavily selected against because they did not significantly impact an individual's ability to pass on their genes. It's also worth noting that the human eye has evolved quite a bit over time. Our ancestors did not have the same visual capabilities that we do. For example, early mammals were likely nocturnal and had eyes adapted for low light conditions. As humans evolved, our eyes adapted to our changing lifestyles and environments. In conclusion, while vision has certainly been a factor in human evolution, it's not the only factor, and the need for glasses today is influenced by a combination of genetic, environmental, and lifestyle factors. There's no clear evidence to suggest that human vision has been getting progressively better or worse over time.

In general, computer and video games rely on Window's operating system to manage their open applications. When a computer game, for example, needs to open an application that is not already open, it first checks to see if the application is open by another user. If so, the game will open and use the existing instance of the application. If not, it will create a new instance of the application. Computer games generally do not allow two instances of the same application to exist. If two games try to open a spreadsheet, for example, and both spreadsheet applications are open, the computer will display an error message. Games that open new windows and applications quickly are often intentionally designed to run on low-end systems with slower hardware. These games use minimal graphical effects and do not create new windows and applications when the user clicks buttons. This is efficient because the game is not competing with other applications and windows for system resources. On the other hand, games that open new windows and applications slowly or that crash when a user clicks a button are likely to be highly graphical and require more system resources to run. The game is competing with other applications and windows for system resources. Most games are optimized to run well on systems with moderate configuration. The games are generally not written with lower-end systems in mind. So, why do some computer games alt-tab quickly and others crash or take a long time to re-open? The answer lies in the design of the game. Some games are designed for low-end systems. When a user alt-tabs out of the game, the game leaves open windows and applications. When the game tries to open those windows or applications, they are already open and the game must either wait or crash. Games that open windows and applications quickly are optimized to close and reopen those windows and applications quickly. Games that open windows and applications slowly or that crash when a user clicks a button are likely to be poorly optimized.

Dopamine is a neurotransmitter that plays a crucial role in how we feel pleasure and reward. It's also involved in other functions such as movement, memory, mood, and learning. In theory, it is possible to artificially increase dopamine levels in the brain using certain medications or drugs. However, it's not as simple as making someone feel rewarded for something they don't like. Firstly, dopamine doesn't work in isolation. It interacts with other neurotransmitters and systems in the brain, and its effects can vary depending on the specific brain regions it acts upon. For example, dopamine released in the prefrontal cortex has been linked to cognitive functions like attention and decision-making, while dopamine released in the nucleus accumbens is more associated with pleasure and reward. Secondly, artificially manipulating dopamine levels can have unintended side effects. For instance, drugs that increase dopamine, such as amphetamines, can lead to addiction, as they can cause a temporary feeling of euphoria followed by a "crash" that leaves the person feeling low and craving more of the drug. Long-term use of these drugs can also lead to tolerance (needing more of the drug to get the same effect) and dependence. Thirdly, making someone feel rewarded for something they don't like would likely involve more than just increasing dopamine. It would probably also require changing their thoughts, beliefs, and attitudes towards that thing, which is a complex process that can't be achieved simply by altering brain chemistry. In conclusion, while it's theoretically possible to artificially increase dopamine levels in the brain, doing so is unlikely to make someone feel rewarded for something they don't like, at least not in a sustainable or healthy way. It's also important to note that such manipulations could have serious side effects and ethical implications.

Cell Phones Are Not Allowed In Our School Cell phones are not allowed at our school because they can distract students from their studies. Students may be tempted by text messages that pop up during class time instead of paying attention to what is being taught. They could also play games while taking notes which would make it difficult for them to remember important information later when studying for tests. Some people think allowing cell phones into the classroom will help teachers communicate more effectively with parents if there was something wrong with one student's behavior. However, this argument does not hold much weight since most teachers already have other ways to contact students' families such as emailing or calling home directly. Another reason why we do not allow cell phones at our school is due to safety reasons. Tajima Chōyū (田島長勇) is a renowned Japanese calligrapher and educator, known for his significant contributions to the art of calligraphy in Japan and worldwide. Born on November 12, 1948, in Hiroshima, Japan, Tajima has dedicated his life to the study and practice of calligraphy, a traditional form of Japanese art that involves writing characters with a brush and ink. Tajima began his calligraphy journey at a young age, under the tutelage of his father, who was also a calligrapher. He later attended the prestigious Tokyo University of the Arts, where he honed his skills and deepened his understanding of the art form. After graduating, Tajima began teaching calligraphy, sharing his knowledge and passion with students of all ages. Tajima's style is characterized by its fluidity and grace, with each stroke of his brush reflecting a deep understanding of the character's meaning and history. His works are often described as a perfect blend of tradition and innovation, as he seamlessly incorporates modern elements into the ancient art form. His calligraphy has been exhibited in numerous galleries and museums around the world, and he has received several awards for his work, including the prestigious Japan Calligraphy Art Academy Award. In addition to his work as a calligrapher, Tajima has also made significant contributions to the field of calligraphy education. He has written several books on the subject, including "The Art of Japanese Calligraphy" and "Calligraphy: A Comprehensive Guide". These books have been translated into multiple languages and are used as textbooks in calligraphy classes worldwide. Tajima has also been instrumental in promoting calligraphy outside of Japan. He has conducted workshops and demonstrations in various countries, including the United States, China, and France. His efforts have helped to increase global awareness and appreciation of Japanese calligraphy. Tajima Chōyū's dedication to the art of calligraphy and his contributions to its education and promotion have made him a respected figure in the field. His work continues to inspire and influence calligraphers around the world, cementing his legacy as one of the most influential calligraphers of his generation.

Online education has become increasingly popular over recent years. It is now possible for students who are unable or unwilling to attend class at their local university to take courses from other universities around the world.
Advantages
One major advantage that comes along with online education is convenience. Students can complete coursework wherever they have access to internet service without having to travel long distances. They do not need to leave work early just so they can get home before it gets dark; nor do they have to worry about finding childcare if they want to go back to school while raising children. 
Another benefit associated with online education is cost savings. Online schools often offer tuition rates lower than those charged by brick-and-mortar institutions because there aren't as many administrative costs involved (e.g., rent). This means more money left over after graduation which could be used towards paying off student loans. In addition, some online programs may allow you to transfer credits earned elsewhere into your degree program thereby reducing overall time spent earning a bachelor's or master's degree. 
Finally, online education offers greater flexibility when it comes to scheduling. You don't have to wait until Monday morning to start working through assigned reading materials; instead, you can begin studying whenever it's convenient for...

The authors present an interesting approach to combine two popular reinforcement learning algorithms, namely Policy Gradient (PG) with Deep-Q Learning(DQL). The main idea is that PG can be used as a critic in DQN which leads to faster convergence of training process.
However there are some issues I would like to point out. 
First issue concerns about how to use PG algorithm efficiently when it comes to continuous action space problems such as robotics control or autonomous driving. In this case we need to define a good representation of actions so that they could be easily learned by neural network model. This problem has been studied extensively but still remains unsolved. 
Second one is related to exploration strategy adopted in their work. They adopt -greedy method where agent chooses random action from all possible ones at each step during training phase. However if you look closely into Figure 1, you will find that most of time agents choose same action repeatedly even though they have different policies. It seems that -greedy does not help much on exploring new state-action pairs compared to other methods e.g., Softmax based Exploration Strategy

This dance is traditionally done with everyone standing in a circle, with enough space between each person so that people can move around without bumping into one another. You should have about an arm's length between you and the people on either side of you.
However, if you're performing the dance for an audience, then the dancers will all stand facing the front, in multiple staggered rows if necessary, so everyone can see your moves.;
As the first lyrics go, "You put your right foot in." Simply place that right foot out in front of you and let it hang a few inches from the floor or just move it out in front of you and point your foot on the floor. You can keep your hands on your hips or at your sides. Most people like to sing the song along with doing the motions, so you can join the singing! This can get your energy up and make it easier to remember what moves to do next.
As the next lyrics go, "You put your right foot out." Just take that foot and move it back to its starting position, next to your other foot.
As the next words go, "You put your right foot in and you shake it all about." Now, just put that right foot in again and shake it back and forth, up and down, or however you'd like to shake it. Just make sure to keep your balance!
As the next lyrics go, "You do the hokey pokey and you turn yourself around..." Now, you can just place each arm out and up at your sides at a ninety degree angle, point the fingers of both hands and wiggle them up and down as you turn yourself around. This only takes a few seconds, so make sure to keep up with your fellow dancers!
After you're done turning yourself around, just clap once or twice at these lyrics. For a variation, you can clap your hands down on your knees instead.
Congratulations! You've done one full cycle of the hokey pokey—now, you'll repeat these same motions with different parts of your body until the song is over.
Follow the lyrics, "You put your left foot in." Now, place your left foot in, a few feet out in front of you. You can either point it or let it hover a few feet above the ground; repeat whatever you did with your left foot.
Follow the lyrics, "You put your left foot out." Now, just move your left foot back to its original position.
Follow the lyrics, "You put your left foot in and you shake it all about." Now, just put your left foot back out in front of you and shake it around as much as you want!
Follow the words, "You do the hokey pokey and you turn yourself around." Simply do the hokey pokey dance again and turn around in a circle, so that you're facing forward again once the words are over.
After you're done turning yourself around, just clap once or twice at these lyrics. For a variation, you can clap your hands down on your knees instead.
As the first words to the song go, "You put your right hand in..." Just dip down a bit and place your right hand in to the center of the circle or out in front of you. You can leave your other arm at your side or keep it up on your hip, depending on what you and the group decide. It's that simple.
As the song goes, "You put your right hand out..." Now, take that right hand and place it back at your side or out at your side. Just exaggerate the gesture a bit. Again, keep your left hand either at your side or on your hip, depending on where it was before.
As the song goes, "You put your right hand in..." Just do exactly what you did before with your right hand.
Next, the song says, "And you shake it all about." This just means you shake that hand up and down, from side to side, or however you want to shake it. You can get a little crazy and really shake it up and down, depending on your energy level!
Follow the words, "You do the hokey pokey and you turn yourself around" and do the hokey pokey and turn around again.
After you're done turning yourself around, just clap once or twice at these lyrics. For a variation, you can clap your hands down on your knees instead.
Follow the words, "You put your left hand in." Place your left hand out in front of you.
Follow the words, "You put your left hand out." Just move your left hand back "in" and either return it to its original position or let it hover above your left side a bit.
Follow the words, "You put your left hand in and you shake it all about." Simply place that hand back in and shake it however much you want to.
Follow the words, "You do the hokey pokey and you turn yourself around" and do the hokey pokey and turn around again.
After you're done turning yourself around, just clap once or twice at these lyrics. For a variation, you can clap your hands down on your knees instead.
Follow the words, "You put your whole self in," and either jump around a foot into the center of the circle or lean your body forward. This is the final "body part" that you will move during the hokey pokey, no matter what variations may come before it!
Follow the words, "You put your whole self out." Now, just either jump back to your original position or lean back to your starting position, depending on what you did to "put your whole self in."
Follow the words, "You put your whole self in and you shake it all about." Just move your body around from side to side, moving your hands up and down at your sides. You can lower and raise your body, shimmy, point your fingers, or really do anything you'd like to do in order to "shake it all about" once last time!
Follow the words, "You do the hokey pokey and you turn yourself around" and do the hokey pokey and turn around again.
There are some variations of the end of this dance that repeat the words "That's what it's all about" an extra time for a grand finale.
After you're done turning yourself around, just clap once or twice at these lyrics. For a variation, you can clap your hands down on your knees instead. Congratulations—you're all done! Repeat as many times as you'd like until the fun wears off!
If you really want to be an expert at the Hokey Pokey, then you should know the song before you begin the dance. This will help you get oriented with what to do next with your body, and it will also just help you have more fun when you're doing the dance! Though the words change slightly depending on where and with whom you're singing them, here's what you should know:
You put your right foot in
You put your right foot out
You put your right foot in
And you shake it all about
You do the hokey pokey
And you turn yourself around
That's what it's all about!
You put your left foot in
You put your left foot out
You put your left foot in
And you shake it all about
You do the hokey pokey
And you turn yourself around
That's what it's all about!
You put your right hand in
You put your right hand out
You put your right hand in
And you shake it all about
You do the hokey pokey
And you turn yourself around
That's what it's all about!
You put your left hand in
You put your left hand out
You put your left hand in
And you shake it all about
You do the hokey pokey
And you turn yourself around
That's what it's all about!
You put your whole self in
You put your whole self out
You put your whole self in
And you shake it all about
You do the hokey pokey
And you turn yourself around
That's what it's all about!
Though the version here only uses the arms, feet, and whole body, you can add as many variations to this dance as you'd like, as long as you still end with the whole body. Once you finish the arms and legs, you can also add other body parts, such as putting the nose, shoulders, knees, elbows, or even your bottom into the mix!
You should also note that, in some versions of the song, the arms go in before the feet. It's all up to you!
In some versions of the song, the lyrics for the lines that say, "You put your out," actually go, "You take your out," instead.

April 1, 1954 - The April Fool's Day Massacre is released in the United States.
The plot revolves around two men who are trying to commit suicide by jumping off of a bridge into San Francisco Bay and then decide that they would rather kill someone else instead so as not to be left behind on their own. 
 
 This movie was directed by George Marshall

In this work, we present an algorithm for the analysis and comparison of profile Hidden Markov Models (HMMs). The proposed method is based on the concept of Structural Similarity Index Measurement (SSIM) which has been widely used in image processing to measure similarity between two images. We use SSIM as a distance metric to compare HMMs by measuring their similarities at different levels of granularity. In addition, our approach allows us to identify regions that are responsible for differences among models. Our experiments show that the proposed method can be effectively applied to analyze and compare HMMs generated using various algorithms such as PSI-BLAST or SAM-T2K. This research was supported by NSERC Discovery Grant RGPIN-2014-04567 awarded to Dr. Yi-Chun Wu. Profile hidden markov model(HMM),Structural similarity index measurement(SSIM),Similarity score matrix(SCM),PSI-BLAST

This article presents a comprehensive study on the high redshift neutral hydrogen fog, a critical component in understanding the early universe. We utilize advanced computational models and observational data to investigate the properties and distribution of this fog. Our analysis reveals significant insights into the reionization epoch and the role of neutral hydrogen during this period. We also discuss the challenges in observing this fog due to its high redshift nature and propose potential solutions to overcome these limitations. Our findings contribute to the ongoing discourse on the evolution of the universe and provide a foundation for future research in this field.

We show individual high resolution spectra of components A, B, and C of thenearby late-M type multiple system LHS 1070. Component A is a mid-M star, B andC are known to have masses at the threshold to brown dwarfs. From our spectrawe measure rotation velocities and the mean magnetic field for all threecomponents individually. We find magnetic flux on the order of severalkilo-Gauss in all components. The rotation velocities of the two late-M objectsB and C are similar (vsini = 16km/s), the earlier A component is spinning onlyat about half that rate. This suggests weakening of net rotational braking atlate-M spectral type, and that the lack of slowly rotating late-M and L dwarfsis real. Furthermore, we found that magnetic flux in the B component is abouttwice as strong as in component C at similar rotation rate. This indicates thatrotational braking is not proportional to magnetic field strength in fullyconvective objects, and that a different field topology is the reason for theweak braking in low mass objects.

The authors present their work very clearly. They have done extensive experiments showing how well they can perform across several different datasets.
I think this article will be useful for anyone interested in learning about deep learning methods applied to computer vision problems as it provides some new insights into how these techniques might be used more effectively going forward. Strengths: I like the way the authors describe their approach throughout the text which makes understanding easier even if you don't know much about machine learning algorithms yet. 
Weaknesses: 
There isn't really anything wrong but there could perhaps be additional discussion around why the proposed solution works so well when combined with CNN features. 
Overall: 4 stars

Mandatory community service should not be required of students. This proposal sounds like a reasonable solution to the problems that it raises, but in reality, it creates many more problems than it solves. First, there is a concern that students who do not do community service will receive a lower GPA. However, it could be that some students decide not to do community service in order to cheat, and then receive a higher GPA as a result. Another issue is that students may not be able to participate in community service for a number of reasons, such as scheduling conflicts or getting sick. Then there are the issues of who determines what types of community service the student has to perform. Finally, there is the concern that unqualified students are performing the service. This could lead to students doing unqualified work, and providing poor quality service. Because of these problems, mandatory community service should not be required of students. The drawbacks of mandatory community service for students include: -Unqualified students could be performing the service. -Students who do not do community service will receive a lower GPA. -Some students could cheat to receive a higher GPA by not doing community service. -Scheduling conflicts and getting sick could prevent students from performing community service. -Some students feel that they do not have the skills to perform certain types of community service. -A system should be implemented to determine what community service the students have to perform, and unqualified students might perform the service.

Charles William Dyson Perrins FRAS (25 May 1864 – 29 January 1958) was an English businessman, bibliophile and philanthropist. He was born in Claines, near Worcester, the son of James Dyson Perrins, the owner of the Lea & Perrins Worcestershire sauce factory and the grandson of William Perrins, co-originator of the Lea & Perrins secret recipe. He was educated at Charterhouse School and The Queen's College, Oxford, and then served in the Highland Light Infantry. After the death of his father, he took over management of Lea & Perrins. His father had also been a director of Royal Worcester Porcelain Factory, and Charles followed him, becoming a director in 1891. He became chairman in 1901, and supported the factory financially. He bought the company outright in 1934 and ensured its continuity from his own fortune, until it could be taken public in 1954. In 1927, he purchased the Royal Worcester Porcelain Factory's historic ceramics collection for a price above market value, to assist the firm's cashflow. In 1946, he established the Perrins Trust to unite the factory museum collection and his own private holdings of Royal Worcester and ensure their survival. After his death, his widow established the "Dyson Perrins Museum" at the factory site to house the collection. It is now called "The Museum of Royal Worcester" (previously "Worcester Porcelain Museum"). Perrins lived in Malvern for most of his life and amongst his many charitable deeds in Worcestershire was the endowment of Dyson Perrins Church of England Academy in Malvern. He served as Mayor of Worcester for 1897–98 and as High Sheriff of Worcestershire for 1899–1900. He was a major benefactor of the Royal Grammar School, Worcester, and endowed new buildings including Perrins Hall (1914) named after his father, an old boy. The school organ is in this building and is played regularly at assemblies. He became a Six Master and chairman of the governors of that school until the 1950s. His portrait by A. Hacker c. 1907 hangs in the hall. In Oxford, he funded the building of the Dyson Perrins Laboratory, which was the main centre for research into organic chemistry at Oxford University from its foundation in 1916 until its retirement as a laboratory in 2003. He received an honorary DCL from Oxford University. During Perrins's life he amassed one of the most important book collections in the world, particularly strong in medieval illuminated manuscripts and printed ballads. To help finance and re-establish the Royal Worcester factory after World War II, he decided to sell his important collection of early printed books, and they were mostly dispersed in a series of sales at Sotheby's in London in 1946 and 1947. His illuminated manuscripts and other remaining printed books were sold after his death in three major auction sales in 1958 to 1960. A record total for a single collection of £1,100,000 was raised by these sales. Items once owned by C. W. Dyson Perrins now form the basis of many other prominent collections, such as the Lessing J. Rosenwald Collection now in the Library of Congress. Many other items from his collection were given or bequeathed by him to public institutions such as the Victoria and Albert Museum, the National Gallery, the Ashmolean Museum, Winchester Cathedral library, and the British Museum. For example, his Mughal manuscript the Emperor Akbar's Khamsa of Nizami is now in the British Library and Palestrina by J. M. W. Turner is in the National Gallery. Catherine Christina Gregory at St Giles' Cathedral, Edinburgh in October 1889. They had five children. Catherine died at Davenham, the family home, on 31 January 1922, and was buried at the Municipal cemetery, Great Malvern. Florence Winifred Midwood Milne (known as Frieda) at St John, Chelsea on 6 September 1923. She died on 28 December 1968. Charles William Dyson Perrins died at Davenham on 29 January 1958, and was buried at Great Malvern.

Communism is a political and economic ideology that aims to create a classless, stateless society where resources are shared equally among all members. In many ways, it is a response to the perceived inequalities and injustices of capitalism, which is characterized by the private ownership of the means of production and the accumulation of wealth by a small, elite class.
Communism gained a significant following in largely agrarian countries that barely had capitalism because these countries were more vulnerable to the negative effects of capitalism, such as poverty, inequality, and exploitation. In these countries, many people were already living in poverty and were thus more likely to support an ideology that promised to address these issues. Additionally, the lack of developed industries in these countries made them more susceptible to the influence of communist leaders who could easily gain control of the means of production and distribution.
In contrast, industrialized and developed countries that had already experienced the full effects of capitalism were less likely to support communism. These countries had already established democratic systems and had a large middle class that was generally content with the status quo. Additionally, the influence of powerful industries and businesses in these countries made it more difficult for communist leaders to gain control of the means of production and distribution.
Overall, the historical and economic conditions of a country can significantly influence the adoption of communist ideology. While communism gained a strong following in some agrarian countries that barely had capitalism, it was less successful in industrialized and developed countries where the effects of capitalism were more visible and the political and economic systems were already established.

Children from 6 to 15 months of age often cry and refuse to go to anyone other than a parent - referred to as "stranger anxiety”.So when a young child wakes in the middle of the night, they will automatically search the room for a parent as they are feeling vulnerable and alone.
This creates a cycle where the child cries if they are alone in their room and cannot locate a parent. If you then go and check on your child, you now have to deal with an awake and crying child.Keep in mind there is nothing wrong with parents who cannot get their child to sleep through the night in their own bed. Sleep is a flexible behavior, and it is very normal for children to look for their parents if they wake up at night.;
There are many benefits to weaning your child from your bed, as it teaches your child about boundaries, it allow them to gain their own space, and it nurtures their self-confidence and independence.Young children are not born with self control, it is something they have to learn from their parents. Remember that it is okay and normal for children to cry. In fact, it is often unavoidable if they are going to learn to control their behavior.
As a parent, it's important for you to be firm and persistent about self control in your child, especially when it comes to sleeping arrangements. If you constantly soften and give in to your child's crying and protests, your child learns that if they cry long enough they will get want they want., There are many different reasons why your child is not comfortable in their own bed and it can be hard, when confronted with an upset child, to figure out what's going on instead of just letting them sleep in with you. It's worth exploring a few of the more common issues:Are they afraid of things that go bump in the night or monsters under the bed or in the closet?
Are they jealous of a younger sibling and feel that this is the only way to get the attention that they want?
Are there sounds they can hear that disturb them?
Is their room too dark and in need of a night light?
You can also have your child draw or paint their anxieties around bedtime on a piece of paper.Give your child lots of time to open up and don't react negatively if they say something you don't want to hear. It's best to determine what exactly is causing them to react badly to sleeping in their own bed.
Their favorite toy or a fluffy stuffed animal can be comforts and protectors against any "monsters" or shadows in your child's room.Another great way to help your child feel safe and protected is to let different stuffed animals represent different members of their family, so sleeping with a certain bear or toy can be just like sleeping with Mom or Dad.
You can also record a tape of you reading child's favorite songs and stories so they can turn it on when they feel alone or need comfort., These can also go a long way toward banishing night terrors. For some children, the dark can turn a familiar room into a place that is very scary, and having a light that comes on in the dark can be instrumental to making the room seem friendly again.
You can also give them a flashlight that they can keep by their bed or play with before bed so they feel like they are in control and can't get scared by things they cannot see in the dark., This will allow them to shoot monsters with the spray if they come out of the closet or under the bed.You can also move the furniture around in your child's room to eliminate any large shadows or spooky corners in their room.
You can then put duct tape over the minutes and ask your child to tell you the number they see in the dark. Indicate to them the numbers that correspond with sleepy time, such as no one is awake in your home after 8 pm and no one should be awake before 5 am., As a compromise, you could allow your child to sleep in your room, but rather than in your bed, have them sleep in a tent or "camp" out in your room.Set up a kid's tent at the foot of your bed and let your child "camp" every night. Then, slowly move the tent out of your room and into your child's room.
Allow your child to sleep in the tent in their room every night, but move your child's bedding and stuffed animals into their bed. Eventually, your child should avoid the hassle of moving all their sleep stuff back into the tent and just stay in their bed.
Start talking to your child about what they should expect at bedtime (in their own bed, in their own room, with a nice long sleep throughout the night) in the afternoon, so your child will know what to expect by lights-out., Some children need to be wound down slowly and putting them to bed before they are completely calmed down can lead to a sleepless night for them, and for you. Having a consistent bedtime ritual will indicate it is time for them to go to sleep and they will be in the right frame of mind for bed.
Your ritual could be giving them a bath and then reading them a story or singing them a song before bed.You could also make your child a "sleepytime book", nothing fancy, just a few pages stapled together that your child can color or look at before bed. It could tell a story about sleeping well in a new bed or new home, and encourage your child to recognize the value of sleeping in their own bed., If your child has a hard time falling asleep without your presence, withdraw yourself gradually. Sit on their bed and after a few days, switch to sitting in a chair a few feet from their bed. Then, over time, move the chair closer to the doorway of your child's room and/or into the hallway.Avoid talking to your child as you sit with them until they fall asleep. The idea is to bore them into slumber, not stimulate them., Reward each night your child spends in their own bed with a sticker, and after a full week of sleeping in their own room, tell them they win a prize like a special outing or a new set of pyjamas.You could also create a toy-ticket program to stop your child from sneaking into your bed. If your child sleeps for several consecutive nights in their own bed, they can win tickets to trade in for new toys or a playdate., Instruct your child to close their eyes and think about what they want for their birthday or what their day at school is going to be like tomorrow. Giving your child something to think about is a great tool to help them fall back asleep., Most sleep consultants and parents who've been there say that once you decide to start training your child to sleep in their own bed, bed sharing needs to end entirely. Each time your child gets out of bed, carry them back and calmly place them in their bed.Avoid yelling or nagging them as you do this. Your child will realize that they are not getting your attention, even negative attention, and this can cut down on their night-time wanderings from bed., While it doesn't need to be locked, closing the door to your child's room will ensure they are not woken up by any noise in the hallway or other rooms in the house, and they will be less distracted as they try to fall asleep., It may not be reasonable to demand that a child who's accustomed to sleeping in their parent's bed suddenly has to stay in their own room all night. So try making the separation more gradual.If your child began by sleeping in a crib in a separate room, you may have an easier time with gradually moving out of their room as they sleep.But if they began by sleeping in your bed with you, every night, you may need to put a comforter or air mattress on the floor and sleep in their room with them for a few nights until they get used to sleeping in their own bed.

Obviously it's impossible to specifically say. There are very few cases where we can retroactively review medical cases for any sort of evidence to diagnose cancer and get a realistic viewpoint on how "big of a deal it was". Furthermore, the diagnostic methods and understanding that modern medicine now possesses make any meaningful comparison very difficult. They simply never could have found some forms of cancer we know about today.
Hippocrates used the term *carcinos* in his Corpus texts and spoke about a black bile that he attributed to the masses. Hippocrates other writings seem to clearly present that Ancient Greeks were aware that removing these tumors was possible and would benefit the health of the patient, describing what we could infer is a Mastectomy. He even had the knowledge to clearly outline when he thought a tumor should or should not be removed, and he was able to identify several "superficial" and "deep-seated" types of tumors of the vagina, breasts, stomach, armpit, groin, and other soft tissue.
The Greeks and Romans prescribed a variety of potions, salves, concoctions, rites, and other things to try to mitigate cancer, but none of them appeared to be even remotely successful. I don't have any specific references to these curatives/treatments in any meaningful fashion. Hippocrates did advocate, in the event medicine didnt work, treating tumors via knife. And if that did not work, cautery.
I can only speculate on the state of mind the ancient Greeks or Romans would have had about cancer, but from the choice of phrasing, it did not appear particularly optimistic. "No treatment" is a commonly used term.
I'm tangentially aware of other cultures with older 'knowledge' of cancer, especially Egypt, but I don't know that material well enough to comment and would absolutely need to call someone else in on that.
I recommend first reading Hippocrates' Corpus (numerous translations are available, all mostly okay) and then perhaps following it up with Celsus's work, De Medicina.

Reionization is thought to be dominated by low mass galaxies, while directobservations of resolved galaxies probe only the most massive, rarest objects.The cross-correlation between fluctuations in the surface brightness of thecumulative Ly-alpha emission (which serves as a proxy for the star formationrate) and the redshifted 21cm signal from neutral hydrogen in the intergalacticmedium (IGM), will directly probe the causal link between the production ofionizing photons in galaxies and the reionization of the IGM. We discuss theprospects for detecting this cross-correlation for unresolved galaxies. We findthat on angular scales <10 arc-minutes, detection will be practical usingwidefield near-IR imaging from space in combination with the forthcomingMileura Widefield Array - Low Frequency Demonstrator. When redshifted 21cmobservations of the neutral IGM are combined with space-based near-IR imagingof Ly-alpha emission, the detection on angular scales <3 arc-minutes will belimited by the sensitivity of the 21cm signal, even when a small apertureoptical telescope (~2m) and a moderate field of view (~10 square degrees) areused. On scales >3 arc-minutes, the measurement of cross-correlation will belimited by the accuracy of the foreground sky subtraction.

Before you canter from the walk, it's essential that you're able to canter from the trot. This is a fundamental skill that will help you transition smoothly and maintain control over your horse. Start by picking up a sitting trot around the arena or in the field, whichever you're more comfortable with. Trot for at least five paces to get your horse into the rhythm. Next, move your outside leg back behind the girth, but only far back enough that you feel your hip kind of "drop". Shift your inside leg as if you're pushing your hip up to your elbow. This is the "canter position". Squeeze your inside leg while pushing with your outside leg. This should be a squeeze but with a slight forward motion. Encourage your horse with your seat. Keep your reins tight, but don't pull. Continue moving your seat in time with your horse's hooves. This will help you maintain balance and control. Focus on the tempo and pace of your horse's canter, so you know what to expect when you transition from the walk. After you've mastered the canter from the trot, bring your horse down to a trot again, then change directions. Repeat the canter on the opposite lead. This will help you and your horse get used to cantering in both directions. Next, bring your horse down to a working walk. Move your legs into the "canter position" again. Begin moving your seat as you do in the canter. Repeat the "canter position" with your legs in place, and be prepared for the canter. Remember, it's okay if you don't canter on the first try. It takes practice and patience to master this skill. Keep trying, and with time, you'll be able to canter from the walk with ease.

We present here the first study of the large-scale structure of the red galaxy (RG) halo population in the SDSS using the halo model. We identified 27,351 galaxies with photometric redshifts in the SDSS DR7 catalog of luminous RGs with Mrk > -18.5, a selected group which has the same properties as the optically-selected halo RGs but with a significantly higher number of galaxies, enabling a more robust analysis of the large-scale structure. We find that the galaxies in this sample follow the halo model very well, with the projected density distribution in very good agreement with the predictions of the model, and the corresponding spherical halo profiles are consistent with those obtained in previous studies of optically-selected RGs. We find no evidence of a core in the projected density distribution, in agreement with previous results for optically-selected RGs, and we obtain a scaling length of $h \sim 250$ kpc. The number density profile follows the halo model with a core, and we find that the Virial Theorem is satisfied with a Virial ratio of $\sim 2.1$. We find that the bulk of the population is comprised of galaxies in groups, and that only a small fraction of the population is comprised of galaxies in the large voids; this fraction is consistent with the predictions of the model. We also present a catalog of 9,302 optically-selected RGs with Mrk > -18.5 in the same redshift range as the photometric RGs, and find that the photometric and optically-selected samples have very similar properties, in agreement with the predictions of the halo model. We conclude that the halo model successfully describes the large-scale structure of the SDSS luminous RGs in this redshift range.

The stock market exists to facilitate the buying and selling of stocks, bonds, and other securities. It serves several important functions: 1. Capital Raising: It provides a platform for companies to raise capital by selling shares to the public. This capital can be used for various purposes such as expansion, research and development, or paying off debts. 2. Liquidity: The stock market provides liquidity, which means that investors can easily buy and sell securities. This makes investing more attractive as it allows investors to cash out their investments whenever they need. 3. Price Determination: The stock market helps in determining the price of securities. The price of a stock is determined by the supply and demand for that particular stock in the market. This provides an objective value of the company. 4. Economic Indicator: The stock market is often seen as a reflection of the country's economic health. When stock prices are rising, it often indicates that the economy is doing well. Conversely, falling stock prices can indicate an economic downturn. 5. Wealth Creation: The stock market provides an opportunity for individuals and institutions to invest their money and potentially earn a return. This can lead to wealth creation over time. 6. Corporate Governance: Publicly listed companies are required to follow strict financial reporting standards and regulations, which can lead to better corporate governance and transparency. In summary, the stock market plays a crucial role in the functioning of the global economy. It helps companies to grow, provides investors with opportunities to earn returns, and acts as an economic indicator.

There are several reasons why we might not hear about these discoveries again: 1. Early Stage Research: Many of these discoveries are in the early stages of research when they are first reported. It can take years, even decades, to move from an initial discovery to a marketable product or treatment. This is especially true in the field of medicine, where rigorous testing and trials are required to ensure safety and efficacy. 2. Funding: Research and development is expensive. Even if a discovery has potential, it may not receive the necessary funding to move forward. This is particularly true for treatments that may not be profitable, such as those for rare diseases. 3. Regulatory Approval: Even after a product or treatment has been developed and tested, it must still receive approval from regulatory bodies like the FDA in the United States. This process can be lengthy and there's no guarantee of approval. 4. Media Hype: Sometimes, the media can overhype a discovery, making it seem more significant or closer to market than it really is. This can create unrealistic expectations about when or if the discovery will become widely available. 5. Failed Trials: Not all discoveries pan out. A treatment that looks promising in the lab may not work as well in human trials, or it may have unforeseen side effects. In these cases, the research may be abandoned. 6. Intellectual Property Disputes: Sometimes, disputes over who owns the rights to a discovery can delay or even halt its development. 7. Commercial Viability: Even if a discovery is scientifically sound and has passed all necessary trials and regulations, it may not be commercially viable. For example, a cheaper alternative to an existing product may not be profitable enough for a company to manufacture and market. So, while these discoveries are exciting and have the potential to make a big impact, there are many hurdles to overcome before they can become widely available.

The consensus amongst reviewers' was that this paper, incorporating global context into classification, is not ready for publication. It provides no novelty over similar methods. The evaluation did not convince most of the reviewers. The paper seems peppered with unjustified and (as rather bluntly, but accurately, put by one reviewer) unscientific claims. Disappointingly, the authors did not respond to pre-review questions. Perhaps more understandably, they did not respond to the uniformly negative reviews of their paper to defend it. I see no reason to diverge from the reviewers' recommendation, and advocate rejection of this paper.

The final Byzantine-Sassanian War was (for my money) the antique world's closest equivalent to a world war. As always in premodern contexts, however, our sources on the size of the forces involved are dubious.
The Byzantine *Chronicon Paschale*, compiled shortly after the war's end, is probably our best source. The author, however, has an irritating habit of merely referring to "vast throngs" of the enemy. Fortunately, he is slightly more forthcoming about the great Siege of Constantinople in 626, when the Avars and Slavs invested the land walls and a large Persian army camped across the Bosporus at Chalcedon.
According to the author of the *Chronicon,* the Avar Khagan's vanguard numbered about 30,000, and Constantinople was defended by only 12,000 cavalry. George of Pisidia adds that the entire Avar/Slav force numbered 80,000. The *Chronicon* also notes that, when the Khagan was attempting to compel Constantinople to surrender, he boasted that the Persians in Chalcedon were ready to send 3,000 troops (presumably a small portion of their force) across the Bosporus at a moment's notice. Theophanes the Confessor, describing a Persian army earlier in the war, numbers it at 40,000 men; I doubt the Persian army at Chalcedon was larger. 
Theodore the Syncellus adds the picturesque, if imprecise, detail that the inhabitants of Constantinople spitted "a multitude of \[Avar\] heads on lances" after the siege.
As for the size of the general populations, the consensus seems to be (according to Warren Treadgold's *History of the Byzantine State and Society*) that the Byzantine Empire had about 17-18 million inhabitants at the beginning of the war. Sassanid Persia probably (there is no hard data) had about half this number.
Those intrigued by this period might be interested in [my page on final Byzantine-Sassanian War](_URL_0_), which examines that conflict through the ruins of a church built to commemorate the Byzantine victory.

Overall, this is a very nice paper. It has good content and represents real work. The math is clearly explained and the examples are intuitive and useful. The paper is not very long and it would be very useful for the authors to elaborate on their ideas. The main idea is the importance of sound combining (i.e. averaging) of statistics when training DNNs. The authors illustrate this with multiple examples using a Clothes learning application. I have a few comments and suggestions about the paper. The examples in the paper are very specific to the type of loss functions being used (i.e. softmax cross entropy loss) and the type of DNN being trained (i.e. logistic regression). I think it would be good to have one example using global RMSProp and RMSProp with momentum and a deep neural network with ReLU activation functions. Another good addition would be a comparison to standard averaging techniques (e.g. using batch normalization), which are also known to provide benefits in terms of training stability and generalization. The type of examples (i.e. problems) used in the paper are very specific to the application (i.e. Clothes) and it would be good to see examples using different datasets and different types of models (e.g. deep neural networks with fully connected layers, etc.). In addition, I think it would be good to explicitly mention the architectures of the DNNs being used in the examples. For example, the first example has a DNN with 7 hidden layers, while the other examples have DNNs with 3 hidden layers. This would make it easier for readers to understand what assumptions the examples are making about the models and also compare the examples more easily. Overall, I think this is an interesting idea and the approach is definitely worth exploring. I suggest the following directions for the paper: 1) illustrate the importance of sound combining for general DNN training; 2) show a practical example using different types of models and datasets; 3) discuss how the example could be improved by using more sophisticated averaging techniques.

For your average medieval person living in an area that logistically allowed for it, hunting game (even in the form of poaching) was a way to get by. It was a way to supplement your regular diet, and if you did it right it was free. (If you did it wrong, you were in a lot of trouble.) But for medieval noblemen and women it became an art, a sport, and a status symbol -- perhaps *because* they didn't necessarily need to hunt those animals down themselves if they wanted to eat meat. Hunting itself was ubiquitous throughout the entire Middle Ages and likely throughout history, but the medieval European hunt as an aristocratic custom and pastime reached its fullest flower in the 14th and 15th centuries; I don't think it's a coincidence that this is also where texts describing how and what to hunt (written both for the delight of genteel audiences and as advice for the staff that would help them) also proliferate.
For a look at literary constructions of medieval hunting, you could do worse than to look at Gaston Phoebus' *Livre de chasse* -- Gaston III, count of Foix, viscount of Béarn, was an authentic French nobleman of the second half of the 14th century with a nobleman's concerns. The chronicler Froissart describes Gaston in some detail, but among his remarks is this:
 > He mightily loved dogs above all other animals; and during the summer and winter amused himself much with hunting.
One of those dogs comes to save Gaston's life later when it's used as an unwitting test for poison, administered to Gaston by his own son (Also named Gaston.) In general, the keeping of dogs as nobles' pets owes a lot to the practice of hunting using hounds, even if the later development into dogs that are more recognizably lapdogs defeats that purpose somewhat. 
Gaston Phoebus wrote his *Livre de chasse* some time in the late 1380s, dedicating it to Philip the Bold, and it became one of the most broadly-disseminated texts on aristocratic hunting -- this might account for how many surviving manuscripts of it exist. (The English translation of Phoebus' text, *The Master of Game*, is also the product of an aristocratic writer -- Edward of Norwich, Duke of York. His edition is dedicated to the future Henry V of England.) It's a four-part treatise covering the benefits of hunting, the best ways to go about it, the qualities of different game and how to hunt them, as well as why men should hunt at all. 
 > Furthermore I will prove by sundry reasons in this little prologue, that the life of no man that useth gentle game and disport be less displeasable unto God than the life of a perfect and skilful hunter, or from which more good cometh. The first reason is that hunting causeth a man to eschew the seven deadly sins. Secondly men are better when riding, more just and more understanding, and more alert and more at ease and more undertaking, and better knowing of all countries and all passages; in short and long all good customs and manners cometh thereof, and the health of man and of his soul. For he that fleeth the seven deadly sins as we believe, he shall be saved, therefore a good hunter shall be saved, and in this world have joy enough and of gladness and of solace, so that he keep himself from two things. One is that he leave not the knowledge nor the service of God, from whom all good cometh, for his hunting. The second that he lose not the service of his master for his hunting, nor his own duties which might profit him most. Now shall I prove how a hunter may not fall into any of the seven deadly sins. When a man is idle and reckless without work, and be not occupied in doing some thing, he abides in his bed or in his chamber, a thing which draweth men to imaginations of fleshly lust and pleasure. For such men have no wish but always to abide in one place, and think in pride, or in avarice, or in wrath, or in sloth, or in gluttony, or in lechery, or in envy. For the imagination of men rather turns to evil than to good, for the three enemies which mankind hath, are the devil, the world and the flesh, and this is proved enough.
These are some pretty lofty aims that expand far beyond putting meat on the table. Phoebus details how to go about hunting harts, boars, wolves, and roe deer, as well as otters, badgers, hares, and foxes, and he deals with what we'd now call broadly "deer" in terms of their different life stages and how that dictated their qualities. From Norwich's translation, regarding the hart:
 > The hart is a common beast enough and therefore me needeth not to tell of his making, for there be few folk that have not seen some. The harts be the lightest beasts and strongest, and of marvellous great cunning. They are in their love, which men call rut, about the time of the Holy Rood in September and remain in their hot love a whole month and ere they be fully out thereof they abide [in rut] nigh two months. And then they are bold, and run upon men as a wild boar would do if he were hunted. [...] he smiteth as the stroke of the springole, for he has great strength in the head and the body. They slay, fight and hurt each other, when they be in rut, that is to say in their love, and they sing in their language [Norwich adds 'that in England hunters call bellowing'] as man that loveth paramour. They slay hounds and horses and men at that time and turn to the abbay ['be at bay'] as a boar does especially when they be weary. 
This isn't a 101-level text; it's clearly for people who know their stuff, or who would like to learn their stuff. He goes on to describe other actions of the hart by the season in which they occur, the behavior of harts when young and old, how harts seek to evade hunters by concealing their smell from the hounds, and so on in quite fine-grained detail for the other animals he describes as well. It's a mix of Gaston's (and Norwich's) folkloric understanding of the animal kingdom (harts live to be a hundred years old) and their practical understanding of it gained from practical experience as well as the study of other hunting texts. Illustrations on both version of the text showcase both the practical and the aesthetic concerns of hunting -- this scene of men on horseback accompanied by men on foot [hunting the roebuck](_URL_0_) shows the trappings of hunting (hounds, horns, swords and spears) as well as the excitement of the pursuit. 
It took resources to put together a formal hunting party this way -- it took time and money to maintain horses and dogs as well as to employ lymerers (guys whose whole purpose is to act as handlers for scenthounds) and other staff to smooth the process along, and one had to be of a certain social class to access forests and game reserves, hence all the poaching. A host of weapons were employed (not just the bow and arrow we associate with genteel medieval hunting -- things could get pretty down and dirty) and aristocratic women might well be involved, even if their use of weapons (and birds of prey, in the case of falconry) had a gendered element. The birds one might keep and use for the purpose of hunting were closely dictated in such manuals along the lines of rank and class, not unlike sumptuary restrictions -- in Juliana Berners' 14th-century guide to falconry she dictates that gyrfalcons are for kings, merlins for ladies, goshawks for yeomen, and sparrowhawks for priests. (Berners herself may have been an abbess; well-off clergy weren't above hunting as a pastime.) This was nominally due to practical concerns (how could a gentle lady wrangle a massive bird of prey? how could a poor man afford an expensive bird more fitting to an emperor?) but primarily due to the aesthetics and social cachet of each kind of bird, just as sartorial restrictions on who could wear which velvet or what fur lining on garments served a practical purpose primarily to back up their social purpose.
