Although making out is a very modern term, people have been doing it since the beginning of time. In the colonial period, people courted, which meant they got to know each other well. Courtship was a very serious business. Couples spent much time talking to each other, either in person or by letter. They would exchange books and play music together. Both the man and woman had to be of marriageable age, which was usually around 18. Courtship might last a few months or several years. In the 1700s, it was common for couples to marry in their early 20s.
Courtship was a very private matter, and families were very protective of their daughters. The Puritans frowned upon couples who were too open with their affections. A man who showed too much attention to a woman before marriage was called a fortune hunter. It was also considered improper for men and women to dance together. When they did dance, they were not supposed to touch each other. Courtship in colonial times was complicated.
There were not a lot of places for young couples to go to be alone. They would often meet in the home of a relative or a friend of the family. Going for a walk together was another popular activity. Sometimes the couple would sit on a bench or on the grass. Holding hands or stealing a kiss were signs that a couple was becoming serious. Even though courtship was very restrained, people were able to find ways to express their love for each other.

The paper titled "Rare Entity Prediction: Language Understanding with External Knowledge using Hierarchical LSTMs" is a well-structured and comprehensive study that explores the integration of external knowledge into language understanding tasks using hierarchical Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks. The authors have done an excellent job in presenting the problem statement and the motivation behind the study. The literature review is thorough and provides a solid foundation for the research. The authors have also done a commendable job in explaining the methodology, particularly the use of hierarchical LSTMs and how they can be used to incorporate external knowledge into language understanding tasks. The experimental setup and results are well presented and discussed. The authors have used a variety of datasets to validate their approach, which adds to the robustness of the study. The results show significant improvements over the baseline methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach. However, there are a few areas that could be improved. Firstly, the authors could provide more details on the external knowledge sources used in the study. It would be interesting to see how different types of external knowledge affect the performance of the model. Secondly, the paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion on the limitations of the proposed approach and potential ways to overcome them. In terms of readability, the paper is well-written and easy to follow. The figures and tables are well-designed and effectively support the text. However, some sections could benefit from further clarification, particularly the section on the implementation details of the hierarchical LSTMs. Overall, this paper is a valuable contribution to the field of natural language processing and offers a novel approach to rare entity prediction. With some minor improvements, this paper has the potential to have a significant impact on the field.

What most people think of as cutting off their circulation is actually compression of the nerves which innervate that area (typically a limb). The blood supply is generally not compromised at all, it takes quite a while (hours) for ischaemia to present with neurological signs (tingling etc).
The compression of the nerves causes abnormal discharging of the the sensory neurones which is in turn perceived as tingling (parasthesia) or other abnormal sensations. This is usually reversible but if the nerve is compressed for a long time it can persist or become permanent. You can look up Saturday Night Syndrome if you are interested.
Edit: spelling and a missed “signs”
Edit 2: Thanks for all the comments, my apologies if I don’t reply to them all.
I perhaps should have been slightly clearer in my language as some have rightly pointed out that direct compression of nerves leads to changes in the microvascular supply to that region of the nerve and this is implicated in the development of symptoms. I more meant my above comment to convey the fact that it is the compression of the nerve itself (and subsequent microvascular and other sequalae) not the arteries supplying a limb which leads to the parasthesia many people experience.

This paper introduces an approach for integrating a direct acyclic graph structure of the data into word / code embeddings, in order to leverage domain knowledge and thus help train an RNN with scarce data. It is applied to codes of medical visits. Each code is part of an ontology, which can be represented by a DAG, where codes correspond to leaf nodes, and where different codes may share common ancestors (non-leaf nodes) in the DAG. Instead of embedding merely the leaf nodes, one can also embed the non-leaf nodes, and the embeddings of the code and its ancestors can be combined using a convex sum. That convex sum can be seen as an attention mechanism over the representation. The attention weights depend on the embeddings and the weights of an MLP, meaning that the model can separate learning the code embeddings and the interaction between the codes. Embedding codes are pretrained using GloVe, then fine-tuned.
The model is properly evaluated on two medical datasets, with several variations to isolate the contribution of the DAG (GRAM or GRAM+ vs. RNN or RandomDAG) and of pretraining the embeddings (RNN+ vs RNN, GRAM+ vs GRAM). Both are shown to help achieve the best performance and the evaluation methodology seems thorough.
The paper is also well written, and the case for MLP attention instead of a plain dot product of embeddings was made by the authors.
My only two comments would be:
1) Why is there a softmax in equation 4, given that the loss is multivariate cross-entropy (in the predicted visit, several codes could be equal to 1), not a a single-class cross-entropy?
2) What is the embedding dimension m?

The analysis of gravitational wave data involves many model selectionproblems. The most important example is the detection problem of selectingbetween the data being consistent with instrument noise alone, or instrumentnoise and a gravitational wave signal. The analysis of data from ground basedgravitational wave detectors is mostly conducted using classical statistics,and methods such as the Neyman-Pearson criteria are used for model selection.Future space based detectors, such as the \emph{Laser Interferometer SpaceAntenna} (LISA), are expected to produced rich data streams containing thesignals from many millions of sources. Determining the number of sources thatare resolvable, and the most appropriate description of each source poses achallenging model selection problem that may best be addressed in a Bayesianframework. An important class of LISA sources are the millions of low-massbinary systems within our own galaxy, tens of thousands of which will bedetectable. Not only are the number of sources unknown, but so are the numberof parameters required to model the waveforms. For example, a significantsubset of the resolvable galactic binaries will exhibit orbital frequencyevolution, while a smaller number will have measurable eccentricity. In theBayesian approach to model selection one needs to compute the Bayes factorbetween competing models. Here we explore various methods for computing Bayesfactors in the context of determining which galactic binaries have measurablefrequency evolution. The methods explored include a Reverse Jump Markov ChainMonte Carlo (RJMCMC) algorithm, Savage-Dickie density ratios, the Schwarz-BayesInformation Criterion (BIC), and the Laplace approximation to the modelevidence. We find good agreement between all of the approaches.

The dynamics of many socioeconomic systems is determined by the decisionmaking process of agents. The decision process depends on agent'scharacteristics, such as preferences, risk aversion, behavioral biases, etc..In addition, in some systems the size of agents can be highly heterogeneousleading to very different impacts of agents on the system dynamics. The largesize of some agents poses challenging problems to agents who want to controltheir impact, either by forcing the system in a given direction or by hidingtheir intentionality. Here we consider the financial market as a model system,and we study empirically how agents strategically adjust the properties oflarge orders in order to meet their preference and minimize their impact. Wequantify this strategic behavior by detecting scaling relations of allometricnature between the variables characterizing the trading activity of differentinstitutions. We observe power law distributions in the investment timehorizon, in the number of transactions needed to execute a large order and inthe traded value exchanged by large institutions and we show that heterogeneityof agents is a key ingredient for the emergence of some aggregate propertiescharacterizing this complex system.

Restretching a silkscreen frame with adhesive is a straightforward process that can be done at home with a few simple tools. Here's how to do it: 1. Start by cleaning your silkscreen frame with a unbinding solvent like acetone. This will help to remove any old adhesive and make the frame ready for the new mesh. 2. Once the frame is clean, peel the used mesh off the frame. Be careful not to damage the frame in the process. 3. If your frame is wooden, use 80-grit sandpaper to roughen up the areas of your frame that will be glued. This will help the adhesive to stick better. 4. After sanding, wipe the frame with a tack cloth to remove any dust or debris. 5. Choose an adhesive that is suitable for your frame and mesh. There are many types available, so be sure to read the instructions and choose one that is appropriate for your needs. 6. Cut a new piece of mesh to size that is slightly bigger than the frame. This will ensure that the mesh covers the entire frame and has enough room to be stretched. 7. Place the frame on wooden blocks in the middle of your screen stretching machine or device. This will help to keep the frame stable while you are stretching the mesh. 8. Place the mesh over the frame and insert it into the tension bars on your stretching device. 9. Increase the tension slowly, checking the tension with a mesh tension meter, until you have reached the tension that was last used on the frame. 10. Apply a thin coat of adhesive where the fabric attaches to the frame using a plastic applicator or a tongue depressor. Be sure to cover the entire area where the mesh will be attached. 11. Allow the adhesive to dry and cure according to package instructions. 12. Use weights on top of the drying frame to ensure it does not warp or bend. 13. Once the adhesive is dry, remove the tension bars and cut any excess mesh surrounding the frame. By following these steps, you can successfully restretch a silkscreen frame with adhesive.

Modern driving requires many concentrated bursts of mental activity. The use of phones distracts drivers from the important task of focus mental activity, and thus is a major threat to vehicular safety. Statistically, there is a strong correlation between cell phone usage and car accidents. Approximately one-third of all car accidents are caused by cell phone users, and the risk of an accident increases more than twofold when the cell phone user is engaged in a phone conversation. While governments have attempted to reduce the risk of cell phone use while driving through education campaigns, these have had mixed results. In some jurisdictions, using a hands-free device while driving still allows the driver to present both hands and eyes to the task of operating the vehicle, whereas legislation could eliminate the use of all cell phones while driving. break Modern driving requires mental activity, and cell phones are a major cause of distracted driving. Phone usage while driving increases the risk of car accidents by nearly twofold, and existing education campaigns on the subject have had a minimal effect. The only solution is to prohibit phone usage while driving, as it removes the driver from the primary activity of driving and transfers responsibility for safe driving to an independent third party. water Modern driving requires concentrated bursts of mental activity. The paper titled "Efficient Summarization with Read-Again and Copy Mechanism" addresses the problem of creating efficient and accurate summaries of large text data. It proposes a novel approach that incorporates a read-again and copy mechanism to improve the quality of generated summaries. Strengths: The paper's main strength lies in its innovative approach to text summarization. The proposed read-again and copy mechanism is a novel idea that has the potential to significantly improve the quality of summaries. The paper is well-structured and the methodology is clearly explained, making it easy for readers to understand the proposed approach. The authors also provide a comprehensive evaluation of their method, including comparisons with existing techniques, which adds credibility to their claims. Weaknesses: However, the paper has a few weaknesses that need to be addressed. Firstly, the authors do not provide a detailed explanation of how the read-again and copy mechanism works, which makes it difficult for readers to fully understand the proposed method. Secondly, the paper lacks a thorough literature review, which is necessary to understand the context and significance of the proposed method. Lastly, while the authors provide results of their method, they do not provide a detailed analysis of these results. This makes it difficult for readers to understand the implications of the results and how they support the authors' claims.

The paper titled "Energy-Based Spherical Sparse Coding" is a well-structured and comprehensive study that delves into the realm of energy-based models and sparse coding. The authors have done a commendable job in presenting a novel approach to spherical sparse coding, which is a significant contribution to the field. The paper is well-written with a clear and concise abstract, providing a good overview of the study. The introduction section is well-structured, providing sufficient background information and clearly stating the research problem. The literature review is thorough and provides a good context for the study. The methodology section is detailed and provides a clear explanation of the energy-based spherical sparse coding model. The mathematical formulations are well-presented and the authors have done a good job in explaining the complex concepts in a simple and understandable manner. The use of diagrams and figures further enhances the understanding of the model. The results and discussion section is well-presented with clear and concise explanations. The authors have done a good job in interpreting the results and discussing their implications. The comparison with other existing models is well-done and provides a good understanding of the advantages of the proposed model. However, the paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion on the limitations of the study and potential areas for future research. Also, the authors could consider providing more real-world examples or case studies to demonstrate the practical applications of the model. In conclusion, the paper is a significant contribution to the field and provides a novel approach to spherical sparse coding. The authors should be commended for their rigorous research and clear presentation of complex concepts. With minor improvements, the paper has the potential to be a seminal work in the field.

In this paper, we present a comprehensive study of heavy-light semileptonic decays within the framework of staggered chiral perturbation theory. We focus on the calculation of form factors, which are crucial for the determination of the elements of the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa (CKM) matrix. Our analysis includes the effects of finite lattice spacing and finite volume, which are essential for the extrapolation of lattice QCD results to the physical point. We also provide predictions for decay rates and differential decay distributions. Our results are expected to contribute significantly to the ongoing efforts to reduce the theoretical uncertainties in the determination of the CKM matrix elements, and hence, to the test of the Standard Model in the flavor sector.

The authors have done some research in exploring the macro discourse structure based on their previous work about CDTB. They also propose an improved version called MCDTB which can be used as a benchmark corpus for further studies related to discourse structure analysis. 
In addition, they introduce two new concepts -primary and secondary-into the field of discourse structure analysis. The concept of primary has been discussed before but not so much attention was paid to it until now when researchers realized its importance especially in analyzing the relationships among sentences within one paragraph. This article provides us more information about how these two concepts are defined and applied into practice through examples taken from real texts. However, there still exist several issues worth discussing. 
First, although the definition of primary sentence seems clear enough according to the author's explanation, I think it's necessary to provide concrete examples showing exactly where each type of primary sentence should appear because sometimes it may become difficult to tell whether certain sentences belong to either category without seeing them together. For example, if you want your readers to know who did something wrong, shouldn't you use "wrong" instead of "did something wrong"? Yes

The shape of dark matter (DM) halos is a fundamental property, reflecting the complex process of DM aggregation and the effects of astrophysical processes such as hierarchical structure formation and feedback. The evolution of DM halos is simulated using a variety of numerical methods, making it possible to predict DM halo shape as a function of halo and orbital parameters. In this work, we present the first measurement of the effect of orbital parameters and chaos in DM halo structure. We find that orbits with similar properties to that of our local group result in triaxial, highly prolate halos, with degree 2 rotation – contrary to observations which imply spherical, axisymmetric halos in such configurations. Halos in more chaotic orbital scenarios are less well-aligned with observations: they are either nearly spherical, but with significant triaxiality, or are extremely prolate, but with lower degree of rotation. The evolution of DM halos in a given cosmological simulation is also often described using cosmological Principal Component Analysis. We show that the first two components of this multivariate distribution of halo properties are well-correlated with halo orbital anisotropy and chaos, providing a practical means of predicting the shape of DM halos in cosmological simulations.

Matthew Arrandale (born June 15, 1976) is an American actor. Arrandale was born in Baltimore, Maryland. He attended the Baltimore School for the Arts, where he studied music and graduated in 1994. He then attended New York University, where he studied musical theatre and graduated in 1998. In 1998, Arrandale made his Broadway debut in the original Broadway production of RENT as Paul, and later as Roger Davis. He remained with the show for three years, and is featured on the original Broadway cast recording. In 2001, he appeared in the Off-Broadway premiere of Adam Bock's play The Egg, and in 2002 he appeared in the Off-Broadway premiere of The 25th Annual Putnam County Spelling Bee. In 2004, he appeared in the New York premiere of Michael John LaChiusa's The Little Dog Laughed. In 2006, Arrandale appeared in the original Broadway production of The Drowsy Chaperone as Mr. Feldzeig. He remained with the show for a year and is featured on the original Broadway cast recording. In 2007, he appeared in the Off-Broadway premiere of The Little Dog Laughed. In 2008, he appeared in the Off-Broadway premiere of By the Way, Meet Vera Stark. Arrandale appeared in the 2009 New York premiere of Sunset Boulevard as Max, and in the 2010 New York premiere of The People in the Picture as Sam. In 2012, Arrandale appeared in the Off-Broadway premiere of The Blue Flower, and in 2013 he appeared in the Off-Broadway premiere of Indian Blood. Arrandale has appeared in three Broadway productions that have won the Tony Award for Best Musical: RENT, The Drowsy Chaperone, and Hamilton. In 2015, Arrandale appeared in the original Broadway production of Hamilton as John Laurens/Philip Schuyler. Arrandale is featured on the cast recordings for RENT, The Drowsy Chaperone, and Hamilton. In 2016, Arrandale performed in the workshop production of Hamilton's sister show, Hamilton: The Revolution. In 2017, Arrandale appeared in the Off-Broadway premiere of Club desir. In 2018, Arrandale appeared in the Off-Broadway premiere of Ripe.

Researchers have long known that plants respond and adapt to their environment through changes in gene expression and metabolism. However, relatively few studies have investigated how fluctuations in the light quanta signal are converted into adaptive responses at the molecular level. Here, we examined the effects of Ni(II) stress on the physiological and morphological responses of Brassica juncea seedlings, and explored how these are correlated with changes in the light quanta signal using a bioluminescence-based imaging approach. We found that Ni(II) stress suppressed hypocotyl elongation, and the level of hypocotyl elongation was correlated with the amount of light quanta received by the seedlings. The physiological parameters affected by Ni(II) stress were found to be light-dependent, while the morphological parameters were not. Our results suggest that Ni(II) stress impairs the perception of light by seedlings, and the degree of impairment is proportional to the severity of Ni(II) stress. Furthermore, we observed that the responses of the seedlings to Ni(II) stress correlated well with the previously identified “oxidative stress response” and the level of salicylic acid (SA). We speculate that Ni(II) stress suppresses hypocotyl elongation by inhibiting the conversion of light quanta to visual signals, leading to an imbalance in the levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and SA, which have been shown to be involved in Ni(II) stress responses. Overall, this work provides evidence of the correlation between light quanta, physiological and morphological responses in B. juncea seedlings subjected to Ni(II) stress, and identifies potential signal transduction pathways involved in these processes.

We study Ricci flows of some classes of physically valuable solutions inEinstein and string gravity. The anholonomic frame method is applied forgeneric off-diagonal metric ansatz when the field/ evolution equations aretransformed into exactly integrable systems of partial differential equations.The integral varieties of such solutions, in four and five dimensional gravity,depend on arbitrary generation and integration functions of one, two and/ orthree variables. Certain classes of nonholonomic frame constraints allow us toselect vacuum and/or Einstein metrics, to generalize such solutions fornontrivial string (for instance, with antisymmetric torsion fields) and matterfield sources. A very important property of this approach (originating fromFinsler and Lagrange geometry but re-defined for semi-Riemannian spaces) isthat new classes of exact solutions can be generated by nonholonomicdeformations depending on parameters associated to some generalized Gerochtransforms and Ricci flow evolution. In this paper, we apply the method toconstruct in explicit form some classes of exact solutions for multi-parameterEinstein spaces and their nonholonomic Ricci flows describing evolutions/interactions of solitonic pp-waves and deformations of the Schwarzschildmetric. We explore possible physical consequences and speculate on theirimportance in modern gravity.

Windhorse (film) Windhorse is a 1993 American drama film directed by Carroll Ballard and starring Richard Harris, Robert Wisdom, and Jeremy Irons. The film is based on the life of Frank Farmar, a horse trainer and jockey who, after suffering a career-ending injury, turned to training racehorses. The film is set in the horse racing industry in general and the sport of Steeplechase racing in particular. The title refers to the "wind horse," or "invisible horse" in Tibetan culture, a concept of happiness and good fortune, and the horse that carries the wind. Frank Farmar (Wisdom) is a gifted jockey and horse trainer whose career is ended by a serious fall. He begins training racehorses for wealthy businessman Sir Henry Lee (Irons), but the emotionally damaged Farmar is unable to take pleasure in his work. In a desperate attempt to regain his love for life, he decides to breed and train his own horse, despite his lack of money and resources. Farmar becomes emotionally attached to a young horse he names Windhorse, and his journey to heal himself is paralleled with the horse's own journey to recover from a broken leg. Windhorse was nominated for the Grand Jury Prize at the 1993 Sundance Film Festival. The film's theme song, "This House," was written and performed by Bruce Hornsby. The film was released on DVD by Warner Home Video on September 27, 2005. Carroll Ballard later directed a television series based on the film, also called Windhorse, which premiered on CBS in 1998. Windhorse (film) is a 1993 American drama film directed by Carroll Ballard and starring Richard Harris, Robert Wisdom, and Jeremy Irons. The film is based on the life of Frank Farmar, a horse trainer and jockey who, after suffering a career-ending injury, turned to training racehorses.

We report the detection of an infrared dark cloud (IRDC) in the vicinity of the open cluster NGC 6334, using data obtained with Spitzer Space Telescope's Infrared Array Camera (IRAC). The IRDC is associated with the molecular cloud complex G327.3+0.6 and has been identified as Bok globule CB190 by Clemens & Barvainis (1988). We find that this object exhibits a prominent 24 micron shadow which may be caused by absorption against bright mid-infrared emission from nearby protostars or young stellar objects. This feature suggests that the cloud contains dense cores at different evolutionary stages. Using near-infrared extinction mapping we identify two candidate starless cores within the cloud. These are located near the center of the cloud where the 24 micron shadow is most pronounced. Our analysis shows that these cores have masses between 0.5 Msun to 1 Msun and radii ranging from 1000 AU to 3000 AU.

The World Tenpin Bowling Championships is a global event that invites all countries that are members of International Bowling Federation to participate. The first World Championships was in 1954, held in Helsinki where 58 men from 7 federations took part. The next three World Championships (1955, 1958, and 1960) only had men participating. Women first participated in the 1963 World Championships in Mexico City. From 1963 to 2003, the World Championships were conducted every fourth year. As a result of the expanding number of federations competing, it was agreed in 2001 to divide the two genders in World Championships beginning in 2005. This affected the schedules for the following two 4 years cycles as follows: World Women Championships was conducted in 2005, 2007, 2009 and 2011. World Men Championships was conducted in 2006, 2008 and 2010 (2012 was moved to 2013). World Championships for both genders were reintroduced in 2009 after a World Congress resolved to hold them every fourth year. In 2013, the inaugural edition of these so-called Combined World Championships took place. The number of teams allowed to compete in the Combined World Championships is limited to 36 teams per gender, one of which is the host federation's team, and the other 35 federations are chosen from the Zones based on the number of federations in each zone as of the qualifying date. The following are the most recent four-year World Championship cycles: The above-mentioned four-year cycles will be repeated for the 2022-2025 cycles, and so on. Format of the games and disciplines through the years The format for the championships has changed many times throughout its history. In the early years of the championships, men competed in four different categories: Doubles, 4-man team, 8-man team and masters. Up until 1963 women did not participate in the event. The first year that women did take part, they competed in 4 different categories, doubles, four-person team (European Style), four-person team (American Style) and masters. This is the only time the "European Style" has been used in the championships. In the following championships the European style four-person team was replaced with the five-person team event (American Style) and was used until 1979. Singles, Doubles, Trios, Team of Five, All Events, and Masters have been the disciplines for both genders since 1979, with 6 women and 6 men on each team. The medalists for these events except the Masters from 1979 to 2007 were determined by total pinfall. After the conclusion of singles, doubles, trios, and five-person team events, the All Events medals are presented to the top three bowlers of both genders who have accumulated the most pinfall over the 24 games. Beginning in 2008, a medal round was introduced for singles, doubles, trios, and 5 five person team event. The Medal round consisted of the top four qualifiers playing a knockout format to determine the medalists (1 vs 4 and 2 vs 3, winners of those matches face each other in the final). The masters event has only recently changed with the onset of the championships splitting into 2 different events. Up until 2005 the top 16 would bowl a 16-game Round Robin with the top 3 bowlers after the 16 games advancing through to a stepladder final. From 2005 to 2011, the masters was played using the matchplay style, best of 5 format. From 2013-2015, the top 24 men and women in All-Events advanced to the Masters event. All players will bowl six games with the All-Events total carried forward. The top 8 after 30 games advance to seven games of round robin match play, where 20 bonus points are awarded for a won match and 10 bonus points for a tied match. The top 4 advance to the semifinals, which is a one-game match, 1 vs 4 and 2 vs 3. The semifinal losers will earn a bronze medal. The semifinal winners advance to a one-game final match to determine gold and silver medals. In 2017, the masters format (still consisting of top 24 men and women) reverted to matchplay style, best of 3. Beginning in 2017, the five-person team event was modified. Qualifying was still all five players bowl ten frames each with their scores added together to determine the overall score. In the medal round, the five players bowl a best of 3 baker format, where players bowl in order one frame each (frames 1-5) and repeat the order from frames 6-10. In 2005 the World Championships adopted the "dual pattern format" lane conditions. The two patterns are chosen from a bank of oil patterns certified by World Bowling. These oil patterns are classified as "short", "medium", and "long". Each bowler at the championships will bowl an equal number of games on the two patterns. In the masters, each match is played on alternating lane patterns with the highest seed having the choice of which pattern to start on. The concept of having two different lane patterns is to force the bowlers to be more versatile in the championships. The two different lane patterns force the bowlers to attack each pattern from different angles, using different styles of play, such as ball speed, hand position and ball choice. It has been argued that in previous championships around the world, using one pattern for the whole event would suit certain bowlers over others, which was deemed as being unfair. It was agreed that having two different lane conditions would be a fairer way of determining the best bowlers at the championships. Many other championships around the world have also adopted this format, such as the Men's and Women's European Championships, the Asian Games, the World Ranking Masters, The Commonwealth Championships and the Asian Championships. World Bowling announced in December 2015 only one lane pattern will be used for the entirety of future World Championships, ending the dual pattern format. World Bowling Executive Board have awarded the 2021 Combined World Championships hosting rights to Kuwait. Hong Kong will host the Men's World Championships in 2018, and Las Vegas will host the Women's World Championships in 2019. A new event, WTBA World Singles Championships for men and women, was held for the first time in Limassol, Cyprus from September 18–26, 2012, and will be held subsequently every four years. With this new event, WTBA will stage a World Championship event every year. Please see List of WTBA World Tenpin Bowling Championships medalists. Official European Tenpin Bowling Federation website

In this paper, we study the perturbative aspects of a "B-twisted"two-dimensional $(0,2)$ heterotic sigma model on a holomorphic gauge bundle$\mathcal E$ over a complex, hermitian manifold $X$. We show that the model canbe naturally described in terms of the mathematical theory of ``ChiralDifferential Operators". In particular, the physical anomalies of the sigmamodel can be reinterpreted as an obstruction to a global definition of theassociated sheaf of vertex superalgebras derived from the free conformal fieldtheory describing the model locally on $X$. In addition, one can also obtain anovel understanding of the sigma model one-loop beta function solely in termsof holomorphic data. At the $(2,2)$ locus, one can describe the resultinghalf-twisted variant of the topological B-model in terms of a $\it{mirror}$"Chiral de Rham complex" (or CDR) defined by Malikov et al. in \cite{GMS1}. Viamirror symmetry, one can also derive various conjectural expressions relatingthe sheaf cohomology of the mirror CDR to that of the original CDR on pairs ofCalabi-Yau mirror manifolds. An analysis of the half-twisted model on anon-K\"ahler group manifold with torsion also allows one to draw conclusionsabout the corresponding sheaves of CDR (and its mirror) that are consistentwith mathematically established results by Ben-Bassat in \cite{ben} on themirror symmetry of generalised complex manifolds. These conclusions thereforesuggest an interesting relevance of the sheaf of CDR in the recent study ofgeneralised mirror symmetry.

We extend the SU(4) model [1-5] for high-Tc superconductivity to an SU(4)kmodel that permits explicit momentum (k) dependence in predicted observables.We derive and solve gap equations that depend on k, temperature, and dopingfrom the SU(4)k coherent states, and show that the new SU(4)k model reduces tothe original SU(4) model for observables that do not depend explicitly onmomentum. The results of the SU(4)k model are relevant for experiments such asARPES that detect explicitly k-dependent properties. The present SU(4)k modeldescribes quantitatively the pseudogap temperature scale and may explain whythe ARPES-measured T* along the anti-nodal direction is larger than othermeasurements that do not resolve momentum. It also provides an immediatemicroscopic explanation for Fermi arcs observed in the pseudogap region. Inaddition, the model leads to a prediction that even in the underdoped regime,there exist doping-dependent windows around nodal points in the k-space, whereantiferromagnetism may be completely suppressed for all doping fractions,permitting pure superconducting states to exist.
